There is, unquestionably, a distinction between these several undertakings; and always so in regard to a mere guarantor. But as to the subsisting relations between a principal and surety, they rarely affect the contract between the creditor and surety. A man may be equally a surety and an original promisor; as where the promise is, I, A. B., as principal, and I, C. D., as surety, promise to pay; or where the party signs, and adds to his name the

<br>

### --- Page 0083 ---

UNION BANK OF WEYMOUTH, ETC. v. WILLIS.

word “surety.” This does not make him less a promisor. It only defines the relation between him and his co-promisor; and, as promisor, the necessity of a presentment to him is not dispensed with, if the intention of the holder of the note is to charge the indorser. It is not for the holder to choose in what character he will consider the party who has put his name on the note; but he must treat him as sustaining that legal relation which the facts establish. If he put his name on the note at the time it was made, like the case at bar, he is a promisor; if, after the making of the paper, he is a surety or a guarantor, according to the agreement upon which he gives his signature. The fixing of the relation of the party, when he enters into the contract, is necessary for the protection of holders, and for guarding the rights of indorsers, whose liability is conditional. If it were held otherwise, I do not well see how such contracts could be supported against the objection of being void as within the Statute of Frauds. And, as it is, I consider these engagements rather as exceptions to the statute, than in any other light, and as growing out of, or rather ingrafted upon, the law merchant applicable to regularly drawn bills of exchange and promissory notes.

Upon this view of the law, as drawn from the various cases, we consider Mirick & Co. to have been joint and several promisors with Thompson, and liable in like manner with him.

<br>

### --- Page 0084 ---

82 FORM AND REQUISITES.

SPEAR AND PATTON v. PRATT.  
(2 Hill, 582. Supreme Court of New York, May, 1842.)

What constitutes acceptance. — If the drawee of a bill of exchange writes his name across the face of the bill, this binds him as an acceptor; and this too, though the statute requires acceptance to be in writing, and signed by the acceptor or his agent.

The defendant, drawee of a bill of exchange, wrote his name across the face of the bill. He was a resident of New York. The question was whether he was bound as an acceptor.

Cowen, J. Any words written by the drawee on a bill, not putting a direct negative upon its request, as “accepted,” “presented,” “seen,” the day of the month, or a direction to a third person to pay it, is prima facie a complete acceptance, by the law merchant. Bayley on Bills, 163, Am. ed. of 1836, and the cases there cited. Writing his name across the bill, as in this case, is a still clearer indication of intent, and a very common mode of acceptance. This is treated by the law merchant as a written acceptance, — a signing by the drawee. “It may be,” says Chitty, “merely by writing his name at the bottom or across the bill;” and he mentions this as among the more usual modes of acceptance. Chitty on Bills, 320, Am. ed. of 1839.

It is supposed that the rule has been altered by 1 Rev. St. 175, 2d ed. § 6. This requires the acceptance to be in writing, and signed by the acceptor or his agent. The acceptance in question was, as we have seen, declared by the law merchant to be both a writing and signing. The statute contains no declaration that it should be considered less. An indorsement must be in writing and signed; yet the name alone is constantly held to satisfy the requisition. No particular form of expression is necessary in any contract. The custom-

<br>

### --- Page 0085 ---

COOLIDGE v. PAYSON. 33

ary import of a word, by reason of its appearing in a particular place, and standing in a certain relation, is considered a written expression of intent quite as full and effectual as if pains had been taken to throw it into the most labored periphrase. It is said the revisers, in their note, refer to the French law as the basis of the legislation which they recommended; and that the French law requires more than the drawee's name, — the word accepted, at least. That may be so; but it is enough for us to see that both the terms and the spirit of the act may be satisfied short of that word, and more in accordance with the settled forms of commercial instruments in analogous cases. The whole purpose was probably to obviate the inconveniences of the old law, which gave effect to a parol acceptance. New trial denied.

Coolidge et al. v. Payson et al.
(2 Wheaton, 66. Supreme Court of the United States, February, 1817.)

Promise to accept. — A letter written within a reasonable time, before or after the date of a bill of exchange, describing it in terms not to be mistaken, and promising to accept it, is, if shown to the person who afterwards takes the bill on the credit of the letter, a virtual acceptance, binding the person who makes the promise; and this too though it was drawn in favor of a person who took it for a pre-existing debt.

THE case is sufficiently stated in the opinion of the court.

Marshall, Ch. J. This suit was instituted by Payson & Co., as indorsers of a bill of exchange, drawn by Cornthwaite and Cary, payable to the order of John Randall, against Coolidge & Co., as the acceptors.

At the trial the holders of the bill on which the name of John Randall was indorsed, offered, for the purpose of proving the indorsement, an affidavit made by one of the defendants.

<br>

### --- Page 0086 ---

ants in the cause, in order to obtain a continuance, in which he referred to the bill in terms which, they supposed, implied a knowledge on his part that the plaintiffs were the rightful owners. The defendants objected to the bill's going to the jury without further proof of the indorsement; but the court determined that it should go with the affidavit to the jury, who might be at liberty to infer from thence that the indorsement was made by Randall. To this opinion the counsel for the defendants in the Circuit Court excepted, and this court is divided on the question whether the exception ought to be sustained.

On the trial, it appeared that Coolidge & Co. held the proceeds of part of the cargo of the 'Hiram,' claimed by Cornthwaite and Cary, which had been captured and held as lawful prize. The cargo had been acquitted in the District and Circuit Courts, but, from the sentence of acquittal, the captors had appealed to this court. Pending the appeal, Cornthwaite & Co. transmitted to Coolidge & Co. a bond of indemnity, executed at Baltimore with scrolls in the place of seals, and drew on them for $2,700. This bill was also payable to the order of Randall, and indorsed by him to Payson & Co. It was presented to Coolidge & Co. and protested for non-acceptance. After its protest, Coolidge & Co. wrote to Cornthwaite and Cary a letter, in which, after acknowledging the receipt of a letter from them, with the bond of indemnity, they say: "This bond, conformably to our laws, is not executed as it ought to be; but it may be otherwise in your State. It will therefore be necessary to satisfy us that the scroll is usual and legal with you instead of a seal. We notice no seal to any of the signatures. We shall write our friend Williams by this mail, and will state to him our ideas respecting the bond, which he will probably determine. If Mr. W. feels satisfied on this point, he will inform you, and in that case your draft for $2,000 will be honored." On the same day, Coolidge & Co. addressed a letter to Mr.

<br>

### --- Page 0087 ---

COOLIDGE v. PAYSON. 85

Williams, in which, after referring to him the question respecting the legal obligation of the scroll, they say: "You know the object of the bond, and, of course, see the propriety of our having one, not only legal, but signed by sureties of unquestionable responsibility, respecting which we shall wholly rely on your judgment. You mention the last surety as being responsible; what think you of the others?"

In his answer to this letter, Williams says: "I am assured that the bond transmitted in my last is sufficient for the purpose for which it was given, provided the parties possess the means; and, of the last signer, I have no hesitation in expressing my firm belief of his being able to meet the whole amount himself. Of the principals, I cannot speak with so much confidence, not being well acquainted with their resources. Under all circumstances, I should not feel inclined to withhold from them any portion of the funds for which the bond was given."

On the day on which this letter was written, Cornthwaite and Cary called on Williams, to inquire whether he had satisfied Coolidge & Co. respecting the bond. Williams stated the substance of the letter he had written, and read to him a part of it. One of the firm of Payson & Co. also called on him to make the same inquiry, to whom he gave the same information, and also read from his letter-book the letter he had written.

Two days after this, the bill in the declaration mentioned, was drawn by Cornthwaite and Cary, and paid to Payson & Co. in part of the protested bill of $2,700, by whom it was presented to Coolidge & Co., who refused to accept it, on which it was protested, and this action brought by the holders.

On this testimony, the counsel for the defendants insisted that the plaintiffs were not entitled to a verdict; but the

court instructed the jury that if they were satisfied that Williams, on the application of the plaintiffs, made after see-

<br>

### --- Page 0088 ---

FORM AND REQUISITES.

ing the letter from Coolidge & Co. to Cornthwaite and Cary, did declare that he was satisfied with the bond referred to in that letter, as well with respect to its execution, as to the sufficiency of the obligors to pay the same; and that the plaintiffs, upon the faith and credit of the said declaration, and also of the letter to Cornthwaite and Cary, and without having seen or known the contents of the letter from Coolidge & Co. to Williams, did receive and take the bill in the declaration mentioned, they were entitled to recover in the present action; and that it was no legal objection to such recovery that the promise to accept the present bill was made to the drawers thereof, previous to the existence of such bill, or that the bill had been taken in part-payment of a preexisting debt, or that the said Williams, in making the declarations aforesaid, did exceed the private instructions given to him by Coolidge & Co. in their letter to him.

To this charge the defendants excepted; a verdict was given for the plaintiffs, and judgment rendered thereon, which judgment is now before this court on a writ of error.

The letter from Coolidge & Co. to Cornthwaite and Cary contains no reference to their letter to Williams which might suggest the necessity of seeing that letter, or of obtaining information respecting its contents. They refer Cornthwaite and Cary to Williams, not for the instructions they had given him, but for his judgment and decision on the bond of indemnity. Under such circumstances, neither the drawers nor the holders of the bill could be required to know, or could be affected by, the private instructions given to Williams. It was enough for them, after seeing the letter from Coolidge & Co. to Cornthwaite and Cary, to know that Williams was satisfied with the execution of the bond and the sufficiency of the obligors, and had informed Coolidge & Co. that he was so satisfied.

This difficulty being removed, the question of law which arises from the charge given by the court to the jury is this:

<br>

### --- Page 0089 ---

COOLIDGE v. PAYSON. 87

Does a promise to accept a bill amount to an acceptance to a person who has taken it on the credit of that promise, although the promise was made before the existence of the bill, and although it is drawn in favor of a person who takes it for a pre-existing debt?

In the case of Pillans and Rose v. Van Mierop and Hopkins, 8 Burr. 1668, the credit on which the bill was drawn was given before the promise to accept was made, and the promise was made previous to the existence of the bill. Yet in that case, after two arguments and much consideration, the Court of King's Bench (all the judges being present and concurring in opinion) considered the promise to accept as an acceptance.

Between this case and that under consideration of the court, no essential distinction is perceived. But it is contended that the authority of the case of Pillans and Rose v. Van Mierop and Hopkins is impaired by subsequent decisions.

In the case of Pierson v. Dunlop et al., [2] Cowp. 571, the bill was drawn and presented before the conditional promise was made on which the suit was instituted. Although, in that case, the holder of the bill recovered as on an acceptance, it is supposed that the principles laid down by Lord Mansfield, in delivering his opinion, contradict those laid down in Pillans and Rose v. Van Mierop and Hopkins. His lordship observes: "It has been truly said, as a general rule, that the mere answer of a merchant to the drawer of a bill, saying 'he will duly honor it,' is no acceptance, unless accompanied with circumstances which may induce a third person to take the bill by indorsement; but, if there are any such circumstances, it may amount to an acceptance, though the answer be contained in a letter to the drawer."

If the case of Pillans and Rose v. Van Mierop and Hopkins had been understood to lay down the broad principle that a naked promise to accept amounts to an acceptance, the case of Pierson v. Dunlop certainly narrows that principle.

<br>

### --- Page 0090 ---

88 FORM AND REQUISITES.

principle so far as to require additional circumstances proving that the person on whom the bill was drawn was bound by his promise, either because he had funds of the drawer in his hands, or because his letter had given credit to the bill and induced a third person to take it.

It has been argued that those circumstances to which Lord Mansfield alludes must be apparent on the face of the letter. But the court can perceive no reason for this opinion. It is neither warranted by the words of Lord Mansfield, nor by the circumstances of the case in which he used them. 'The mere answer of a merchant to the drawer of a bill, saying he will duly honor it, is no acceptance unless accompanied with circumstances,' &c. The answer must be 'accompanied with circumstances;' but it is not said that the answer must contain those circumstances. In the case of Pierson v. Dunlop, the answer did not contain those circumstances. They were not found in the letter, but were entirely extrinsic. Nor can the court perceive any reason for distinguishing between circumstances which appear in the letter containing the promise, and those which are derived from other sources. The great motive for construing a promise to accept as an acceptance is that it gives credit to the bill, and may induce a third person to take it. If the letter be not shown, its contents, whatever they may be, can give no credit to the bill; and, if it be shown, an absolute promise to accept will give all the credit to the bill which a full confidence that it will be accepted can give it. A conditional promise becomes absolute when the condition is performed.

In the case of Mason v. Hunt, Doug. 296, Lord Mansfield said: 'There is no doubt but an agreement to accept may amount to an acceptance; and it may be couched in such words as to put a third person in a better condition than the drawee. If one man, to give credit to another, makes an absolute promise to accept his bill, the drawee, or any other person, may

<br>

### --- Page 0091 ---

show such promise upon the exchange to get credit; and a third person, who should advance his money upon it, would have nothing to do with the equitable circumstances which might subsist between the drawer and acceptor.

What is it that "the drawer, or any other person, may show upon the exchange?" It is the promise to accept, the naked promise. The motive of this promise need not and cannot be examined. The promise itself, when shown, gives the credit; and the merchant who makes it is bound by it.

The cases cited from Cowper and Douglas are, it is admitted, cases in which the bill is not taken for a pre-existing debt, but is purchased on the credit of the promise to accept. But in the case of Pillans v. Van Mierop, the credit was given before the promise was received or the bill drawn; and in all cases the person who receives such a bill in payment of a debt will be prevented thereby from taking other means to obtain the money due to him. Any ingredient of fraud would, unquestionably, affect the whole transaction; but the mere circumstance that the bill was taken for a pre-existing debt has not been thought sufficient to do away the effect of a promise to accept.

In the case of Johnson and another v. Collins, 1 East, 98, Lord Kenyon shows much dissatisfaction with the previous decisions on this subject; but it is not believed that the judgment given in that case would, even in England, change the law previously established. In the case of Johnson v. Collins, the promise to accept was in a letter to the drawer, and is not stated to have been shown to the indorser. Consequently, the bill does not appear to have been taken on the credit of that promise. It was a mere naked promise, unaccompanied with circumstances which might give credit to the bill. The counsel contended that this naked promise amounted to an acceptance; but the court determined otherwise. In giving his opinion, Le Blanc, J., lays down the

<br>

### --- Page 0092 ---

40 FORM AND REQUISITES.

rule in the words used by Lord Mansfield in the case of Pierson v. Dunlop; and Lord Kenyon said that “this was carrying the doctrine of implied acceptances to the utmost verge of the law; and he doubted whether it did not even go beyond it.” In Clarke and others v. Cock, 4 East, 57, the judges again express their dissatisfaction with the law as established, and their regret that any other act than a written acceptance on the bill had ever been deemed an acceptance. Yet they do not undertake to overrule the decisions which they disapprove. On the contrary, in that case they unanimously declared a letter to the drawer promising to accept a bill, which was shown to the person who held it and took it on the credit of that letter, to be a virtual acceptance. It is true, in the case of Clark v. Cock, the bill was made before the promise was given, and the judges, in their opinions, use some expressions which indicate a distinction between bills drawn before and after the date of the promise; but no case has been decided on this distinction; and, in Pillans and Rose v. Van Mierop and Hopkins, the letter was written before the bill was drawn.

The court can perceive no substantial reason for this distinction. The prevailing inducement for considering a promise to accept as an acceptance is that credit is thereby given to the bill. Now, this credit is given as entirely by a letter written before the date of the bill as by one written afterwards.

It is of much importance to merchants that this question should be at rest. Upon a review of the cases which are reported this court is of opinion, that a letter written within a reasonable time before or after the date of a bill of exchange, describing it in terms not to be mistaken, and promising to accept it, is, if shown to the person who afterwards takes the bill on the credit of the letter, a virtual acceptance binding the person who makes the promise. This is such a case.

There is, therefore, no error in the judgment of the Circuit Court, and it is affirmed with costs.
Judgment affirmed.

<br>

### --- Page 0093 ---

KONIG v. BAYARD.

William Konig, an Alien, Plaintiff below, v. WILLIAM Bayard, WILLIAM BAYARD, Jr., ROBERT BAYARD, and Jacob Le Roy.

(1 Peters, 250. Supreme Court of the United States, January, 1828.)

Acceptance supra protest by stranger.—It is no objection that a stranger has intervened as acceptor for the honor of an indorser, or that his acceptance has been made at the request and under the guaranty of the drawee. But in such case the indorser may avail himself of all defenses which he could have made had the drawee accepted for his honor and then sued upon such acceptance.

THE case is stated in the opinion of the court.

Marshall, C. J. This suit was brought in the Court of the United States for the Second Circuit and District of New York, on a bill of exchange, drawn by John C. Delprat, of Baltimore, on Messrs. N. and J. and R. Van Staphorst, of Amsterdam, in favor of Le Roy, Bayard, & Co., of New York, and indorsed by them. The bill was regularly presented, and protested, after which it was accepted and paid by the Plaintiff, for the honor of the defendants. The jury found a verdict for the plaintiff, subject to the opinion of the court, on a case stated by the parties. The judges of the Circuit Court were divided in opinion on the following points: —

1. Whether the letters offered in evidence by the defendants, and objected to, ought to have been admitted.

2. Whether the plaintiff had a right, under the circumstances, to accept and pay the bill in question, under protest, for the honor of the defendants, and is entitled to recover the amount with charges and interest.

The first question is understood to be waived. It is a question which was decided by the court at the trial, and could not arise after verdict, unless a motion had been made for a new trial.

<br>

### --- Page 0094 ---

FORM AND REQUISITES.

The second requires an examination of the case stated by counsel. The bill was transmitted by Le Roy, Bayard, & Co. to Messrs. Rougemont and Behrends, of London, to have it presented for acceptance, who enclosed it to the plaintiff in a letter, from which the following is an extract: "We beg you to have the enclosed accepted: 1st of fl. 21,500, 60 days, on N. and J. and R. Van Staphorst, and hold the same to the disposal of 2d, 8d, and 4th. You will oblige me by mentioning the day of acceptance, and, in case of refusal, you will have the bill protested."

The plaintiff gave immediate notice of the dishonor of the bill, and of their intervention for the honor of the defendants.

Messrs. N. and J. and R. Van Staphorst addressed a letter to the defendants, dated the 26th November, 1822, giving notice that the bill was dishonored, the drawer having no right to draw, and that they were advised by counsel not to interpose in their own names for the honor of the defendants. The letter adds: "In this predicament, we applied to our friends, William Konig & Co., who had the said bill in hand, informed them of the whole case, and requested these gentlemen, under our guarantee, to intervene on behalf of your signature, with acceptance and payment of the above bill; which favor these gentlemen have not refused to us; so that, without our prejudice, and completely without yours, we have duly protected your interest."

The defendants also gave in evidence a letter from the plaintiff, stating that he had intervened, at the request of N. and J. and R. Van Staphorst, and under their guarantee; but that they required him to proceed against the defendants, as preliminary to the performance of that guarantee.

It was admitted that the bill was drawn by J. C. Delprat, on his own account, and not on any shipment for a debt due from him to the defendants for advances previously made to him; and that he had given to the defendants an order on

<br>

### --- Page 0095 ---

KONIG v. BAYARD.

N. and J. and R. Van Staphorst for all balances due from them to him.

It is not alleged that the drawees had any funds of the drawer in their hands.

The plaintiff in this case must be considered as the agent of N. and J. and R. Van Staphorst, and as having paid the bill at their instance. All parties concur in stating this fact. The Van Staphorsts adopted this circuitous course, instead of interposing directly in their own names, under the advice of counsel. They, however, immediately stated the transaction in its genuine colors to the defendants. It is impossible to doubt that a person may thus intervene, through an agent, if it be his will to do so. The suspicion which might be excited by proceeding, unnecessarily, in this circuitous manner, cannot affect a transaction which was immediately communicated, with all its circumstances, to the persons in whose behalf the intervention had been made; unless those persons were exposed to some inconvenience to which they would not have been exposed had the interposition been direct. This is not the case in the present instance, since it cannot be doubted that the defendants might have availed themselves of every defence in this action of which they could have availed themselves had N. and J. and R. Van Staphorst been plaintiffs. The case shows plainly that the bill was not drawn on funds, and that the drawees were not bound to accept or pay it. No reason, therefore, can be assigned why the person who has made himself the holder of the bill, by accepting and paying it under protest, should not recover its amount from the drawer and indorsers.

This cause came on to be heard, on a certificate of division of opinion of the judges of the Circuit Court of the United States, for the Southern District of New York, and on the points on which the said judges were divided in opinion, and was argued by counsel, on consideration whereof, this court is of opinion that the plaintiff had a right, under the circum-

<br>

### --- Page 0096 ---

FORM AND REQUISITES.

stances, to accept and pay the bill in question, under protest, for the honor of the defendants, and is entitled to recover the amount, with charges and interest; which is ordered to be certified to the said Circuit Court.

§1. Promise as Maker. — It is immaterial upon what part of the paper a party places his name, if his purpose in placing it upon the paper is the execution of the contract. Rodocanachi v. Buttrick, 125 Mass. 134; National Bank v. Lougee, 108 Mass. 371; Essex Co. v. Edmands, 12 Gray, 273; Wright v. Morse, 9 Gray, 337; Lincoln v. Hinzey, 61 Ill. 435; Quin v. Sterne, 26 Ga. 228; and cases cited infra.

Hence a person may be a joint maker of a note with another, though his signature be placed upon the back of the instrument. See, for example, among the many cases to this effect, Union Bank v. Willis, ante, p. 24; Way v. Butterworth, 108 Mass. 509; Allen v. Brown, 124 Mass. 77; Phillips v. Cox, 61 Ind. 845; Herbage v. McEntee, 40 Mich. 887; Wetherwax v. Paine, 2 Mich. 655; Semple v. Turner, 65 Mo. 696; Childs v. Wyman, 44 Maine, 488; Martin v. Boyd, 11 N. H. 885; Carpenter v. Oaks, 10 Rich. Law, 17; Gilpin v. Marley, 4 Houst. 284; Baker v. Robinson, 63 N. C. 191; Ives v. Bosley, 85 Md. 262; Houghton v. Ely, 26 Wis. 181; Perkins v. Barstow, 6 R. I. 605. But to hold one as maker when his signature is out of the usual place, the fact being unexplained by the paper itself, there must be evidence sufficient to show the intention as matter of fact, or sufficient to fix the intention as matter of law. Evidence would not indeed be admissible to show in terms that one who appears to be an indorser intended to bind himself as maker. The contract of an indorser is in legal intendment a written contract, and its terms cannot, generally speaking, be affected by parol evidence. See title Indorsement, the second note, § 3, on Parol Evidence.

But, while this is true, evidence is admissible to show the time when the indorsement was executed; and if it be shown that this was prior to the negotiation of the instrument by the payee, and was a binding contract with the payee, and not merely for his accommodation, the party so signing is not, in the absence of statute, to be treated as an indorser, entitled to require demand of payment of the maker and notice of dishonor. See the cases above cited; also Greenough v. Smead, 3 Ohio St. 415; Seymour v. Mickey, 15 Ohio St. 515; Rey v. Simpson, 22 How. 841; Sylvester v. Downer, 20 Vt. 355. Contra, Hall v. Newcomb, 7 Hill, 416; and cases cited infra. If the signature, being thus substantially prior to the payee’s indorsement or negotiation of the note, was made at the same time with the execution of the note, the party, according to the more general rule, becomes a surety for, and hence jointly liable with, the maker. In other words, he is a maker of the note. If he signed subsequently to the execution of the note, and still before negotiation by the payee, he becomes a guarantor if liable at all. Ives v. McHard, 2 Bradw. (Ill.) 176; Benthall v. Judkins, 18 Met. 265; Irish v. Cutter, 81 Maine, 586. He will not be liable at all in such a case, except to an indorsee for value without notice, if there was no new consideration for his promise. Ives v. McHard, supra; and many other cases.

This is the more widely prevailing rule. But there is no little want of harmony among the authorities upon

<br>

### --- Page 0097 ---

this point. In New York, it is held that one who writes his name upon the back of commercial paper, though at the time of the execution of the same, and for the further security of the payee, is to be treated as an indorser (as to the matter of notice of dishonor), and cannot be sued as a maker. Coulter v. Richmond, 59 N. Y. 479; Hall v. Newcomb, 7 Hill, 416; Spies v. Gilmore, 1 Comat. 821; Ellis v. Brown, 6 Barb. 282; Waterbury v. Sinclair, 26 Barb. 455; Phelps v. Vischer, 60 N. Y. 69. So in Tennessee, Clouston v. Barbiere, 4 Sneed, 836. In Iowa also, Fear v. Dunlap, 1 Greene, 831. And in Pennsylvania, Mississippi, and Indiana, in the absence of evidence of intention, Eilbert v. Finkbeiner, 68 Penn. St. 243; Jennings v. Thomas, 13 Smedes & M. 617; Wells v. Jackson, 6 Blackf. 40; Nurre v. Chittenden, 56 Ind. 462. By statute in Massachusetts, such a party is now entitled to Notice of dishonor. St. 1874, c. 404. By other courts, the rule of the principal case is applied with the qualification that evidence of the facts and circumstances at the time of the transaction may be produced to show the real intention of the parties; the rule of liability as maker governing in the absence of such evidence. Sylvester v. Downer, 20 Vt. 855; Rey v. Simpson, 22 How. 841; Good v. Martin, 95 U.S. 90; Cook v. Southwick, 9 Texas, 615; Carr v. Rowland, 14 Texas, 275. See also Watson v. Hurt, 6 Gratt. 633; Clark v. Merriam, 26 Conn. 676; Schollenberger v. Nehf, 28 Penn. St.; Schneider v. Schiffman, 20 Mo.

In Ohio, Union Bank v. Willis has been criticised, and the rule of liability as maker where the signature in question was written contemporaneously with the execution of the paper, is applied, provided the signature was written for the further security of the payee; if not, the party is treated as an indorser. Greenough v. Smead, 8 Ohio St. 415; Seymour v. Leyman, 10 Ohio St. 283. And this appears now to be the rule in Massachusetts, apart from statute. Thus, where one or more indorse at the same time substantially with the execution of the paper, merely for the accommodation of the payee, to enable him to discount the same, and not to contract with him for value, the parties so signing are indorsers in the proper sense, and not otherwise liable. See the leading case of Bigelow v. Colton, post, and the note thereto, § 8, on Parol Evidence, title Indorsement; Clapp v. Rice, 13 Gray, 403.

By some courts the party thus giving his name to the note is held a guarantor. Camden v. McKoy, 8 Scam. 4387; Boynton v. Pierce, 79 Ill. 145; Stowell v. Raymond, 83 Ill. 120; Fuller v. Scott, 8 Kans. 25; Ford v. Henderson, 84 Cal. 673; Holbrook v. Camp, 38 Conn. 23. But as a guarantor stands much in the situation of a surety, being at most entitled to dishonor only when the failure to give it would prejudice him, this class of cases will not be found to differ very greatly from that represented by the principal case. See, however, Holbrook v. Camp, supra; Crooks v. Tully, 60 Cal. 254.

It will thus be seen that the rule in the principal case, holding the party liable as joint maker when signing at substantially the same time with the maker, is the more widely prevailing rule, either as fixing an absolute or a prima facie liability; and that the rule entitling the party to the rights of an indorser has not found general favor. By the weight of authority, the party promises, prima facie, as maker or as guarantor.

When a promissory note begins “I promise to pay,” and is then signed by more than one person, it is the joint and several note of the parties, in the

<br>

### --- Page 0098 ---

absence of any thing indicating the contrary in the body of the note or in connection with the signatures. Monson v. Drakeley, 40 Conn. 552; Wallace v. Jewell, 21 Ohio St. 163. It is laid down in the first of these cases that a party cannot, after the execution and delivery of a note, except in pursuance of an arrangement made before such acts, become a joint promisor and maker. See Ives v. McHard, 2 Bradw. (Ill.) 176.

Unless the signer of a promissory note or of a bill of exchange, or an indorser of either, use words which, fairly interpreted, indicate that the promise is not his promise, but the promise of another, he will be personally bound; and adding to his name words which describe him as the treasurer or other officer of a company, corporation, or association, is not sufficient to relieve him from such responsibility. Mellen v. Moore, 68 Maine, 890; Sturdivant v. Hull, 59 Maine, 172. And parol evidence is not admissible to change the party’s liability. Ib.; Bartlett v. Hawley, 120 Mass. 92; Tucker Manuf. Co. v. Fairbanks, 98 Mass. 101. Hence an indorsement in the form “A. B., Treasurer,” binds A. B. personally. Towne v. Rice, 122 Mass. 67; Bartlett v. Hawley, supra.

The fact that a note begins “we promise to pay,” and is signed by A. B., treasurer of a corporation named, does not make the note other than the note of A. B. Mellen v. Moore, 68 Maine, 890.

The words “we promise” are perfectly consistent with the signature of a single person. Whitmore v. Nickerson, 125 Mass. 496; Rice v. Gove, 22 Pick. 158; Holmes v. Sinclair, 19 Ill. 71.

When the names of both principal and agent appear in the body of a note, it will be the contract of him who signs the paper, unless (if it be signed by the agent) there be apt words to show another intention. Powers v. Briggs, 79 Ill. 493.

FORM AND REQUISITES.

Hence, a fortiori, a note executed by individuals describing themselves as trustees of a society, which shows a personal undertaking, and in which there is no attempt by apt words to bind the society, is the personal note of the signers. Burlingame v. Brewster, 79 Ill. 615. And the same is true, though to the signatures the words be added, “trustees of the” particular organization. Hayes v. Matthews, 68 Ind. 412; Hays v. Crutcher, 64 Ind. 260. Nor will the subsequent erasure of such words, without consent of the signers, alter the case, unless it was done fraudulently. Hayes v. Matthews, supra.

The question, indeed, whether a party to a bill or note has executed the same in his individual or in an official capacity must be determined by the intent as gathered from the whole instrument, however inartificially drawn, or however informally the intent may be expressed. Klosterman v. Loos, 58 Mo. 290.

If a person describe himself in the body of a note or bill as trustee, and then sign his name, adding the word “trustee,” he is not personally liable thereon. Blanchard v. Kaull, 44 Cal. 440; Jones v. Clark, 42 Cal. 180. And this has been said to be equally true where parties thus describe and sign themselves as trustees of a corporation which does not exist. Blanchard v. Kaull, supra.

Where the name of a principal is given as the heading to a bill of exchange, and the signer is described as an agent in the margin, the bill being drawn upon the principal for the payment of an obligation made by the principal, the draft will be regarded as the draft of the principal, though signed by the agent simply with his own name. Chipman v. Foster, 119 Mass. 189. See Carpenter v. Farnsworth, 106 Mass. 661. And if the name of the principal

<br>

### --- Page 0099 ---

PROMISE AS MAKER.

is given in the body of the note as the promisor, the fact that it is signed “A. B., agent,” does not make it the personal note of A. B. Whitney v. Stow, 111 Mass. 368. As to an indorsement of this kind, see Houghton v. First National Bank, 26 Wis. 663.

A note signed “A. B., Treas. for St. Paul’s Parish,” is the note of the parish. Sheridan v. Carpenter, 61 Maine, 83; Morell v. Codding, 4 Allen, 408. But if such words occur only in the body of the note, the signer is personally liable. Ib.

It is said that no case can be found in which a promise “as agent” or “as trustee,” accompanied with an express disclaimer of personal liability in the instrument, would fail to exempt the signer, Shoe & Leather Bank v. Dix, 123 Mass. 148; except perhaps where the agent is acting for an undisclosed principal. Ib.; Winsor v. Griggs, 5 Cush. 210, Wilde, J., quoting Story, Agency, §§ 266, 267. In explanation of this exception, it is said by the author just cited that, until the disclosure of

the principal, it is impossible to suppose that the other contracting party is willing to enter into a contract exonerating the agent, and trusting to an unknown principal who may be insolvent or incapable of binding himself.

The court of Maryland holds that, where by the terms of a note it is not clear whether persons executing the same did so as agents or individually, evidence of the intention may be given. Haile v. Peirce, 82 Md. 327.

It is also laid down in the same case, in general terms, that parol evidence of intention is admissible if it can be shown that the payee knew that the defendants signed in the capacity of agents. But this may be doubted. The evidence would of course be admissible if the payee was the principal, but hardly where he is not.

The mere fact that the seal of a corporation is added to the personal signatures of the directors of the company to a promissory note, does not make the note the contract of the corporation, or exempt the directors from personal liability. Dutton v. Marsh, Law Rep. 6 Q. B. 361.

The effect of the English authorities is this, that where parties in making a promissory note or accepting a bill describe themselves as directors, trustees, or agents, but do not state on the face of the instrument that it is on account or on behalf of those whom they might otherwise be considered as representing, — if they merely describe themselves as directors or the like, but do not state that they are acting on behalf of another, — they are individually liable. But, on the other hand, if they state that they are signing the note or the acceptance on account or on behalf of some company or person whom they represent, then they are not personally bound. Dutton v. Marsh, Law Rep. 6 Q. B. 361, Cockburn, C. J.; Lindus v. Melrose, 3 Hurl. & N. 177.

If a party signs the name of another as maker of a promissory note, who has not authorized him to do so, and who therefore is not bound by the signature, the signer is not liable upon the note (since that is not in terms his contract), even if he signs his own name also, as that of the agent affixing the other signature, and the party whose name he assumes to sign to the note is incapable of making such a contract. His only liability is in an action for falsely representing himself to be authorized to sign the name of the other person. Bartlett v. Tucker, 104 Mass. 336, Gray, J.; Jefts v. York, 4 Cush. 371; Long v. Colburn, 11 Mass. 97. The action may, according to good authority (notwithstanding the statement of the learned judge in Bartlett v. Tucker that it must be in tort)

<br>

### --- Page 0100 ---

be in contract for breach of an implied warranty of authority. Collin v. Wright, 7 El. & B. 301; Simons v. Patchett, ib. 568; Dickson v. Reuter’s Tel. Co., Law Rep. 8 C. P. 1, affirming Law Rep. 2 C. P. Div. 62

So far as an action upon the note is concerned, however, it is well settled that the person who takes a negotiable promissory note contracts only with those whose names appear upon the paper as parties. Bartlett v. Tucker, supra; Bank of British North Am. v. Hooper, 5 Gray, 567; Tucker Manuf. Co. v. Fairbanks, 98 Mass. 101, 104; Brown v. Parker, 7 Allen, 387; Williams v. Robbins, 16 Gray, 77.

This rule of course does not prevent an action upon the note against one who signs his initials only, or signs in cipher, if he intended thereby to bind himself. Bartlett v. Tucker, supra; Merchants’ Bank v. Spicer, 6 Wend. 448; Fuller v. Hooper, 3 Gray, 334; Williamson v. Johnson, 2 Dowl. & R. 281; 8. c. 1 Barn. & C. 146.

A promissory note, given by an executor, is his personal contract, and will not support an action against him in his official capacity, although words descriptive of his representative character are added to his signature. Christian v. Morris, 50 Ala. 688.

§ 2. Promise as Acceptor. — The drawee of a bill by the act of acceptance binds himself to pay it much as the maker of a note is bound by his signature. Supposing the acceptance to have been made without condition, the contract of the acceptor is absolute, like that of the maker of a note. The acceptor of a bill and the maker of a note are corresponding parties. The holder can bring suit against the acceptor any time after maturity of the bill without a previous demand; and this too though the bill be made payable on its face at a particular place. Wallace v. M’Connell, post, p. 66, where this subject is presented at length in § 1.

If the bill be accepted for value as between the drawer and the drawee, the latter (now become acceptor) is the party ultimately bound; and payment by him, like payment by the maker of a note executed for value, extinguishes the paper for all purposes of suit. The liability of the acceptor, however, like that of the maker of a note, does not depend upon the question whether his promise is made for value (that is, upon a consideration), except when the paper is in the hands of the drawer, or of one for whose accommodation he has accepted. Such a person could not compel him to pay, by reason of the want of consideration; but he would be liable to all others who had taken the paper for value in the due course of trade.

Acceptance may be effected in a variety of ways. Considering first the manner of effecting acceptance by a writing upon the bill, acceptance is commonly made by the drawee writing his signature across the face of the bill. This, as was decided in the principal case, Spear v. Pratt, is a binding acceptance even under a statute requiring acceptance to be made in writing, signed by the party to be charged. See Byles, Bills, 192, 193, 18th Eng. ed. It will be seen that the law in England is now the same as that declared in the principal case; the case of Hindhaugh v. Blakey, Law Rep. 3 C. P. Div. 186, to the contrary having given rise to the statute of 41 Vict. c. 13, declaring a mere signature by the drawee sufficient. Such a signature, indeed, is deemed by some of our courts a good acceptance, though it be accompanied with a statement of money paid upon the bill. Thus, the signature of the drawee of a bill under the words, "Paid on this order forty dollars," has been held an acceptance as to the whole amount of the bill; the

<br>

### --- Page 0101 ---

PROMISE AS ACCEPTOR.

words quoted indicating the party’s acknowledgment of a promise to pay the bill. Peterson v. Hubbard, 28 Mich. 197.
Haines, 9 Cal. 261; Cook v. Baldwin, 120 Mass. 317.

In the absence of statutory provision to the contrary, any words written upon the bill from which an intention to accept may reasonably be inferred will amount to an acceptance. Such is held true of such words as “accepted,” “presented,” “seen,” the day of the month, or a direction to a third person to pay the amount. Byles, Bills, 193, 18th Eng. ed. See Harper v. West, 1 Cranch, C. C. 192; Brannin v. Henderson, 12 B. Mon. 61; Peterson v. Hubbard, 28 Mich. 197. The same is true of the words “honored,” Anonymous, Comb. 401, “I will pay the bill,” Ward v. Allen, 2 Met. 58.

It is worthy of reflection, however, if some of the decisions have not gone to the verge of the law upon this matter of the interpretation of words written on the bill by the drawee; as in the case of Peterson v. Hubbard, supra. Evidence could doubtless be introduced against the drawee to show that he understood and meant his act to signify an acceptance; and as between him and the payee it would seem that, if the words did not clearly import an acceptance, evidence drawn from the res gestae at the time of the writing would be equally admissible in favor of the drawee, to show that an acceptance was not intended, or that a limited acceptance only was intended. A secret understanding of the drawee himself could not be shown in his favor; but a common understanding of the parties (the writing not being clear), whether evidenced by express words, or by acts, would always be provable. Such evidence, however, could not be introduced by the drawee

See, however, Bassett v.

against a subsequent party who had no notice of the facts when he took the bill: such a case would have to stand upon the writing, unless indeed such holder could produce evidence tending to show that the drawee had in fact intended an acceptance. In the absence of such evidence, it would seem improper to construe doubtful language into an acceptance, when the very fact of its uncertainty of import must have prevented the holder, as a man of common prudence, from acting upon it when he took the bill. He must have taken the paper upon the credit of the other parties. See upon this subject Cook v. Baldwin, 120 Mass. 637, in which part payment was held insufficient to bind the drawer as acceptor in respect of the balance. Bassett v. Haines, 9 Cal. 261.

The position of the signature of the drawee appears to be wholly immaterial. Thus, it is held that when a bill of exchange exhibits the signature of one to whom it is not directed across its face, and another name in the lower left-hand corner where that of the drawee is usually placed, the latter will be deemed the drawee, and the former will be considered the indorser, guarantor, or acceptor, supra protest, according to the evidence. Walton v. Williams, 44 Ala. 347.

The drawee’s signature, as acceptor may also be written above or below that of the drawer. Thomson, Bills, 220, referring to an unreported case (Dalrymple v. Bryson, 1810) as to a signature written above that of the drawer, and to Chitty, Bills, 199, and Gray v. Milner, 8 Moore, 90, as to a signature below. The same writer, in the same connection, says that acceptance may be indorsed.

This latter statement leads to the suggestion that just as a person who has not been a party to a note may, by signing his name upon the back of

<br>

### --- Page 0102 ---

the instrument at the time of its execution, bind himself as a joint maker (ante p. 44), one who should similarly write his name upon the back of a bill at the time of acceptance by the drawee, and for the further security of the holder, might doubtless be treated as a surety to the acceptor, and hence liable with him as an absolute promisor to pay. It may reasonably be presumed that this was the case referred to by Mr. Thomson. However, it has been held that there cannot be several acceptances of a bill drawn upon one person, and that the signature of one not a drawee or otherwise a party to the bill, but who has signed with the drawee, should be declared upon as a collateral undertaking. Jackson v. Hudson, 2 Campb. 447. That is, the party is a guarantor. But, upon the analogy of the similar act in the case of a promissory note, it may be doubted if that rule would be accepted in this country, except by those courts which deny the rule of joint makers. Ante, p. 44.

An acceptance may be given after the maturity of the bill, and then in the absence of written stipulation the bill becomes payable on demand. Byles, Bille, 192, 18th Eng. ed. It may also be accepted after a previous refusal to accept. Ib.; Wynne v. Raikes, 5 East, 514.

Acceptance by a partner, in his own name, of a bill properly drawn upon the firm binds the firm. Tolman v. Hanrahan, 44 Wis. 183.

The doctrine of the principal case, Coolidge v. Payson, that a written promise to accept an existing or a non-existing bill, described in terms not to be mistaken, is a good acceptance in favor of anyone who has duly acquired the bill in reliance upon the promise to accept, is well settled. Schimmelpennich v. Bayard, 1 Peters, 264; Boyce v. Edwards, 4 Peters, 111; Adams v. Jones, 12 Peters, 207; McEvers v. Mason, 40 Johns. 207; Exchange Bank v. Rice, 98 Mass. 288; Central Bank v. Richards, 109 Mass. 413. It matters not that the consideration for the promise has subsequently failed. Central Bank v. Richards, supra.

In Townsley v. Sumrall, supra, the court held that if the drawee has no funds in his hands, and the fact is known to the party taking the bill, and yet the inducement to take the bill is the promise of the drawee to accept it, it constitutes a valid contract between the parties, if there is a purchase of the bill on the credit of such promise.

In McEvers v. Mason, 10 Johns. 207, decided in 1813, three years prior to the decision in Coolidge v. Payson, the court drew the same distinction between the rights of one who has taken a bill on the faith of a promise to accept, and one who has not so taken it; and it was held that, as the indorsee had taken the bill in entire ignorance of any such promise, he could not recover from the defendants as implied acceptors. To the same effect, Exchange Bank v. Rice, 98 Mass. 288; s.c. 107 Mass. 87.

And a promise to accept made to a particular person is held to be a mere chose in action, and is not negotiable. No one but him to whom it was made can sue upon it. Exchange v. Rice, 107 Mass. 37, supra. See also Worcester Bank v. Wells, 8 Met. 107; Luff v. Pope, 5 Hill, 418; s.c. 7 Hill, 677. But if the person to whom the promise is made is the agent of the plaintiff, or perhaps if the plaintiff, being drawer of the bill, has drawn it upon the account and by the direction of him to whom the promise to accept was made, the plaintiff can avail himself of the promise and recover against the promisor as acceptor. Grant v. Hunt, 1 C. B. 44; s.c. 14 Law J. C. P. 106.

<br>

### --- Page 0103 ---

PROMISE AS ACCEPTOR.

It is clear that, if the drawee makes a promise to accept solely to the drawer, and not to the drawer as agent to carry the same to the holder and plaintiff, the drawee is not liable to the latter as acceptor; and this is true, though, as to the drawer, the drawee was bound at all events to accept. In this latter case, the promise has added nothing to the duty of the drawee, as he was already bound. If a promise to accept, given to the drawer, could inure to the benefit of another party, it would follow that the holder could always sue the drawee as acceptor, without a promise, when the drawee was bound towards the drawer to honor the paper, because there always is a promise in law in such a case.

But if a promise was made to the drawer to be communicated to the plaintiff, who thereupon takes the bill, not the strictest adherence to the technical rules of privity could exempt the drawee from liability to the plaintiff as acceptor. In Exchange Bank v. Rice, supra, as the facts in the second stage of the case fully show (107 Mass. 37), the drawee against a consignment having at first refused to accept the bill, because the bill of lading did not accompany it, promised the drawer to accept it when the bill of lading should arrive. The plaintiff had already discounted the bill before this promise was made, and had presented it for acceptance, and now sought to avail itself of the promise mentioned, after the arrival of the bill of lading, but without success. The plaintiff had presented the bill, and hence the defendant knew when he made the promise that the plaintiff was interested in it; but the promise was made substantively to the drawer, and not, as it seems, to him as a mere medium for communication to the plaintiff. 107 Mass. 42.

In Grant v. Hunt, supra, the promise was also made to a consignor, B., of the drawee, — the consignor not being the drawer, — but with the evident understanding that the agreement to accept was in the interest of the plaintiff, who was the drawer of the bill and creditor of the party who had obtained the promise. This promise, indeed, was made after the bill had been drawn; but, as the negotiation concerning it had begun before the drawing, the promise was probably deemed to relate to the time of drawing, at which time the plaintiff himself directly communicated with the defendant as to the drawing of the bill as previously authorized by B.

The distinction between these two cases is narrow; but it appears to lie in the fact that in Exchange Bank v. Rice the promise in legal intendment was made to the drawer, and not to the plaintiff; while in Grant v. Hunt it was made to the plaintiff; perhaps to B. also.

If the promise to accept be general, addressed to all persons who may choose to act thereon, there is of course no question of the liability of the party as promisor to any one thus acting. See Carr v. National Security Bank, 107 Mass. 45, 48.

Although doubts were expressed in McEvers v. Mason, supra, whether an agreement to accept a bill thereafter to be drawn would amount to an acceptance, or could be enforced by the indorsee, the law is now considered well settled in America that an agreement to accept is binding if the bill is drawn within a reasonable time, and such agreement was made known to the indorsee, and the bill was indorsed or negotiated on the credit of the acceptor. Per Beardsley, Senator, in Greele v. Parker, 6 Wend. 414, citing Goodrich v. De Forest, 15 Johns. 6. In Greele v. Parker, decided in 1830, Walworth, Chancellor, says: “It is a well settled rule of the commercial law of this

<br>

### --- Page 0104 ---

country, and of most of the nations of Europe, except England, where it has recently been abolished by statute, that an unconditional promise in writing to accept a bill of exchange, if made within a reasonable time before or after the date of the bill, and describing the same in terms not to be mistaken, is a virtual acceptance thereof, in favor of any person to whom such promise has been shown, and who has received the bill for a valuable consideration, on the faith of such promise.” That a promise to accept a non-existing bill constitutes an acceptance, see Steman v. Harrison, 42 Penn. St. 49; Burns v. Rowland, 40 Barb. 868; Crowell v. Van Bibber, 18 La. An. 637; Bayard v. Lathy, 2 McLean, 462; Wilson v. Clements, 3 Mass. 1; Storer v. Logan, 9 Mass. 55; Carnegie v. Morrison, 2 Met. 381; Murdock v. Mills, 11 Met. 5; Russell v. Wiggin, 2 Story, 218; Plummer v. Lyman, 49 Maine, 229, and other authorities cited passim in this note. But it must be observed that in such a case the promise must have been communicated to the indorsee, and that he took the bill on the credit thereof. Chitty, Bills, 285. It has sometimes been held that a promise to accept an existing bill may be sued upon as an acceptance, whether the holder took it on the credit of the promise or not. Jones v. Bank of Iowa, 34 Ill. 813; Read v. Marsh, 5 B. Mon. 8. But this is not the generally accepted rule. Exchange Bank v. Rice, 98 Mass. 288, is a well-considered case to the contrary, and contains a review of the authorities. See S. C. 107 Mass. 37.

In Read v. Marsh, the court say that "it seems to be now well settled that a letter promising to accept or protect a bill, whether written before or after it is drawn, may operate as an acceptance, ... although the holder has not been induced by such letter or promise to take the bill," citing Chitty, 177. 

FORM AND REQUISITES.

But the text does not support this position; and this is certainly not the law respecting non-existing bills. In this case, the letter promising to accept was written after the bill had been drawn.

Frequent expressions of regret occur that the doctrine of virtual acceptance of non-existing bills was ever advanced; and in Wildes v. Savage, 2 Story, 22, by Story, J., it is held that the doctrine must be strictly confined to the case of bills to be drawn payable on demand or after date, and never extended to those payable at or after sight. It is a little remarkable that he should say (p. 20) that he has been unable to find a single case of that kind, when he himself delivered the opinion in Payson v. Coolidge, 2 Gall. 233, the principal case in the court below; from the report of which, as given in 2 Gallison, it appears that the bill in suit, and of which the defendants were there held as acceptors, was payable at sight. No notice of the distinction drawn in Wildes v. Savage was taken either in the court below or on the appeal.

A promise to accept sent by telegraph is binding in favor of one who took the bill in reliance thereon, though the consideration of the promise fail. Central Sav. Bank v. Richards, 109 Mass. 413.

Further, an existing bill of exchange may be accepted at common law by an oral promise to pay it as well as by a written one, and also by such language and conduct on the part of the drawee, when it is presented to him, as justifies the holder in believing that he consents to pay it. Cook v. Baldwin, 120 Mass. 817; Dunavan v. Flynn, 118 Mass. 537; Pierce v. Kittredge, 115 Mass. 874; Exchange Bank v. Rice, 98 Mass. 288; Wells v. Brigham, 6 Cush. 6; Ward v. Allen, 2 Met. 58; Grant v. Shaw, 16 Mass. 341; 3 Kent, Com. 83-

<br>

### --- Page 0105 ---

PROMISE AS ACCEPTOR.

86, 12th ed. Thus, if the drawee of
a bill drawn and indorsed for his accommodation, procure the same to be
discounted and promise to pay the bill
at maturity, this constitutes him an acceptor. Bank of Rutland v. Woodruff,
34 Vt. 89.

The question whether a parol promise to accept a non-existing bill is valid
in favor of an indorsee for value who
took the bill on the faith of such
promise has several times come before
the courts of England and of this country. In Miln v. Prest, 1 Holt, 181
(1816), it is held that a parol promise
in such a case is as valid as if it were
in writing; but the contrary doctrine
is held in Bank of Ireland v. Archer, 11
Mees. & Wels. 383 (1843). And Parke,
B., in this case, says that the report of
Miln v. Prest, in Holt, is inaccurate,
and refers to 4 Camp. 893, for a correct version of it. The language of
Parke, B., seems to cover the case of a
written as well as of a parol promise to
accept a non-existing bill. This is
sustained by the opinion of eminent
English counsel in Russell v. Wiggin,
2 Story, 213; and see Chitty, Bills,
284-286. Mr. Chitty here reviews the
English cases, and considers it "at
least questionable whether a third person, who has taken a bill on the faith of such [written] promise, can treat the promise as equivalent to an acceptance." But see In re Agra, &c. Bank,
Law Rep. 2 Ch. Ap. 391 (1867), in
which it is held that such third person
may in equity compel the party to
accept who had promised to do so.

In Bank of Michigan v. Ely, 17
Wend. 508, the court say that, previously to the statute requiring acceptances to be in writing, it was settled in
that State that a parol promise to
accept a future bill was not binding,
unless the bill was taken by the holder
upon the faith and credit of such promise; citing Ontario Bank v. Worthington, 12 Wend. 693. The converse
would seem to follow from this, that, if
the holder did so take the bill, the
parol promise would be binding. To
this effect are Crowell v. Van Bibber,
18 La. An. 637; Williams v. Winans,
2 Green (N. J.), 339. Contra, Kennedy
v. Geddes, 8 Port. (Ala.) 263; Plummer v. Lyman, 49 Maine, 232. But
the promise in the last case came
within the Statute of Frauds. In Missouri, a verbal promise to accept a non-existing bill is invalid. Flato v. Mulhall, 4 Mo. App. 476. See Scudder v.
Union Bank, 91 U. S. 406.

The doctrine being settled in this
country, differently perhaps from that
of the courts of England, that a written
promise to accept a non-existing
bill under the restrictions above mentioned may be sued upon as an acceptance, there seems to be no solid
ground for the distinction between a
written and a parol promise not within
the Statute of Frauds, except where
the statute requires acceptance to be
in writing, as in England and New
York. The credit given to the indorsee is that which gives the promise its binding force; and the inducement to take the bill may be as strong
when the promise is in parol as when
it is written. That the ordinary promise to accept is not within the Statute
of Frauds is well settled. See Townsley v. Sumrall, 2 Peters, 170, and other
cases, supra. There were special circumstances in Plummer v. Lyman,
supra, which brought the promise within the statute.

The difficulty in the way of allowing
validity to an oral promise to accept a
bill not yet drawn, arises from the
danger of the evidence necessary to
support such a case. It is clear that
evidence for such a purpose should not
be permitted to avail unless the promise were clearly proved, the bill distinctly described, and the plaintiff

<br>

### --- Page 0106 ---

54,

shown to be within the terms of the promise. It is clear, however, that proof that an alleged acceptor promised to accept the bill before it was drawn, and after it was drawn verbally promised to pay it, will support an allegation of acceptance. Sturges v. Fourth National Bank, 75 Ill. 695.

Acceptance of a bill may be implied from acts, such as detention for a long time, contrary to the usage of the parties and under such circumstances as to give credit to the bill. Dunavan v. Flynn, 118 Mass. 537; Pierce v. Kittredge, 115 Mass. 374; Hough v. Loring, 24 Pick. 254, 257; Storer v. Logan, 9 Mass. 55, 60.

But where the detention of the bill has not been contrary to the usual course of dealing between the parties (as where it does not appear that they have had any other dealings), or that the drawee was under any obligation to return the bill to the holder, or detained it for any other reason than that the holder did not call for it, there is no acceptance. And this is true even though the drawee, in addition to such a detention, may have written an acceptance upon the bill, if he did not communicate the acceptance to the drawer or holder. Dunavan v. Flynn, supra; Overman v. Hoboken City Bank, 1 Vroom, 61; 8 C. 2 Vroom, 663; Cox v. Troy, 5 Barn. & Ald. 474; Jeune v. Ward, 1 Barn. & Ald. 653.

All the authorities, both English and American, hold that a check may be accepted, though acceptance is not usual. Merchants’ Bank v. State Bank, 10 Wall. 604, Swayne, J.; Robson v. Bennet, 2 Taunt. 888, 305; Grant, Banking, 89; Chitty, Bills, 10th ed., 261; Boyd v. Emmerson, 2 Adol. & Ellis, 184; Kilsby v. Williams, 5 Barn. & Ald. 816; Story, Promissory Notes, §§ 489, 490. And the certificate of the bank upon which a check is drawn that the check is good is equivalent to acceptance in the case of a bill. Merchants’ Bank v. State Bank, supra; Marine Bank v. National Bank, 59 N. Y. 67; Security Bank v. National Bank, 67 N. Y. 458. But the certification or acceptance of a check would not add grace to it. First National Bank v. Leach, 52 N. Y. 350.

The certification, as was said by Mr. Justice Swayne in Merchants’ Bank v. State Bank, supra, “implies that the check is drawn upon sufficient funds in the hands of the drawee, that they have been set apart for its satisfaction, and that they shall be so applied whenever the check is presented for payment. It is an undertaking that the check is good then, and shall continue good [Cooke v. State Bank, 52 N. Y. 96]; and this agreement is as binding on the bank as its notes of circulation, a certificate of deposit payable to the order of the depositor, or any other obligation it can assume. The object of certifying a check, as regards both parties, is to enable the holder to use it as money. The transferee takes it with the same readiness and sense of security that he would take the notes of the bank. It is available also to him for all the purposes of money. Thus it continues to perform its important functions until, in the course of business, it goes back to the bank for redemption, and is extinguished by payment.”

But in some States it is held that the teller of a bank has no authority as such to certify checks to be good, so as to bind the bank to pay the amount thereof to any person who may afterwards present it, and that a usage for him so to do, to enable the holder to use the check at his pleasure, is bad. Mussey v. Eagle Bank, 9 Met. 306. On the other hand, it is held in New York that a bona fide holder for value of a negotiable check, certified

<br>

### --- Page 0107 ---

PROMISE AS ACCEPTOR.

to be good by the paying teller of the bank on which it is drawn, whose authority to certify is limited to cases where the bank has funds of the drawer to meet the check, can recover of the bank the amount of the check, though the drawer had no funds in the bank, and though the certification by the teller was in violation of his duty, and for the drawer’s accommodation. Farmers’ & M. Bank v. Butchers’ & D. Bank, 16 N. Y. 125.

Notice by a bank of refusal to adopt the general custom of banks to certify checks does not exempt the bank from liability upon a check certified by its cashier in favor of a holder for value, without knowledge of such refusal. Cooke v. State Bank, 562 N. Y. 96.

The certification of a check by a person as “assistant cashier” is sufficient to put the holder upon inquiry as to the authority of the party to certify, where the general custom is for the cashier to make the certification. Pope v. Bank of Albion, 57 N. Y. 126. The authority of a subordinate officer or clerk to do any act out of the ordinary routine of business must be shown to bind the bank. Ib.; Potter v. Merchants’ Bank, 28 N. Y. 641.

But where a subordinate officer or clerk has been permitted to pursue a particular practice in certifying checks, for customers or others, his acts, though wrongful, will bind the bank in favor of a person who fulfills the conditions of a holder in good faith. Pope v. Bank of Albion, supra; Farmers’ & M. Bank v. Butchers’ & D. Bank, 16 N. Y. 126.

According to the New York authorities, the certification of a check as good differs in effect in one important particular from the acceptance of a bill. It is held to amount to a discharge of the drawer, and payment by him of that for which the check was drawn. First National Bank v. Leach, 52 N. Y. 850.

But see contra, Bickford v. First National Bank, 42 Ill. 238.

On the other hand, the act amounts to an affirmation of the genuineness of the signature of the drawer. Cook v. State Bank, 62 N. Y. 96. But it does not warrant the genuineness of the body of the check. Marine Bank v. National Bank, 59 N. Y. 67; Security Bank v. National Bank, 67 N. Y. 458.

Little need be said upon the subject of conditional and qualified acceptances. It should be observed that such acceptances do not affect the bill as a bill; the condition being external to the body of the instrument. But in any action upon the bill, whether against the acceptor, drawer, or indorsers, it devolves upon the plaintiff to allege and prove the performance of the condition; and even then, if the condition was received without the consent of any of the parties whose names were then upon the paper, the plaintiff will not be entitled to recover against such parties. Chitty, Bills, 300, 9th Eng. ed.; Byles, Bills, 795, 4th Eng. ed. The holder should have insisted upon an absolute acceptance, failing of which he should have given notice of dishonor to all parties (standing upon their rights) whom he wished to hold. Parties indorsing subsequently, however, indorse with reference to the condition attached to the acceptance.

After receiving a conditional acceptance, the holder should not give notice of dishonor, for this would be to preclude himself from suing the acceptor. Sproat v. Matthews, 1 T. R. 182; Bentinck v. Dorrien, 6 East, 200; Byles, ut supra. It seems that an offer of an absolute acceptance as to part of the sum named in the bill may be received without the consent of the other parties; though notice of the partial acceptance should be given them. Chitty, Bills, 300, 9th Eng. ed.

<br>

### --- Page 0108 ---

56 FORM AND

Farther than this, the rules touching conditional acceptances relate to the construction of the language used in the acceptance. A few examples may be given.

In Wintermute v. Post, 4 Zabr. 420, the force of an acceptance “when in funds,” came under consideration. “The term ‘when in funds’ literally means when the acceptor is in the possession of cash which the drawer has a present right to demand and receive or to appropriate by his bill, whether such funds be the product of labor or of commodities furnished, of goods sold or money deposited or collected, or any other source. And such, in my judgment, is its fair commercial and judicial construction.” Per Haines, J. In this case S., a day laborer, drew on his employer, P. (to whom S. was indebted), in favor of the plaintiff. P. wrote upon the bill “accepted when in funds.” S. continued to draw his wages as he earned them; and the court held that it was not to be supposed that the parties meant that the pittance of each day’s work should be withheld from the necessities of the laborer’s family till they could accumulate the amount of the bill. But if after such appropriation for the necessaries of life a balance should be left in the hands of the acceptor, then his acceptance would become absolute, and he would be bound to pay, and not till then. Ib. 424.

See Campbell v. Pettengill, 7 Greenl. 126, where it was determined, in construing the same expression, that available securities were not funds until actually converted into money. See also Hunter v. Ingraham, 1 Strob. 271; Gallery v. Prindle, 14 Barb. 186; Owen v. Iglanor, 4 Cold. 15.

And the burden of proof in such case is of course on the plaintiff, in an action against the drawer, to show funds in the hands of the acceptor.

REQUISITES.

Andrews v. Baggs, Minor, 178. See Owen v. Lavine, 14 Ark. 389.

If the funds are not received in the lifetime of the acceptor, but are collected by his administrator, the latter is liable in his representative character upon the acceptance of the deceased. Swansey v. Breck, 10 Ala, 533.

In Perry v. Harrington, 2 Met. 368, it was held that an acceptance to pay a certain sum out of the first money received by the drawee, bound the acceptor to pay, from time to time, on reasonable request, such funds as he received of the drawer; and that a judgment for a sum which he had refused to pay was no bar to an action for a further sum received since the first action.

The following are further examples of conditional or qualified acceptances: An acceptance to pay “as remitted for;” Banbury v. Lissett, 2 Strange, 1212; or “on account of the ship Thetis, when in cash, for the said vessel’s cargo;” Julian v. Shobrooke, 2 Wils. 9; or a promise to accept a returned bill “when it shall come back;” Cox v. Coleman, cited in Lumley v. Palmer, Hardw. 74; or to accept, “as soon as he should sell such goods;” Smith v. Abbott, 2 Strange, 1152; or an answer that “the bill would not be accepted until a navy bill was paid;” Pierson v. Dunlop, 2 Cowp. 571; or “until the ship with the wheat arrived;” Milne v. Prest, 4 Camp. 393; or that “he would have accepted the bills if he had had funds (meaning the fund on which the bills were drawn), that he had not been able to obtain those funds from France, but that when he did obtain them he would pay the bills;” Mendizabel v. Machado, 8 Moore & S. 841; or “that the drawer had consigned a ship and cargo to him (the drawee) and another person at Bristol, but that as he could not then tell whether the ship would arrive at

<br>

### --- Page 0109 ---

PROMISE AS ACCEPTOR.

London or at Bristol he could not accept at that time;" Sproat v. Matthews, 1 T. R. 182; or, to pay "if a certain house should be given up to the drawee before a named day." Swan v. Cox, 1 Marsh. 177; Chitty, Bills, 302.

If a person intend to make a conditional acceptance, and accept in writing, he should be careful to express the condition; for if the acceptance be in writing, but the condition not, he clearly cannot prove the existence of it against any subsequent party, if such party or any intermediate one between him and the person to whom the acceptance was given took the bill without notice of the condition and gave a valuable consideration for it. Chitty, Bills, 302.

And the burden will be upon the acceptor to prove the condition and notice thereof, or that the plaintiff is not a holder for value. Ib.

And, if the undertaking of the alleged conditional acceptor be in writing, it is difficult to understand how evidence of the existence of a condition not expressed could be received even in an action by the party to whom the acceptance was given. The engagement of the acceptor, though expressed merely by the name of the drawer is a written contract, as was held in the principal case, Spear v. Pratt; and this being the case, parol evidence would not be admissible to vary its terms. Hoare v. Graham, 8 Camp. 67; Adams v. Wordley, 1 Mees. & W. 874; Besant v. Cross, 10 C. B. 895. And even a written condition, if on a separate paper, would not be admissible against one claiming subsequently as a bona fide holder for value. Bowerbank v. Monteiro, 4 Taunt. 844. The case is analogous to that of parol evidence to vary the legal effect of an indorsement in blank, as to which see the second note under title Indorsement, § 3.

A conditional acceptor is not liable, though compliance is prevented by the operation of law. Browne v. Coit, 1 McCord, 408. In this case, the defendant accepted a bill upon condition that he should sell certain goods of the drawer in his hands, which goods were attached before the maturity of the bill, and before they had been sold. The court held that the defendant was not liable.

Acceptance supra protest, called also acceptance for honor, is not common in this country, but the subject cannot be omitted. Such an acceptance may be given not only upon a refusal by the drawee to accept, as in the principal case, Konig v. Bayard; but an accepted bill which has been dishonored may be accepted again for honor upon the insolvency of the drawee. See Ex parte Wackerbath, 5 Ves. 574; Ex parte Lambert, 13 Ves. 179.

The acceptor supra protest is considered in the light of an indorser; and therefore at maturity the bill must be again presented to the drawee, and if still dishonored by him it should be protested, and notice given to the acceptor for honor, or he will be discharged. Hoare v. Cazenove, 16 East, 391; Williams v. Germaine, 7 B. & C. 468; Schofield v. Bayard, 8 Wend. 488; Lenox v. Leverett, 10 Mass. 1.

The mode of accepting supra protest is said to be as follows: The acceptor supra protest must personally appear before a notary-public, with witnesses, and declare that he accepts such protested bill in honor of the drawer or indorser, or of all the parties, as the case may be, and that he will satisfy the same at the appointed time; and then he must subscribe the bill with his own hand, thus—"Accepted supra protest, in honor of A. B.," or as is more usual, "Accepts S. P." (supra protest). Byles, Bills, 267, 13th Eng. ed.

<br>

### --- Page 0110 ---

A general acceptance, supra protest, not specifying for whose honor it is made, is not prima facie an acceptance for all parties, but for the drawer. Chitty, Bills, 344, 9th Eng. ed.; Byles, ut supra. But the drawee of a bill or check which he is not bound to honor may refuse to honor it when presented to him for payment, and purchase it supra protest, acquiring a right of action thereby against all the parties to whom notice of the dishonor has been duly given. Swope v. Ross, 40 Penn. St. 186. Indeed, after a protest, any other party, or a stranger, may, by the English law, pay the same for the honor of any party to the bill, and thereby acquire the rights of an indorser against the party for whose honor he has paid, and against all parties liable to that party. This course of payment supra protest should be preceded by a declaration, in the presence of a notary, made by the payer, stating for whose honor the bill is paid; and the declaration should be recorded by the notary either in the protest or in a separate instrument. Notice to the party to be protected should also be given. Gazzam v. Armstrong, 3 Dana, 554; Wood v. Pugh, 7 Ohio, 156, Curwen’s ed. 501. See Geralopulo v. Wieler, 10 C. B. 690, 709; Phenix Bank v. Hussey, 12 Pick. 483. Failure to give such notice within a reasonable time will be fatal to the party paying. Wood v. Pugh, supra.

Such payment, however, is extremely rare, and can hardly be made except when the holder of a bill requiring indorsement refuses to indorse even sans recours, for even that indorsement would warrant the genuineness of the whole bill. See upon this subject Byles, Bills, 272, 13th Eng. ed. This, it may be added, is not acceptance supra protest, but payment supra protest.

Any one may accept a bill supra protest; and the drawee himself, though he may refuse to accept the bill generally, may yet, as we have just said, accept it supra protest for the honor of the drawer or of an indorser, provided the situation be not such that he was bound to accept in the usual manner. Schimmelpennich v. Bayard, 1 Peters, 264. If the drawee of a bill, refusing to honor it, were bound to accept it, he would not be permitted to change the relation in which he stood to the parties on the bill by a wrongful act. The drawee in such a case can acquire no rights as holder of the bill, paid supra protest, if he were bound to honor it in his character of drawee. Ib. As one bound to accept, he could of course have no right of action against any of the parties to the bill. Ib. And of course if a drawee accept, he cannot dishonor the bill when presented to him for payment, and then accept supra protest.

As to when a drawee is bound to accept, it is laid down in the case just cited, that a drawee who has been in the habit of receiving consignments from the drawer, with whom he has an open account therefor, is not bound to accept bills drawn upon him against a particular shipment, which bills the drawer in his letter of advice says may be charged in account, if the account actually show that the drawer had no funds in the hands of the drawee.

If, however, a bill be drawn in strict pursuance of an authority granted by the drawee, the latter is bound to accept, so far as any question of a right of action by him as acceptor supra protest against any of the indorsers is concerned. Ib. And this probably is the general test; the question being whether the drawee has expressly or by clear implication agreed to accept the particular bill. -

FORM AND REQUISITES.

But though the acceptor supra protest may have been bound to accept in

<br>

### --- Page 0111 ---

PROMISE AS ACCEPTOR.
