they can have no such tendency that we forbear to pursue the subject. Ayer v. Hutchins, 4 Mass. 370; Wiggin v. Bush, 12 Johns. 806; Cone v. Baldwin, 12 Pick. 545; Brown v. Taber, 5 Wend. 566.

But it is a very different matter, when it is proposed to impeach the title of a holder for value, by proof of any facts and circumstances outside of the instrument itself. He is then to be affected, if at all, by what has occurred between other parties, and he may well claim an exemption from any consequences flowing from their acts, unless it be first shown that he had knowledge of such facts and circumstances at the time the transfer was made. Nothing less than proof of knowledge of such facts and circumstances can meet the exigencies of such a defence; else the proposition as stated is not true, that a party who acquires commercial paper in the usual course of business, for value and without notice of any defect in the title, may hold it free of all equities between the antecedent parties to the instrument. Admit the proposition, and the conclusion follows. And the question whether the party had such knowledge or not is a question of fact for the jury, and, like other disputed questions of scienter, must be submitted to their determination, under the instructions of the court; and the proper inquiry is, Did the party seeking to enforce the payment have knowledge, at the time of the transfer, of the facts and circumstances which impeach the title, as between the antecedent parties to the instrument? and, if the jury find that he did not, then he is entitled to recover, unless the transaction was attended by bad faith, even though the instrument had been lost or stolen. Every one must conduct himself honestly in respect to the antecedent parties, when he takes negotiable paper, in order to acquire a title which will shield him against prior equities. While he is not obliged to make inquiries, he must not wilfully shut his eyes to the means of knowledge which he knows are at hand, as was plainly intimated by Baron Parke, in May

<br>

### --- Page 0482 ---

430 BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

v. Chapman, 16 Mees. & W. 355, for the reason that such conduct, whether equivalent to notice or not, would be plenary evidence of bad faith. Mere want of care and caution, which was the criterion assumed in the instruction, falls so far below the true standard required by law, which is knowledge of the facts and circumstances that impeach the title, that we feel indisposed to pursue the general discussion, and proceed to confirm the views we have advanced as to what the law is by referring to some of the decisions in the English courts, from which, as an important source of commercial law, most of our own rules upon the subject have been derived.

The leading case, among the more modern decisions in that country, is that of Goodman v. Harvey, 4 Adol. & Ellis, 870. That was a case in bank, on a rule nisi, which was made absolute. Lord Denman, in delivering judgment, said: “* We are all of opinion that gross negligence only would not be a sufficient answer, where a party has given consideration for the bill; gross negligence may be evidence of mala fides, but it is not the same thing. Where the bill has passed to the plaintiff without any proof of bad faith in him, there is no objection to his title.” That case was followed by Uther v. Rich, 10 Adol. & Ellis, 784, which was also argued before a full court, and the same learned judge held that the only proper mode of implicating the plaintiff in the alleged fraud by pleading was to aver that he had notice of it, leaving the circumstances by which that notice was to be proved, directly or indirectly, to be established in evidence; and he further held that an averment that the plaintiff was not a bona fide holder was not equivalent. According to the rule laid down in Goodman v. Harvey, which indubitably is the settled law in all the English courts, proof that the plaintiff had been guilty of gross negligence in acquiring the bill ought not to defeat his right to recover; and if not, it serves to exemplify the magnitude of the error assumed in the instruction, that any facts and circumstances which would excite the suspicion

<br>

### --- Page 0483 ---

GOODMAN v. SIMONDS. 431

of a careful and prudent man were sufficient to destroy the title. It is clear that one or the other of these rules must be incorrect; both cannot be upheld. Gross negligence is defined to consist of the omission of that care which even inattentive and thoughtless men never fail to take of their own property; and, if such neglect would not defeat the right to recover,—and clearly it would not, unless attended by bad faith,—it cannot require any further reasoning to demonstrate that the instruction was erroneous. Several cases have been decided in England upon the same subject and to the same effect; and the rule laid down in Goodman v. Harvey is now adopted and sanctioned by the most approved elementary treatises upon commercial law. Raphael v. The Bank of England, 33 Eng. L. & Eq. 276; Palmer v. Richards, 1 Eng. L. & Eq. 529; Arbouin v. Anderson, 1 Adol. & Ellis, Nn. 8. 498; May v. Chapman, 16 Mees. & W. 355; Chitty, Bills, 12th ed., 257; Story, Bills, 3d ed., § 416; Byles, Bills, 4th Am. ed., 121–126; Smith’s Mer. Law, ed. 1837, 255; Edwards, Bills, 309; 1 Saund. Pl. & Ev. 591; Wheeler v. Guild, 20 Pick. 545; Brush v. Scribner, 11 Conn. 868; Backhouse v. Harrison, 5 Barn. & Adol. 1098; Gwynn v. Lee, 9 Gill, 138.

These cases, beyond controversy, confirm the rule laid down by this court in Swift v. Tyson, and they also furnish the fullest evidence, by their harmony each with the other, as well as by their entire consistency with the principal case, that the law has been uniform since the decision in Goodman v. Harvey, which was decided in 1836; and we think it will appear, upon an examination, that it has always been the same, at least from a very early period in the history of English jurisprudence down to the present time, except for an interval of about twelve years, while the doctrine prevailed which is now invoked in support of the instruction in this case. That doctrine had its origin in Gill v. Cubitt, 8 Barn. & C. 466, and it was followed by the other cases referred to

<br>

### --- Page 0484 ---

432 BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

in the printed argument for defendant. It was decided in 1824, and it is true, as the cases cited abundantly show, that it was acquiesced in for a time as a correct exposition of the commercial law upon the subject under consideration. At the same time, it is proper to remark that there is not wanting respectable authority that it had been much disapproved of before it was directly questioned; and it is certain that nearly two years before it was finally overruled, Parke, B., in delivering judgment in Foster v. Pearson, regarded it as mere “dicta, rather than the decision of the judges of the King’s Bench.” See Raphael v. The Bank of England, supra, per Cresswell. The reasons assigned for that departure from the long-established rule upon the subject are as remarkable and unsatisfactory as the change was sudden and radical, and yet their particular examination at this time is unnecessary. It is a sufficient answer to the case to say that it has been distinctly overruled in the tribunal where it was decided, and has not been considered an authority in that court for more than twenty years. The doctrine, says Mr. Chitty in his Treatise on Bills, is now completely exploded, and the old rule of law that the holder of bills of exchange, indorsed in blank and transferable by delivery, can give a title which he does not possess, to a person taking them bona fide for value, is again re-established in its fullest extent. It was not, however, accomplished at a single blow, but the error, so to speak, was literally broken up and destroyed by instalments. The foundation of the superstructure was severely shaken in Crook v. Jadis, 5 Barn. & Adol. 909, when the full bench first came to the conclusion that want of due care and caution was insufficient to constitute a defence, and that gross negligence, at least, must be shown to defeat a recovery. But it was left to the case of Goodman v. Harvey to announce a complete correction of the error, when Lord Denman declared, “We have shaken off the last remnant of the contrary doctrine.”

<br>

### --- Page 0485 ---

GOODMAN v. SIMONDS. 433

A brief reference to some of the earlier cases will be sufficient to show that the decision in Gill v. Cubitt was a departure from the well-known and long-established rule upon the subject under consideration. One of the earliest cases usually referred to is that of Hinton’s Case, reported in 2 Show. 247. It was an action on the case against the drawer upon a bill of exchange payable to bearer. The court ruled that the holder must entitle himself to it on a consideration, “for, if he come to be bearer by casualty or knavery, he shall not have the benefit of it;” and so in Anonymous, 1 Salk. 126, where a bank-note, payable to A. or bearer, was lost, and found by a stranger, and by him transferred to C. for value. Holt, C. J., held that “A. might have trover against the stranger, for he had no title to it, but not against C. by reason of the course of trade, which creates a property in the bearer.” And again in Miller v. Race, 1 Burr. 452, 462, where an innkeeper received a bank-note from his lodger in the course of business, and paid the balance, Lord Mansfield held he might retain it, as he came by it fairly and bona fide and for value, and without knowledge that it had been stolen. And, on a second occasion, in Grant v. Vaughan, 3 Burr. 1516, where a bill payable to bearer was lost, and the finder passed it to the plaintiff, the same court left it to the jury to find whether he came to the possession fairly and bona fide. But a still stronger case is that of Peacock v. Rhodes, 2 Doug. 632, where a bill of exchange, indorsed in blank, was stolen and passed to the plaintiff by a man not known. It was argued for the defendant that a holder should not in prudence take a bill unless he knew the person. Lord Mansfield answered, “that the law is well settled that a holder, coming fairly by a bill, has nothing to do with the transaction between the original parties. The question of mala fides was for the consideration of the jury.” And, lastly, and to the same effect, is Lawson v. Weston et al., 4 Esp. 56, where a bill of exchange for £500 was lost or stolen, and was discounted by plaintiff for a

<br>

### --- Page 0486 ---

434 BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

stranger. It was insisted for the defendant that “a banker or any other person should not discount a bill for one unknown, without using diligence to inquire into the circumstances.” Lord Kenyon replied that “to adopt the principles of the defence would be to paralyze the circulation of all the paper in the country, and with it all its commerce; that the circumstance of the bill having been lost might have been material, if they could bring knowledge of that fact home to the plaintiff.” The cases cited, commencing in 1694, and ending in 1801, are sufficient to show what the state of the law was in 1824, when Gill v. Cubitt was decided, especially as the judges of the King’s Bench, in giving their opinions on that occasion, did not pretend that there were any later decisions in which it had been modified.

III. But, assuming that the instruction was erroneous, it is still insisted by the course of the argument for the defendant, that it was immaterial; and the argument proceeds upon the ground that the case, as made in the bill of exceptions, shows that the plaintiff was not the holder of the bill for a valuable consideration, in the usual course of business. On the contrary, it is insisted that he held it merely as a collateral security for a pre-existing debt, without any present consideration at the time of the transfer, and that a party who takes negotiable paper under such circumstances does not acquire it in the usual course of business, and consequently takes it subject to prior equities. Whatever may be our impressions in a case like the one supposed, we think the question does not arise in the present record, assuming the facts to be as they are exhibited in the bill of exceptions; and the answer to the argument will be based entirely upon that assumption, without prejudice to what may hereafter appear. When the settlement was made, the new notes were given in payment of the prior indebtedness and the collaterals previously held were surrendered to the defendant, and the time of payment was extended and definitively fixed.

<br>

### --- Page 0487 ---

GOODMAN v. SIMONDS

by the terms of the notes, showing an agreement to give time for the payment of a debt already overdue, and a forbearance to enforce remedies for its recovery; and the implication is very strong that the delay secured by the arrangement constituted the principal inducement to the transfer of the bill. Such a suspension of an existing demand is frequently of the utmost importance to a debtor, and it constitutes one of the oldest titles of the law under the head of forbearance, and has always been considered a sufficient and valid consideration. Elting v. Vanderlyn, 4 Johns. 487; Morton v. Burn, 7 Adol. & Ellis, 19; Baker v. Walker, 14 Mees. & W. 465; Jennison v. Stafford, 1 Cush. 168; Walton v. Mascall, 138 Mees. & W. 453; Com. Dig. action assumpsit, B. 1; Wheeler v. Slocum, 16 Pick. 62; Story, Promissory Notes, § 186, and cases cited. The surrender of other instruments, although held as collateral security, is also a good consideration; and this, as well as the former proposition, is now generally admitted, and is not open to dispute. Dupeau v. Waddington, 6 Whart. 220; Hornblower v. Proud, 2 Barn. & Ald. 327; Rideout v. Bristow, 1 Cromp. & J. 231; Bank of Salina v. Babcock, 21 Wend. 499; Youngs v. Lee, 2 Ker. 551. It seems now to be agreed that, if there was a present consideration at the time of the transfer, independent of the previous indebtedness, a party acquiring a negotiable instrument before its maturity as a collateral security to a pre-existing debt, without knowledge of the facts which impeach the title as between the antecedent parties, thereby becomes a holder in the usual course of business, and that his title is complete so that it will be unaffected by any prior equities between other parties, at least to the extent of the previous debt for which it is held as collateral. White v. Springfield Bank, 8 Sandf. S. C. 222; New York M. Iron Works v. Smith, 4 Duer, 362. And the better opinion seems to be in respect to parol contracts, as a general rule, that there is but one measure of the sufficiency of a consideration,

<br>

### --- Page 0488 ---

436 BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

and, consequently, whatever would have given validity to the bill, as between the original parties, is sufficient to uphold a transfer like the one in this case. We are not aware that the principle, as thus limited and qualified, is now the subject of serious dispute anywhere, and that is amply sufficient for the decision of this cause. Whether the same conclusion ought to follow where the transfer was without any other consideration than what flows from the nature of the contract at the time of the delivery, and such as may be inferred from the relation of debtor and creditor in respect to the pre-existing debt, is still the subject of earnest discussion, and has given rise to no small diversity of judicial decision. It seems it is regarded as sufficient in England, according to a recent case. Poirier v. Morris, 20 Eng. L. & Eq. 108; Byles, Bills, pp. 96, 127. A contrary rule prevails in New York, as appears by several decisions. Coddington v. Bay, 20 Johns. 637; Stalker v. McDonald, 6 Hill, 93; and also in Tennessee, Napier v. Elam, 5 Yerg. 108. It is settled that it is a sufficient consideration in Massachusetts, Vermont, and New Jersey, and such was the opinion of the late Justice Story, as appears from his remarks in Swift v. Tyson, and in his valuable treatise on Bills of Exchange. Stoddard v. Kimball, 6 Cush. 469; Story, Bills, § 192; Chicopee Bank v. Chapin, 8 Met. 40; Blanchard v. Stevens, 3 Cush. 162; Atkinson v. Brooks, 26 Vt. 569; Allaire v. Hartshorne, 1 Zabr. 665. We think, however, that the point does not arise in this case, for the reasons before stated, and consequently forbear to express any opinion upon the subject.

The judgment of the Circuit Court is reversed, and the cause remanded for further proceedings, with directions to issue a new venire.

§ 1. Position of an Indorsee.—It is a fundamental proposition of the law of bills of exchange, promissory notes, and checks, as we have seen in the cases and notes last preceding, that an indorsee has a prima facie right of action in his own name against any prior party to the paper. And this, when the indorsee is not suing as agent, trustee, or the like for some other actual owner, is equivalent to declaring that an indorsee may in some cases

<br>

### --- Page 0489 ---

ACTUAL NOTICE OF DEFENCE.

(in fact in most cases) recover judgment against another party though that party has a good defence to an action by any other person than the present plaintiff. Freeman’s National Bank v. Savery, 127 Mass. 75, 79. This is always, prima facie, the position of an indorsee. All the presumptions are in his favor. If his paper title be perfect, he is presumed in the first instance to be both a bona fide holder and a holder for value, entitled to recover the whole sum named in the bill, note, or check. He need not allege these facts in his declaration; nor will he be called upon to prove them, unless (1) the defendant has denied the presumption by a plea which states that either in one or in both of the particulars thereof (the holding bona fide and for value) the plaintiff’s claim is invalid, or unless (2) the defendant pleads that the paper is absolutely void, or unless (3) he pleads that some indorsement upon the paper is not genuine. Each of these three defences is to be considered in the notes (as well as in the principal cases) under the present head of Bona Fide Holder for Value.

§ 2. Actual Notice of Defence.—The defendant may then repel the presumption in favor of the plaintiff-indorsee by alleging facts which show that the plaintiff is not a bona fide holder; and if the proof sustain the plea, the action fails. Fisher v. Leland, ante, p. 396.

Now the term “bona fide holder” as commonly used, and as here used, means a holder who has taken the bill, note, or check without notice of any defence (often called an equity) of which the defendant could avail himself in an action by a prior holder of the paper. Such notice may be actual or it may be constructive. It is actual when the defence is directly made known to the holder; it is constructive when it consists of facts which in law demand inquiry. And first of actual notice.

It should be stated at the outset, in accordance with Baxter v. Little, ante, p. 399, that in any case the defences or equities of which the plaintiff is alleged to have notice, must be facts relating directly to the execution of the defendant’s contract as maker, acceptor, drawer, or indorser, and not (except by statute) of mere matters of set-off, the defendant showing the contract not to have been binding upon him when executed. Young v. Shriner, 80 Penn. St. 463.

The plaintiff cannot be affected with notice of other dealings of the defendant than those immediately connected with the contract sued upon; and it devolves upon the defendant to show the connection between the facts of which the plaintiff is alleged to have notice and the execution of the contract in question. Illegal conduct or bad faith in one case is not notice of it, per se, in another, though the two may be nearly related. Bottomley v. Goldsmith, 36 Mich. 27; Miller v. Consolidation Bank, 48 Penn. St. 514; Moorehead v. Gilmore, 77 Penn. St. 118; Ihmsen v. Negley, 25 Penn. St. 297; Freeman’s National Bank v. Savery, 127 Mass. 76, 80.

A similar question to that in Baxter v. Little arose in Britton v. Bishop, 11 Vt. 70, and the same decision was reached. Referring to the English rule in Burrough v. Moss, 10 Barn. & C. 658, it was said that the true rule was that the indorsee of an overdue note was liable to all equities arising out of the note transaction itself, and to the application of demands due the maker from the payee when there was any agreement to that effect. But that was the extent of the rule. The rule in Sargent v. Southgate, 6 Pick. 312, which permitted a plea of set-off against

<br>

### --- Page 0490 ---

438

the indorsee, available against the payee, in such a case was treated as an equitable construction of the Massachusetts statute relating to set-off. See also Bond v. Fitzpatrick, 4 Gray, 89; Burnham v. Tucker, 18 Maine, 179; Haywood v. McNair, 2 Dev. & B. 283.

A set-off against the holder of paper taken before maturity is not an admissible defence, even when known to the purchaser when he took the paper. Barker v. Valentine, 10 Gray, 841; Flint v. Flint, 6 Allen, 84. If, however, there be an agreement to accept payment by application upon other outstanding notes due the maker from the payee, that will be a valid defence in such a case. Staley v. Matheny, 80 Ga. 937. So a promissory note, negotiable but not indorsed, given for stock subscribed in a railway corporation, and secured by mortgage, if the corporation give the maker a counter contract, guaranteeing him against loss upon the stock, such counter contract will be a defence against a bill for foreclosure of the mortgage, the stock having become worthless. Peck v. Bligh, 87 Ill. 317.

It was held in Oulds v. Harrison, 10 Ex. 679, that the right of an indorsee of an overdue bill of exchange to sue the acceptor is not defeated by the existence of a debt due from the drawer to the acceptor, and notice by the latter to the drawer, before indorsement, of his election to set off the amount against the bill; nor is the indorsee of such overdue bill of exchange affected by the existence of a right of set-off as between the acceptor and the drawer, although the bill was indorsed without value and for the purpose of defeating the set-off. Parke, B., said: “This plea, though inaccurately stated, we think amounts to an averment that both the indorser and the indorsee knew that there was a debt due, and that the defendant would probably set it off, if the action were brought by the indorser against the defendant, knowing there would probably be a set-off (because it was not quite certain that the debt would still remain due); but knowing there would probably be a set-off, they fraudulently, so far as it was a fraud in law, and no further, agreed that the bill should be indorsed; and it was therefore indorsed without value to the plaintiff. . . . The holder’s power to circulate it is not restrained simply by the existence, at the time, of a debt of equal value, and his circulating it is no infringement of any existing right of the defendant. ... Does it become a fraud in defeating the title, if he actually intends to do that which, under the circumstances, would be the necessary result of this act? and would it become so, if he communicates that intention to the indorsee, and the latter agrees to assist him? This we think is no fraud, and does not avoid the transaction.”

This doctrine proceeds on the ground that set-off, strictly so called, is not such an equity as can be interposed against the indorsee of commercial paper, whether taken before or after maturity. Whitehead v. Walker, 10 Mees. & W. 696; Ex parte Swan, Law Rep. 6 Eq. 344; Chandler v. Drew, 6 N. H. 469; Gullett v. Hay, 15 Mo. 399; Wilkinson v. Jeffers, 30 Ga. 153; Lewis v. Denton, 18 Iowa, 441; Way v. Lamb, 15 Iowa, 79; Arnot v. Woodburn, 85 Mo. 99. But this subject is variously affected by State statutes, and need not be further pursued.

It is also held that an agreement, with notice that certain goods shall be sold and the proceeds applied to the payment of a note taken by the holder, constitutes an equity. Holmes v. Kidd, 8 Hurl. & N. 891, in Ex. Ch. See also Walbridge v. Kibber, 20 Vt. 543; Pecker v. Sawyer, 24 Vt. 459; Robin-

<br>

### --- Page 0491 ---

ACTUAL NOTICE OF DEFENCE.

son v. Lyman, 10 Conn. 30, as to agreements constituting an equity.

It should also be noticed, in accordance with the principal case, Hascall v. Whitmore, that notwithstanding the fact that the plaintiff had notice, when he took the paper, of some good defence available to the defendant in an action by another party, still the plaintiff may recover if between him and the defendant there be any party who would be entitled to recover on the paper against the same defendant. The plaintiff may stand upon the title of such party. Cromwell v. Sac County, 96 U.S. 51; Marion County v. Clark, 94 U.S. 278; Mornyer v. Cooper, 35 Iowa, 257; Simon v. Merritt, 33 Iowa, 587; Peabody v. Rees, 18 Iowa, 571; Boyd v. McCann, 10 Md. 118; Prentice v. Zane, 2 Gratt. 262; Watson v. Flanagan, 14 Texas, 354; Howell v. Crane, 12 La. An. 126; Woodworth v. Huntoon, 40 Ill. 181; Bassett v. Avery, 15 Ohio St. 299; Robinson v. Reynolds, 2 Q.B. 196, 211.

This is no injustice to the defendant, since he is liable to some one; and it is enough for him that the plaintiff can give him a good discharge. Further, as has often been pointed out, if the fact that other persons besides a particular holder had notice of an infirmity in the defendant’s engagement were to operate against them upon purchasing the paper, it might and often would result that the holder’s property would be unmarketable; since there might be no one to buy it who was not affected with notice.

Again, the nature of the paper itself is to be taken into consideration. It does not follow, because a holder purchased with notice of a want of consideration as between the original parties to the paper, that he cannot recover thereon even though he took directly from the original party who could not sue; for the paper may have been signed by the defendant for the accommodation of the vendor, — for the very purpose of enabling him to raise money. It is well settled that a purchaser with notice may recover in such a case. Grant v. Ellicott, principal case, post, and note; where the subject will be further considered.

The facts constituting notice must, further, have been within the cognizance of the plaintiff at the time of his purchase of the paper. His title is not affected by notice subsequently acquired. Indeed, it is no defence to the claim of a bona fide holder for value that when he took the paper he knew of the existence of an agreement between the original parties under which equities have since arisen, if none such existed at the time he became holder. Patten v. Gleason, 106 Mass. 439.

If, however, a person purchase negotiable paper without notice, making but part payment at the time, and, before paying any more, receive notice from the maker or acceptor of a good defence against the transferrer, and a warning not to pay more, he cannot recover from the party giving such notice more than he had already paid. Dresser v. Missouri Const. Co., 93 U.S. 92; Crandell v. Vickery, 45 Barb. 156. See also Garland v. Salem Bank, 9 Mass. 408; Fulton Bank v. Phenix Bank, 1 Hall, 562; White v. Springfield Bank, 8 Sandf. 227.

The fact that a holder takes a negotiable note or bill payable to a certain person or bearer, with knowledge that it was being negotiated by the maker or acceptor to another person than the one named as payee, is not enough, in an action against a surety, to affect the right of action of the holder. Laub v. Rudd, 37 Iowa, 617; Gage v. Sharp, 24 Iowa, 16.

The very important rule laid down in the principal case, Goodman v. Simonds, has finally come to be almost

<br>

### --- Page 0492 ---

440

universally accepted, to wit, that gross negligence (when not disclosing bad faith) is not sufficient to let in equities against an indorser for value. The contrary rule in Gill v. Cubitt, 8 Barn. & C. 466, first overturned in Goodman v. Harvey, 4 Ad. & E. 870, though followed by some of our courts at first, is now received only perhaps in Illinois and in Tennessee. Sturgis v. Metropolitan Bank, 49 Ill. 220, 227; Farlin v. Lovejoy, 29 Ill. 47; Merritt v. Duncan, 7 Heisk. 156. See also the following cases, none of which, however, would probably be accepted at the present day by the courts of the States in which they were decided: Sandford v. Norton, 14 Vt. 228; Hall v. Hale, 8 Conn. 826; Cone v. Baldwin, 12 Pick. 545 (overruled, see Freeman’s National Bank v. Savery, 127 Mass. 75, 79); Boyd v. McIvor, 11 Ala. 822; Nicholson v. Patton, 18 La. 218; Smith’s v. Mechanics’ Bank, 6 La. An. 610; Pringle v. Phillips, 6 Sandf. 157 (overruled, see Magee v. Badger, 34 N. Y. 247; Belmont Bank v. Hoge, 35 N. Y. 65).

As adopting the rule in Goodman v. Harvey, and Goodman v. Simonds, see the following authorities: Jones v. Gordon, Law Rep. 2 App. Cas. 616; Collins v. Gilbert, 94 U. S. 753; Brown v. Spofford, 95 U. S. 474; Bank of Pittsburgh v. Neal, 22 How. 96; Murray v. Lardner, 2 Wall. 110; Farrell v. Lovett, 68 Maine, 326; Seybel v. National Currency Bank, 54 N. Y. 288; Magee v. Badger, 34 N. Y. 247; Belmont Bank v. Hoge, 85 N. Y. 65; Worcester Bank v. Dorchester Bank, 10 Cush. 488; Smith v. Livingston, 111 Mass. 342; Freeman’s National Bank v. Savery, 127 Mass. 75, 79; Crosby v. Grant, 36 N. H. 278; Phelan v. Moss, 67 Penn. St. 59; Morehead v. Gilmore, 77 Penn. St. 118; Hamilton v. Vought, 84 N. J. 187; Lake v. Reed, 29 Iowa, 268; Gage v. Sharp, 24 Iowa, 15; Hamilton v. Marks, 68 Mo. 167, overruling 8. O.

BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

52 Mo. 78; Johnson v. Way, 27 Ohio, 874; Shreeves v. Allen, 79 Ill. 653; Howry v. Eppinger, 34 Mich. 29; Commercial Bank v. First National Bank, 80 Md. 11; Woolfolk v. Bank of America, 10 Bush, 504.

It is not enough that an indorser for value took the paper under circumstances which might tend to excite suspicion. Farrell v. Lovett, 68 Maine, 326; Murray v. Lardner, 2 Wall. 110; Freeman’s Bank v. Savery, 127 Mass. 76, 79; Lane v. Evans, 49 Iowa, 156, and cases supra and infra.

The purchaser of negotiable paper not due is ordinarily under no obligation to make inquiries as to its origin; nor is he required to be on the alert for circumstances which might excite suspicion. Farrell v. Lovett, 68 Maine, 326; Magee v. Badger, 34 N. Y. 247; Belmont Bank v. Hoge, 35 N. Y. 65.

The true question for the jury, it has well been said, is not whether there were suspicious circumstances, but whether the holder took the paper without notice of any infirmity or taint. Smith v. Livingston, 111 Mass. 342, 345, Morton, J. To the same effect, Phelan v. Moss, 67 Penn. St. 59; Morehead v. Gilmore, 77 Penn. St. 118; Hamilton v. Vought, 84 N. J. 187; Lake v. Reed, 29 Iowa, 258; Johnson v. Way, 27 Ohio, 374; Hamilton v. Marks, 16 Am. Law Reg. N. S. 37; s. c. 63 Mo. 167; Collins v. Gilbert, 94 U. S. 758; Brown v. Spofford, 95 U. S. 474; Shreeves v. Allen, 79 Ill. 658; Howry v. Eppinger, 34 Mich. 29; Commercial Bank v. First National Bank, 80 Md. 11.

It is laid down in England, in a final, authoritative exposition of the law, that if value be given for a negotiable bill or note, it is not enough to show that there was carelessness, negligence, or foolishness, in not suspecting that something was wrong in the title or inception of the paper, though there

<br>

### --- Page 0493 ---

ACTUAL NOTICE OF DEFENCE.

were circumstances that might have led another man to such a suspicion. These are matters tending to show that there was dishonesty in not inquiring; but they do not in themselves make a defence to an action upon the paper. To make such a defence, it is necessary to show that the person who gave value for the bill or note, whether the value given be great or small, was affected with notice that there was something wrong about it when he took it. Jones v. Gordon, Law Rep. 2 App. Cas. 614, 628, Blackburn, LL. J.

Lord Blackburn further made the important observation in this case, that it appeared to him unnecessary that the plaintiff should have notice of the nature of the particular wrong. “If,” he continued, “a man knowing that a bill was in the hands of a person who had no right to it, should happen to think that perhaps the man had stolen it, when if he had known the truth, he would have found, not that the man had stolen it, but that he had obtained it by false pretences, that would not make any difference, seeing that he knew there was something wrong about it when he took it. Taking it that way, he takes it at his peril.”

But such evidence of carelessness or blindness, as has been intimated, has a tendency to prove knowledge; and it may with other evidence (but not alone) be good evidence upon the question whether the plaintiff did know that there was something wrong about the paper. If he was honestly blundering and careless, merely, he is entitled to recover. But if the facts and circumstances are such that the jury should come to the conclusion that he was not honestly blundering and careless, but that he must have had a suspicion that something was wrong; and that he refrained from inquiry, not because he was an honest blunderer or a stupid man, but because he thought something was wrong and inquiry would reveal something to prevent his obtaining a valid claim in purchasing the paper, then he could not recover. Jones v. Gordon, supra, Lord Blackburn. See Sturgis v. Metropolitan Bank, 49 III. 220.

That strong circumstantial indications of fraud will cast the burden upon the plaintiff to show value, see also Smith v. Sac County, 11 Wall. 139; Dutchess Co. Ins. Co. v. Hachfield, 73 N. Y. 226.

It is laid down in Missouri, that the circumstances alleged to constitute notice must be of such a strong and pointed character, as necessarily to cast a shade upon the transaction, and to put the holder upon inquiry. Greer v. Yosti, 66 Mo. 307; Hamilton v. Marks, 63 Mo. 167.

The doctrine of Gill v. Cubitt has been asserted in Missouri; but the case was one of actual notice or of wilful blindness, which is the same thing. Buckner v. Jones, 1 Mo. App. 538. Gill v. Cubitt is not law in that State. Hamilton v. Marks, 68 Mo. 167; overruling s. c. 62 Mo. 78.

The result of the authorities upon this subject is that the doctrine of constructive notice, as applied to an indorsee of a bill, note, or check, or to the payee of a bill or check (whose position may be as favorable as that of an indorsee), the doctrine of constructive notice has a special signification, different from that belonging to it in the law of notice generally. Mere knowledge of facts that would lead a prudent man to make inquiry is, by the general law of notice, sufficient to fix upon the party notice of all facts which a reasonable prosecution of the inquiry would disclose. Kennedy v. Greene, 3 Mylne & K. 718; Warren v. Sweet, 81 N. H. 882; Cambridge Bank v. Delano, 48 N. Y. 326; Woodworth

<br>

### --- Page 0494 ---

v. Paige, 5 Ohio St. 70. But this is not true of the position of a holder for value of a bill of exchange, promissory note, or check. Jones v. Gordon, Law Rep. 2 App. Cas. 616; Woolfolk v. Bank of America, 10 Bush, 504; Freeman’s National Bank v. Savery, 127 Mass. 75, 79, and other cases cited in preceding paragraphs.

There must be something amounting to bad faith in the holder, such as a fraudulent turning away from a knowledge of facts; but if there be want of caution only, however great, as distinguished from fraudulent or wilful blindness, the holder is not affected with notice. Gross negligence may, with other facts, however, show mala fides. Jones v. Gordon, Law Rep. 2 App. Cas. 616. And the same may be said of proof of want of consideration, to support the (remote) defendant’s promise. This may, doubtless, be considered, if notice of it be in fact fixed upon the plaintiff, as one of a chain of facts going to establish bad faith in him. But evidence of want of consideration by the remote defendant is of no avail in any case unless the defendant has gone further and established the plaintiff’s knowledge of it. Whittaker v. Edmunds, 1 Moody & R. 366; Mills v. Barber, 1 Mees. & W. 425; Low v. Chifney, 1 Bing. N. C. 267; Smith v. Braine, 16 Q. B. 244, 253; Knight v. Pugh, 4 Watts & S. 445; Fletcher v. Gushee, 82 Maine, 587; Ellicott v. Martin, 6 Md. 509; Ross v. Bedell, 5 Duer, 462. See also Sloan v. Union Banking Co., 67 Penn. St. 470; Heath v. Silverthorn Mining Co., 89 Wis. 146.

The use of the term “due course of business,” which is commonly understood in the sense of taking for value and without notice, still lingers, in the sense, perhaps, of the rule in Gill v. Cubitt. Thus it has been recently held in Iowa, that to instruct the jury that if a negotiable note or bill was endorsed before due and for a valuable consideration, and without notice of a defence, it must be regarded as having been received in the due course of business, would be incorrect. And the rule was thus declared: If the paper was taken out of the due course of business, under circumstances calculated to impart notice of its infirmities, the holder has taken it at his peril, though he took it before due and without notice. Moore v. Moore, 89 Iowa, 461; Iowa College v. Hill, 12 Iowa, 462. This may, however, only amount to a fair interpretation of the term “without notice.” See Jones v. Gordon, Law Rep. 2 App. Cas. 616, 628. Gill v. Cubitt is repudiated in Lake v. Reed, 29 Iowa, 258, in Gage v. Sharp, 24 Iowa, 15, and in Lane v. Evans, 49 Iowa, 156.

§ 3. Constructive Notice. — When the defect or infirmity of title appears on the face of the paper at the time of the transfer, the question whether the party who took it had notice or not, is a question of construction, to be determined by the court. Goodman v. Simonds, ante, p. 415; Fowler v. Brantly, ante, p. 410; Freeman’s National Bank v. Savery, 127 Mass. 76, 79; National Security Bank v. McDonald, Ib. 82; Andrews v. Pond, 138 Peters, 65; Brown v. Taber, 5 Wend. 566.

A partner has no authority, without the assent of his associates, to sign the name of the partnership to a note for the individual debt of himself or of a stranger; and persons who take such a note with knowledge, either from its appearance or otherwise, that it was made for the separate accommodation of one partner or of another person, cannot recover against the other partners, without showing their consent. Thus, where one partner signed a note in his individual name,

<br>

### --- Page 0495 ---

CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE.

and the defendant co-partner’s name was upon the back of the note above that of the payee, in a case arising under a statute which makes the liability of such party defendant conditional upon notice of dishonor, it is apparent upon the note itself that the liability of the partnership was conditional, and therefore that, prima facie at least, the signature was affixed for the accommodation of the individual partner who signed the note. National Bank v. Law, 127 Mass. 72, Gray, C. J., citing Angle v. Northwestern Ins. Co., 92 U. S. 330; West St. Louis Sav. Bank v. Shawnee Bank, 95 U. S. 657; Chazournes v. Edwards, 8 Pick. 5; Sweetser v. French, 2 Cush. 309; Rollins v. Stevens, 81 Maine, 454; Fielden v. Lahens, 2 Abb. N. Y. App. 111; Lamoine v. Bank of North America, 3 Dill. 44. See National Security Bank v. McDonald, 127 Mass. 82.

In like manner, if a note or bill be payable to an executor eo nomine, and so indorsed, the holder has notice that it is assets of the estate of the testator. Payne v. Flournay, 29 Ark. 600.

To indorse “without recourse” is not sufficient to put the indorser upon inquiry. Kelley v. Whitney, 45 Wis. 110; Stevenson v. O’Neal, 71 Ill. 314. Nor is “secured by mortgage.” Kelley v. Whitney, supra; Howry v. Ep-Pinger, 84 Mich. 29. The same is true of an indorsement in the words, “Pay J.&., or order, value in account with J.D.” This does not fix upon the holder, notice of an agreement under which the indorsement was made. Buckley v. Jackson, Law Rep. 3 Ex. 136; Stuart v. Murrow, 8 Moore, P. C. 267. The same is also true of a writing upon the back of a negotiable note to this effect: “I agree that I will not sell or dispose of this note.” Nor does this restrain the further negotiability of the instrument. Leland v. Parriott, 85 Iowa, 454. See ante, p. 187.

The typical case, however, of constructive notice arises where a person acquires the title to a negotiable note, bill, or check, after it has passed its maturity, that is, after the day upon which it has become due and payable. In the ordinary course of things, commercial paper is paid at its maturity; and this fact gives rise to a presumption of fact, that payment in a particular case has been so made. The purchaser after maturity is deemed to have notice on the face of the paper, that the maker, drawer, or acceptor, has paid it, and that it is now in circulation in fraud of such party’s rights.

This might, in principle, be considered as the limit of the presumption, so that other defences could not be let in under it; but, in fact, the courts have treated the presumption as much broader than its apparent foundation, and have uniformly declared that taking commercial paper after its maturity lets in all defences (equities), of every kind, which the defendant could avail himself of in an action by any party prior to the present plaintiff. This, however, is subject to the qualification already considered, that the plaintiff may avail himself of the position of some other party between himself and the defendant who had taken the paper for value, before maturity, without notice of any defence, provided there be such a party. See ante, p. 439.

It should be observed also with regard to business paper (a term used in contradistinction to accommodation paper, business paper being executed by the defendant for value) that the mere fact that the paper was taken by the plaintiff after its maturity is not sufficient to repel the indorsee’s right of action. A plea setting forth such fact alone would be demurrable. The defendant must be able to further allege some substantive defence, such as pay-

<br>

### --- Page 0496 ---

444

ment, want of consideration, fraud, or other fact which, as between him and the person with whom he immediately contracted, would be a good defence in whole or in part to an action by that person upon the instrument.

Whether the same rule should prevail as to accommodation paper, paper made, accepted, drawn, or indorsed by a party for the accommodation of another, the accommodating party not participating in the consideration which supports the paper,—when the action is brought against the party giving the accommodation, is a point of conflict in the authorities. It is held in England that, in this particular, accommodation paper stands upon the same footing with business paper; that to take such paper after maturity has the effect only to let in equities of the ordinary kind; and that this will not permit the defendant accommodating party to set up as a defence the very situation which he voluntarily, and without deception or abuse of confidence practised upon him, assumed, to wit, that he gave his signature for accommodation merely and not for value. Charles v. Marden, 1 Taunt. 224; Sturtevant v. Forde, 4 Man. & G. 101; Caruthers v. West, 12 Q. B. 148; Jewell v. Parr, 13 C. B. 909. See Ex parte Swan, Law Rep. 6 Eq. 344, 358. This is the rule laid down also by Mr. Justice Story. Promissory Notes, § 194.

On the other hand, it is thought to be a reasonable presumption that the defendant loaned his credit for the period of the paper in its natural duration, and hence that he who takes it after maturity, though for value, takes it with notice of an abuse of the defendant’s confidence, and therefore cannot recover. Chester v. Dorr, 41 N. Y. 279; Bower v. Hastings, 36 Penn. St. 285; Kellogg v. Barton, 12 Allen, 527.

A well-defined distinction prevails in fact, and in many respects in law, between accommodation paper and paper which is given by the defendant as business paper on his part, but which turns out as to him to have been founded upon no legal consideration, or upon a consideration which has since failed wholly or in part. In the case of accommodation paper the defendant accommodating party has knowingly and purposely assumed the position of liability without a consideration received by himself; and he cannot, at least before the maturity of the paper, object that the holder has taken him upon his own terms and made purchase of the paper. In the case of mere want or failure of consideration, however, the party alleging such matter did not assume the position with knowledge of the facts, and with a purpose of aiding another to raise money upon the credit of his name. He signed, as he supposed, for value received; and if the holder took the paper with notice, he cannot recover. So that whatever may be the true rule as to purchasing accommodation paper after maturity, in an action against the party who gave the accommodation,—in the case of an action against one who alleges a want or failure of consideration in the ordinary sense, by one who purchased after maturity, the defendant’s plea is good.

It is held that the fact that a check was taken fourteen months after its date affords prima facie evidence that it was then dishonored. Cowing v. Altman, 71 N. Y. 485. The retention of a check by the holder for a considerable time, without presentment, where no defence exists to it, is unusual; and this circumstance is sufficient to put a party taking it upon inquiry. Ib. See Gough v. Staats, 18 Wend. 549; Little v. Phenix Bank, 2 Hill, 425; Down v. Halling, 4 Barn. & C. 830; 2 Daniels, Neg. Instr., § 1633.

<br>

### --- Page 0497 ---

CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE.

But the date of a check (as well as of a bill or note) is only presumptive evidence of the time when it was issued. A check, bill, or note has no inception until delivery; and for all legal purposes it is to be considered as executed upon the day of delivery. Cowing v. Altman, supra; Lansing v. Gaine, 2 Johns. 800. And where the date and time of delivery are not coincident, the time when it in truth has its inception may be shown, as in answer to a plea of infancy or coverture, or to avoid the inference that it was dishonored when received by the holder. Cowing v. Altman, supra; Pasmore v. North, 18 East, 517; Bohm v. Sterling, 7 T. R. 482; Drake v. Rogers, 82 Maine, 644; Story, Notes, §§ 48, 491.

The non-payment of an instalment due on a note is a dishonor of the whole sum, and subjects the note to all the defences which existed against it when the holder took it. Fitchburg Ins. Co. v. Davis, 121 Mass. 121; Vinton v. King, 4 Allen, 662. But omission to give an indorser notice of the non-payment of those instalments does not affect his liability for a later instalment of the non-payment of which he has been duly notified. Fitchburg Ins. Co. v. Davis, supra.

It is not necessary that there should have been a demand and refusal at the maturity of a note or bill to affect one with equities who takes it afterwards. Dryer v. Mercantile Bank, 4 Mo. App. 598.

The mere fact that instalments of interest due on a note or bill are not indorsed as paid does not fix a purchaser of the same with notice of equities; though it may be evidence to fix him with notice in connection with other facts. National Bank v. Kirby, 108 Mass. 497; Boss v. Hewitt, 15 Wis. 260; Kelley v. Whitney, 45 Wis. 110, overruling Hart v. Stickney, 41 Wis. 445; Cromwell v. Sac County, 96 U. S. 51. And this though the paper payable in instalments is to be overdue when the first instalment is overdue and unpaid. Ib.; Vinton v. King, 4 Allen, 662. Indeed, it is plainly intimated in National Bank v. Kirby, supra, that actual knowledge that interest has not been duly paid is not sufficient to fix upon the holder a notice of equities. After observing that want of indorsement of the payment of interest does not apprise the taker of the paper that there has been no payment, and that when paper was (as in the case before the court) taken only as collateral, the fact that overdue interest was not indorsed might have slight influence in putting the purchaser upon inquiry, the court said that a failure to pay interest was not to be viewed in the same light as a failure to pay principal. "Interest is an incident of the debt, and differs from it in many respects. It is not subject to protest and notice to indorsers, or days of grace, according to the law merchant. Interest is not recovered on overdue interest, and the Statute of Limitations does not run against it until the principal is due. The holder of a note, with interest payable annually, loses no rights against the parties to it, whether makers or indorsers, by neglecting to demand it; and he has the election to do so, or wait and collect it all with the principal." See also Brooks v. Mitchell, 9 Mees. & W. 16.

So, too, a promissory note payable on demand cannot be treated as overdue (in the absence of statute) so as to affect an indorser with equities merely because it is indorsed several years after its date, and no interest has been paid upon it for a number of years before such indorsement and transfer. Brooks v. Mitchell, 9 Mees. & W. 16; Boss v. Hewitt, 15 Wis. 260;

<br>

### --- Page 0498 ---

446

National Bank v. Kirby, 108 Mass. 497, 501.

It has recently been laid down that indorsement after maturity by the payee of a note payable to order does not, in favor of one who took the same for value, without notice, before maturity, relate to the time of transfer, so as to cut off equities available against the indorser. Lancaster Bank v. Taylor, 100 Mass. 18; Whistler v. Forster, 14 C. B. xi. 8. 248; Haskell v. Mitchell, 58 Maine, 468; Clark v. Whitaker, 60 N. H. 474; Southard v. Porter, 48 N. H. 379.

The purchaser in such a case is thought to have notice at the outset, by reason of the want of indorsement of a defence to the instrument. But the holder would be entitled, upon making purchase of the paper, to require indorsement at once, by a bill in equity if necessary: Watkins v. Maule, 2 Jac. & W. 237, 248; Brown v. McHugh, 35 Mich. 50; Story, Notes, § 120; Chitty, Bills, 237, 9th Eng. ed.; ante, p. 133; and hence it would seem that he would be entitled thereupon to sue as a bona fide holder, inasmuch as he had purchased for value and without notice. See Anonymous, 1 Campb. 492, note, Lord Ellenborough? Smith v. Pickering, Peake, N. P. 60; Rose v. Sims, 1 Barn. & Ad. 621; Watkins v. Maule, 2 Jac. & W. 242; Whistler v. Forster, 14 C. B. xi. 8. 248, 254; Chitty, Bills, 167, 10th Eng. ed. The purchase must always, in legal intendment, precede the actual transfer; and it is enough, it is apprehended, that the purchase is regular, or, in familiar language, according to the due course of trade. See cases just cited.

The court in Lancaster Bank v. Taylor, supra, conceded that it was good law that where a negotiable note was transferred for value before its dishonor, but not indorsed until afterwards, a previously existing set-off of a distinct demand against the payee was not allowed to prevail. Ranger v. Cary, 1 Met. 369. But such a case was well deemed distinguishable. ‘The set-off of distinct demands is a matter regulated by statute, and not a common-law defence.’ And the fact was also referred to that in Ranger v. Cary the court were careful to limit the application of their judgment, saying there was no question of want or failure of consideration in the case,—no offer to prove payment, but only a set-off. See also Flint v. Flint, 6 Allen, 34, as to set-off in such cases. The case of Lancaster Bank v. Taylor and the others supra are opposed to Baggarly v. Gaither, 2 Jones, Eq. 80; Beard v. Dedolph, 29 Wis. 136.

The cases, however, go no further than to subject the holder of the unindorsed paper to equities of which he has or acquires knowledge, unless it is not indorsed to him until after maturity. If indorsed to him for value before maturity, and no knowledge has then (by the time of the indorsement) reached him, nor anything tantamount to knowledge, he can, probably by all the authorities, recover regardless of defences available against an earlier party. Anonymous, 1 Campb. 492, note; Smith v. Pickering, Peake, 50; Rose v. Sims, 1 Barn. & Ad. 521; Watkins v. Maule, 2 Jac. & W. 287, 243; but if the indorsement was postponed until after the maturity of the paper, then, though the purchaser had paid value and in fact had no notice at any time of the defences, they are, by the weight of authority, available against him. See on both points Whistler v. Forster, 14 C. B. xi. 8. 248.

That, in the absence of equities, indorsement may be made before or at the trial, when the plaintiff had already acquired the equitable title to a negotiable instrument, see Watkins v. Maule, 2 Jac. & W. 237; Flint v. Flint,

<br>

### --- Page 0499 ---

CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE.

7 Allen, 34 (before trial); Brown v. McHugh, 85 Mich. 60. But see Eckford v. Hogan, 44 Miss. 398; and see ante, p. 138. In Watkins v. Maule, supra, the indorsement was made six years after the execution of the paper, and this was held proper.

As to the general question of the situation of one who purchases unindorsed negotiable paper, there has never been any doubt that the holder takes subject to the equities available against the vendor, supposing his indorsement not to have been afterwards obtained. Whistler v. Forster, 14 C. B. n. s. 248; Tucker v. Tucker, 119 Mass. 79; Foss v. Nutting, 14 Gray, 484; Nichols v. Gross, 26 Ohio St. 425; Patterson v. Cave, 61 Mo. 439. Indeed, there could be no question in such a case; for as we have seen in a preceding note, the want of indorsement to the purchaser of a note not payable to order or already indorsed in blank (the only kind of paper now under consideration) leaves the legal title in the vendor, and the purchaser must therefore sue in the name of that party. Ante, p. 183.

A note or bill may be negotiated on the last day of grace within business hours, and the purchaser acquires a good title, unless he has notice of some defence. Farrell v. Lovett, 68 Maine, 826; Crosby v. Grant, 86 N. H. 273. Gross negligence is not sufficient to let in equities against the indorsee. Ib. As to gross negligence, see ante, pp. 440, 442. But it seems that a negotiable note, bill, or check which was indorsed before maturity, but not delivered until after, would be subject to equities in the hands of the holder. Goodwin v. Davenport, 47 Maine, 112.

Notice that a person is engaged in an illegal business is not notice that his note is given in furtherance of that business. Bottomley v. Goldsmith, 36 Mich. 27; Fowler v. Hoffman, 31 Mich. 215; Harbaugh v. Cicott, 33 Mich. 241; Frankenberg v. First National Bank, Ib. 46.

The fact that an indorsee of a note took it with knowledge of the death of the maker is immaterial. Clark v. Thayer, 105 Mass. 216. See Monument Bank v. Globe Works, 101 Mass. 57.

The mere fact that the plaintiff purchased the paper from a broker raises no presumption as to the ownership thereof beyond that which the possession and power of disposition imply. Atlas National Bank v. Savery, 127 Mass. 76.

It is well settled that a general course of dealing may be shown between the parties to a bill or note, as giving character to a particular transaction within its scope, and as affording an inference that a bill or note discounted within it was so discounted with constructive notice of any existing infirmity. First National Bank v. Goodsell, 107 Mass. 149; Merriam v. Granite Bank, 8 Gray, 264.

<br>

### --- Page 0500 ---

448 BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

GRANT AND Cary v. ELLICOTT.  
(7 Wendell, 227. Supreme Court of New York, May, 1831.)

Accommodation paper. Holder with notice. — In an action by an indorsee of a bill of exchange against the acceptor, it is no defence that the bill was accepted for the accommodation of the drawer, and that the indorsee had knowledge of the fact when he took the bill.

THE case is stated in the opinion of the court.

Savage, C.J. The defendant says he ought not to pay the bill, because no consideration passed between him and Graham, and this was known to the plaintiffs; that is, the defendant accepted the bill for the accommodation of the drawer, which the plaintiffs knew. This is no defence; it was so decided in Smith v. Knox, 3 Esp. 46. Lord Eldon there held that, where a bill is given for the accommodation of the drawer or payee, and is sent into the world, it is no answer to an action upon it against the acceptor, that he accepted it for the accommodation of the drawer, and that the fact was known to the holder. In such case, the holder, if he gave a bona fide consideration for it, is entitled to recover, though he had full knowledge of the transaction. In that case, the plaintiff produced no proof, but of handwriting of the parties to the bill.

The case of Charles v. Marsden, 1 Taunt. 224, was very like this case. The action was brought by the indorsee against the acceptor. The defendant pleaded that it was accepted for the accommodation of the drawer, and without any consideration, and that this was known to the plaintiffs when they took the bill, after it was due. Mansfield, C.J., says: "There is no allegation of fraud in this plea, nor any allegation that the plaintiff did not give a valuable consideration for this bill; it must, therefore, be presumed that he did." Lawrence, Justice, says: "In the present case, it is to be

<br>

### --- Page 0501 ---

SMALL v. SMITH, 449

Supposed that the party (drawer) persuades a friend to accept a bill from him, because he cannot lend him money, would there be any objection, if, with the knowledge of the circumstance that this is an accommodation bill, some person should advance money upon it before it was due? Then what is the objection to his furnishing it after it is due? For there is no reason why a bill may not be negotiated after it is due, unless there was an agreement for the purpose of restraining it.

I know of no decision supporting this plea, and it would be extremely prejudicial to commercial paper if it could be supported. The acceptor in a bill is considered in the same light as an indorser of a promissory note; and it is well known that much of the paper discounted in our banks is accommodation paper, and it never has been supposed that the indorser in such case is not liable.

Judgment for plaintiffs on demurrer, with leave to amend, on payment of costs.

SMALL et al. v. SMITH.
(1 Denio, 588. Supreme Court of New York, October, 1845.)

Fraudulent diversion.—One who purchases accommodation paper, with knowledge that the terms and conditions on which the accommodation was given have been violated, is not a bona fide holder as against the party who lent his name for accommodation.

THE case is stated in the opinion of the court.

BEARDSLEY, J. If the evidence given on the trial was true, and that was for the jury to determine, it is perfectly clear that the note was delivered to the plaintiffs in violation of the agreement upon which it had been indorsed by the de-

<br>

### --- Page 0502 ---

450 BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

fendant. The plaintiffs, therefore, were not entitled to recover, unless they received it bona fide and upon a valuable consideration. Both were necessary. It must have been received in good faith, without notice of the arrangement on which the indorsement had been made, and the transfer must have been upon what the law regards as a valuable consideration. These principles admit of no dispute; and although, upon some points of commercial law in close proximity to those I have stated, discordant opinions may be found, Stalker v. McDonald, 6 Hill, 98, Swift v. Tyson, 16 Peters, 1, there is entire harmony as to those I have mentioned.

The judge charged that, if the plaintiffs received the note in payment and satisfaction of a debt due to them from Hulburt, the maker of the note, that was a sufficient consideration for its transfer, and they thereby became purchasers for value. This, as a legal proposition, is not questioned; but the bill of exceptions fails to show any evidence to which this principle could be applied. There was no proof which tended to show that the note had been transferred in extinguishment of the debt of Hulburt. The judge, therefore, in my view of the case, erred in submitting that question to the jury.

But I shall not dwell on this point, for the case may be disposed of on the question of good faith.

It appears by the testimony of Hulburt that he was indebted to the plaintiffs in a sum exceeding the amount of this note, and that Small, one of the plaintiffs, came to Vienna, where Hulburt resided, to secure payment of said debt. Small proposed to Hulburt to give a note at one year with security, and the defendant, who lived in another county, was spoken of for that purpose. Small said he would take the defendant as surety, and it was arranged that, while Small was absent (as he was going West for a few days), Hulburt should go to the defendant’s residence in order to obtain him as such surety. Pursuant to this arrangement, Hulburt went to see the defendant, and told him what he wanted. At first the defend-

<br>

### --- Page 0503 ---

ant refused to indorse; but it was finally agreed between them that he would indorse the note upon condition that one Austin, who then held a note given by the defendant, should deposit the same with a third person, there to remain until the defendant should be discharged from said indorsement. The note in question was accordingly signed by Hulburt and indorsed by the defendant; but it was not to be transferred to Small, or used in any manner, until the one held by Austin had been deposited under said arrangement. Hulburt returned with the note to Vienna, where Austin lived, and told him of the arrangement under which the indorsement had been made. Austin declined to comply with that arrangement; but Hulburt, as he states, left the note in suit on Austin’s table, and did not see it again until Small had returned to Vienna. Hulburt first saw Small after his return at Austin’s office, where, on arriving at the office, according to the testimony of Hulburt, Small said to him: ‘We have fixed that matter, and Mr. Austin has let me have the note.” The witness then inquired of Austin, in Small’s presence, in what manner the note had been turned out, and whether the arrangement of the defendant had been complied with, to which Austin made no answer; but Small said he had prevailed on Mr. Austin to indorse the note, and he had got it. This, according to the witness (Hulburt), was all which passed at that time. Another witness (Paul), who was present, said the remark of Hulburt to Austin was that he supposed he had not turned out the note without complying with the request of Mr. Smith, the defendant, to which Austin made no answer; but Small said he had prevailed on Mr. Austin to indorse the note, and had released Mr. Smith.

It is not material which of these witnesses was correct as to the form of the remarks made at that time. Both come to the same result; for what was said, according to the statement of either witness, was full notice to Small that the indorsement had been procured upon some arrangement or

<br>

### --- Page 0504 ---

452 BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

condition which had not been complied with. Here, then, Small had actual notice that the indorsement was conditional; and, if the note was subsequently transferred to him, he would necessarily take it subject to that condition. When this notice was given, the note was in Small’s hands. He had received it, as he said, of Mr. Austin. But it cannot be pretended he had received it of Austin upon any consideration moving between them. Indeed, the first remark of Small to Hulburt, and all that was said on that occasion, goes to show that whatever might have been done by Austin had been done for Hulburt, and not for himself, and in furtherance of the negotiation which had been commenced between Hulburt and Small. It is not shown that Austin had authority from Hulburt to transfer this note to Small on any terms, although it may be inferred that he was authorized to do so, on complying with the condition upon which the defendant's indorsement had been made. Small did not set up that he had received the note as the property of Austin, and the whole transaction shows he did not. He could not, therefore, upon the facts as disclosed by the witnesses, pretend that he had acquired title to the note in any manner before he was apprised by Hulburt that the indorsement was made on a condition which had not been performed. It is more a matter of inference than of anything like direct proof, that Hulburt at any time assented to the transfer of the note to Small; but, if he did so, after notice to Small of the condition on which the indorsement had been made, it is plain that the plaintiffs ought not to recover, as the condition has never been performed. If the plaintiffs claim as purchasers of the note from Austin, they are met by two objections: first, Small, one of the plaintiffs, was aware that the note belonged to Hulburt, and not to Austin; and, secondly, it is not shown that the plaintiffs paid or advanced anything to Austin, or that any consideration passed between them for the transfer of the note. And as to Hulburt, if he assented to the transfer of

<br>

### --- Page 0505 ---

STODDARD v. KIMBALL. 453

the note to Small, it was after explicit notice that the indorsement was conditional, as is proved by the testimony of both Paul and Hulburt. Had the case been put to the jury upon the point of notice, with suitable explanations, there is no doubt what the verdict should and would have been, unless these witnesses were wholly discredited. I think the case was not so submitted to the jury, and that it should be sent back for a new trial. New trial granted.

CHARLES STODDARD et al. v. JOHN KIMBALL.
(6 Cushing, 469. Supreme Court of Massachusetts, October, 1850.)

Misapplication.—In an action by the indorsee against the indorser, who had indorsed the paper for the maker’s accommodation, the indorser cannot raise the defence that the note was misapplied by the maker, without showing that the plaintiff had knowledge of the misapplication.

Amount of recovery.—If accommodation paper has been taken to secure a pre-existing debt of a less amount than that expressed on the face of the paper, the holder can recover against the accommodation indorser only the amount of the debt, if he (the holder) is not liable to any third person for any surplus.

THE case is stated in the opinion of the court.

SHAW, C.J. This was a suit brought by the plaintiff as indorsee of a promissory note against the defendant as indorser. The defence relied on was, that the defendant indorsed the note, at the request and for the accommodation of the maker, for a special purpose,—that of taking up another note, on which he was indorser, and that it was not so applied, but was negotiated to the plaintiffs as collateral security for a debt due to them. The defendant also contended that the plaintiffs, at the time of taking the note, had notice of the misapplication of the same, as above stated; but this fact

<br>

### --- Page 0506 ---

454 BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

was left to the jury, who found that the plaintiffs had no such notice.

It further appeared that some payments had been made by the maker of the note to the plaintiffs, towards the discharge of the debt, for securing which to the plaintiffs this note was received, and also that the maker being insolvent, the plaintiffs proved this debt against his estate, and received a dividend.

The defendant contended that, if liable at all, he was liable only for the balance of the debt due the plaintiffs, if less than the amount of the note; and the judge who tried the cause so ruled, subject to the opinion of the whole court, and, in case they should be of opinion that the plaintiffs are entitled to recover the whole amount, the verdict is to be altered and amended accordingly.

We think the direction was right. An indorser of an accommodation note, passed by indorsement to a bona fide holder in due course of business, is effectually bound to all the liability to which by law the indorser of a business note is liable. He stipulates to take on himself the qualified obligation of one who indorses and puts in circulation a note taken by himself for value in the course of business.

If, indeed, an accommodation note is obtained from another by fraud, deception, or false practices, or having been obtained for one purpose is fraudulently misapplied to another, and it is negotiated to one, even for value, with full notice of the fraud in obtaining or misusing it, he cannot recover; he is not a bona fide holder; an attempt to recover it would make him a partaker in the fraud; and the same would be true of a business note.

In the present case, it appearing that the note was negotiated to the plaintiffs before it was due for a valuable consideration, and the jury having found that they took it without notice of the misapplication by the maker, it is clear that they have a right to recover, and the only remaining question is

<br>

### --- Page 0507 ---

NOTICE OF ACCOMMODATION. 455

for what amount they may recover. In general, the holder of an indorsed note will be entitled to recover the whole amount of the face of the note, because the presumption of fact, in the absence of counter-proof, is, that he gave the full value for it, or that he took it from some other holder for value, to collect the amount, receive a certain part to his own use, and account to the party from whom he took it for the surplus. Having taken it to secure a pre-existing debt, of a less amount, he is a holder for value in his own right, only to the amount of the debt due him. If, therefore, it appears in proof that the plaintiff is not accountable to any third person for any surplus, then there is no reason why he should recover any more than the balance of the debt, for which he is a bona fide holder for value. Here it appears that the plaintiff received this note of the maker, for whose accommodation the defendant indorsed it. It being obvious that the plaintiff can recover nothing as trustee for the party from whom he received it, he is liable over to nobody for the surplus, and, therefore, can have judgment only for the amount due to himself, for his own use and in his own right, which is so much of the note as may be necessary to satisfy the balance of the debt, for the security of which he received it.

Judgment on the verdict for the plaintiff for the smaller sum.

§ 1. Notice of Accommodation.—The subject of the present note was partly anticipated in the preceding one. Ante, pp. 439, 448, 444. The doctrine of the principal case, Grant v. Ellicott, is fundamental, to wit, that taking accommodation paper with notice that the defendant was the party who gave the accommodation is, when the paper was taken before maturity, no defence. Brown v. Mott, 7 Johns. 861; Montross v. Clark, 2 Sandf. 116; Yeaton v. Bank of Alexandria, 6 Cranch, 49; Violett v. Patton, 6 Cranch, 142; Thompson v. Shepherd, 12 Met. 811; Molson v. Hawley, 1 Blatchf. 409; Winters v. Home Ins. Co., 80 Iowa, 172; Lord v. Ocean Bank, 20 Penn. St. 884; Duncan v. Gilbert, 5 Dutch. 621; Haly v. Lane, 2 Atk. 181; Fentum v. Pocock, 5 Taunt. 192; Kemp v. Balls, 10 Ex. 607. And the same is of course true where the party signs as surety eo nomine. Bank of Rutland v. Buck, 6 Wend. 66. Indeed evidence that the plaintiff took the paper with notice that it was given or indorsed by the defendant for accommodation will not even suffice to require the plaintiff

<br>

### --- Page 0508 ---

456

to show that he paid value for it. Duncan v. Gilbert, supra; Grant v. Ellicott, ante, p. 448; Ross v. Bedell, 5 Duer, 467; Knight v. Pugh, 4 Watts & S. 445; Charles v. Maraden, 1 Taunt. 224.

In no case, however, is the person lending his name for accommodation, or as a surety, liable to the person for whose benefit he thus loans his credit. See Thompson v. Clubley, 1 Mees. & W. 212; Whitwell v. Crehore, 8 La. 540.

We have also seen that the authorities are in conflict as to the effect of taking accommodation paper after maturity; the English authorities holding such fact no defence, while those of New York, Pennsylvania, and Massachusetts hold the contrary. Ante, p. 444. But it is clear by all the authorities, doubtless, that though the plaintiff may have purchased the accommodation paper after its maturity, still if any prior holder could recover against the party who has loaned his credit, the indorsee who purchased after maturity can do so. Thompson v. Shepherd, 12 Met. 811.

§ 2. Fraudulent Diversion.—If the holder purchased the note, bill, or check with notice of a fraudulent diversion of the defendant’s credit from the purpose for which it was given, the plaintiff cannot recover, as was decided in the principal cases, Small v. Smith and Stoddard v. Kimball, and in Chicopee Bank v. Chapin, 8 Met. 40; Wardell v. Howell, 9 Wend. 170; Brown v. Taber, 5 Wend. 606; Key v. Flint, 8 Taunt. 21; Evans v. Kymer, 1 Barn. & Ad. 528; and in many other cases, unless between the plaintiff and the defendant there be one who might have recovered against the same defendant. Farmers’ & C. Bank v. Noxon, 45 N. Y. 762. And evidence of a fraudulent diversion is sufficient to require the plaintiff to show that he took the paper

BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

for value and before maturity. Ib.; Duncan v. Gilbert, 6 Dutcher, 521; Fulton Bank v. Phenix Bank, 1 Hall, 662; Catlin v. Hansen, 1 Duer, 809; Munroe v. Cooper, 5 Pick. 412; Bailey v. Bidwell, 18 Mees. & W. 73; Smith v. Braine, 16 Q. B. 244; Harvey v. Tower, 6 Ex. 656.

Any material diversion of the credit from the intended and understood purpose, made without consent of the defendant, will, it is apprehended, be deemed fraudulent within the rule. The difficulty is as to what constitutes a material diversion. If the defendant’s credit was loaned generally, it seems that the question of diversion cannot arise, unless before the negotiation of the paper the defendant has withdrawn or modified his credit, and the plaintiff has purchased with notice of the fact. Mohawk Bank v. Corey, 1 Hill, 618; Schepp v. Carpenter, 49 Barb. 542; Deems v. Crook, 1 Edm. Sel. Cas. 95.

It is sometimes said that there can be no diversion, such as to defeat the claim of the indorsee against the accommodating party, when the latter is not interested in the use to be made of his credit. Mohawk Bank v. Corey, supra; Fetters v. Muncie Bank, 84 Ind. 261; Purchase v. Mattison, 6 Duer, 87; De Zang v. Fyfe, 1 Bosw. 835. He is interested, for example, when he has given the use of his name for the renewal of a note of the person accommodated, to which note he (the present defendant) had also given the credit of his signature. When the new accommodation paper is used for another purpose than for taking up the old note as agreed, there is a diversion, and an indorsee with notice cannot recover. Mohawk Bank v. Corey, supra.

On the other hand, it is said that where the accommodation party supposed that his credit was to be used to raise money, when in fact it was

<br>

### --- Page 0509 ---

RECOVERY AGAINST ACCOMMODATION PARTY.

used to pay a debt, he is not interested in the change, and is liable to a holder for value. Fetters v. Muncie Bank, supra; Duncan v. Gilbert, 56 Dutch. 521, 525. Indeed, it is said to be well settled that when a note is indorsed for the accommodation of the maker, to be discounted at a particular bank, it is no fraudulent misappropriation of it to have it discounted at another bank, or to use it in payment of a debt “or otherwise” for the credit of the maker. Duncan v. Gilbert, supra; Powell v. Waters, 17 Johns. 177; Bank of Chenango v. Hyde, 4 Cowen, 667; Bank of Rutland v. Buck, 6 Wend. 66; Zellmeyer v. Caffee, 5 Duer, 94.

But it may be doubted if the rule be not too narrow in requiring the party granting the accommodation to have any interest, in this sense, in the paper. If he should choose to say to the party seeking the accommodation that he will lend him his credit upon the sole condition of his using the paper in a particular way, and this condition should afterwards be violated by the party to be benefited, to the knowledge of the plaintiff, it seems contrary to all notions of justice and good law to allow him to recover of the accommodating party, unless somebody before him could do so.

It is clear, however, that if the paper has accomplished substantially the purpose contemplated in the accommodation, that is enough to entitle a holder for value to recover; supposing, of course, if the defendant is a drawer or an indorser, the proper steps have been taken to make his contingent engagement absolute. Duncan v. Gilbert, 5 Dutch. 541; Wardell v. Howell, 9 Wend. 170. And if the agreement as to its use be not one of substance, as where it relates merely to the place of discount, notice of it by a purchaser for value cannot be material. Bank of Rutland v. Buck, 5 Wend. 66; Mohawk Bank v. Corey, supra; Grandin v. Le Roy, 2 Paige, 509; Powell v. Waters, 17 Johns. 176; Bank of Chenango v. Hyde, 4 Cowen, 567.

On the other hand, the diversion is sufficient to defeat the claim of a plaintiff with notice when the defendant has been injured by the misappropriation of his credit. Crook v. Jadis, 5 Barn. & Ad. 909; Uther v. Rich, 10 Ad. & E. 784.

The defence of notice of a fraudulent diversion, it should be added, is available only to the party who gave the accommodation. Wardell v. Hughes, 8 Wend. 418. In this case, the misuse of the paper was set up by the parties themselves who had been guilty of it; but the result would doubtless have been the same had the defence been attempted by any one else than the party whose confidence had been abused. The defendant cannot avail himself of equities not attaching to his own contract.

§ 8. Amount of Recovery against Accommodation Party. — The amount for which the accommodation party is liable depends upon circumstances. If the holder bought the paper for a valuable consideration, without notice of the nature of the defendant’s engagement, he is entitled to recover the entire sum stipulated upon the face of the instrument, though he may have paid much less. He has bought it for what the vendor was willing to take for it, and he is entitled to receive the entire amount.

If, however, the purchase were tainted with usury, instead of being an open, bona fide purchase of the paper at its market value, then, in those States in which usury laws prevail, the purchaser could not recover against the accommodation party. Cameron v. Chappell, 24 Wend. 94.

But though a person has taken accommodation paper for value, without

<br>

### --- Page 0510 ---

usury, and without notice of the fact of accommodation (to take with notice, if the purchaser have no notice of a material diversion, is no defence in toto in any case), it does not follow that he is entitled, necessarily, to recover the whole amount of the paper.
