The presentation to the defendants by a stranger or third party of a check purporting to be payable to their own order, which such third person proposed to negotiate to them for value, was a transaction which should have aroused their suspicion. And the indorsement of the defendants was an assertion to the public of the genuineness of the paper, and tended to divert the attention of the plaintiffs from it.

But even where the duty of inspection rests upon the holder of the paper, and where he may also, as in the foregoing case, have done something (if done innocently and in the usual course of business, as there) which had a tendency to mislead the drawee; still, if the latter has been guilty of actual negligence, aside from the failure to detect the forgery, and that negligence has resulted in prejudice to the holder, the latter will not be required to pay the money back. National Bank v. Bangs, supra.

The right of the drawee to recover the sum paid by him on the ground of a forgery of the signature of the drawer, seems, therefore, to be no exception to the general rule of the right to recover money paid under a mistake; except, perhaps, in this, that the drawee has in ordinary cases to overcome the effect of a positive presumption against him; to wit, his admission of the genuineness of the signature. As to the right in general to recover money paid by mistake to the holder of commercial

<br>

### --- Page 0619 ---

WHAT ACCEPTANCE ADMITS.

Paper, see Merchants’ Bank v. National Eagle Bank, 101 Mass. 281; Boylston Bank v. Richardson, 101 Mass. 287; White v. Continental Bank, 64 N. Y. 316; Bank of Orleans v. Smith, 8 Hill, 560; Kelly v. Solari, 9 Mees. & W. 64.

A qualification to the rule, that the acceptance of a bill is a conclusive admission of the drawer’s signature, has also been made by the court of Louisiana, to the effect that the rule applies only to cases where the holder took the bill after the acceptance. McKleroy v. Southern Bank, 14 La. An. 458. In this case, the holder had taken the bill before the acceptance; and the acceptor who had paid the same was held entitled to recover the money back. The ground of the decision was that the plaintiff had done nothing by which to induce the defendant to purchase the bill. See Bigelow, Estoppel, 405, 2d ed.

The general rule as stated in the principal case, Hortsman v. Henshaw, is that the admission implied by the acceptance of a bill extends only to the drawer’s signature. Welsh v. German Bank, 73 N. Y. 424; Graves v. American Bank, 17 N. Y. 205; Holt v. Ross, 64 N. Y. 472; Canal Bank v. Bank of Albany, 1 Hill, 287; Beeman v. Duck, 11 Mees. & W. 251. And this, too, even though the payee’s name were upon the bill at the time of the acceptance. Canal Bank v. Bank of Albany, supra. Hence the drawee or acceptor, having paid to one who claims under a forged indorsement of the payee, though the person receiving the money be a bona fide holder for value, can recover back the sum paid, if the forgery be promptly notified to such party. And as between the drawer and drawee in such a case, the drawee must, in the absence of special circumstances (such as being misled by the drawer), bear the loss. Welsh v. German Bank, supra; Graves v. American Bank, supra; Morgan v. Bank of New York, 11 N. Y. 404; Hoffman v. Bank of Milwaukee, 12 Wall. 181; Dodge v. National Bank, 20 Ohio St. 234. This rule is subject, however, to any agreement of a reasonable nature which may have been entered into between the drawer and the drawee in respect of forgery. Schoenwald v. Metropolitan Savings Bank, 57 N. Y. 418.

But Hortsman v. Henshaw has established a qualification or rather an extension to this rule; to wit, that where an indorser’s signature is forged by the drawer before the paper passed out of his hands, the acceptor, as against a bona fide holder for value, is estopped to set up the forgery.

A similar question arose in 1847, in Coggill v. American Exchange Bank, 1 Comst. 113. In that case one of the drawers of the bill forged the payee’s name, and then procured it to be discounted; and at maturity the plaintiff, the drawee, paid it. On discovering the forgery, he sued the defendant, a bona fide holder to whom he had paid the bill, to recover the sum paid. The court held that the action could not be maintained, resting their decision on the fact stated, that the payee had no interest in the bill, comparing it to a bill drawn to a fictitious person, such a bill being in effect payable to bearer. Vere v. Lewis, 8 T. R. 182; Minet v. Gibson, 3 T. R. 481; S.C. 1 H. Bl. 569; Collins v. Emmett, 1 H. Bl. 818; Phillips v. Thurn, Law Rep. 1 C. P. 463; Plets v. Johnson, 3 Hill, 112.

The point made in the principal case was also noticed, that, in such case, the drawer is estopped to deny the genuineness of the indorsement; that he is thus liable to the bona fide holder, and that, therefore, the drawee is entitled, on payment, to a credit against the drawer. Taking this test as universal, it would follow that the relation of

<br>

### --- Page 0620 ---

568

the payee (whose indorsement has thus been made by the drawer, without the payee’s authority) to the paper would be wholly immaterial. But according to Coggill v. American Exchange Bank, explaining on this point Canal Bank v. Bank of Albany, 1 Hill, 287, if the payee owned the forged bill, the acceptor who had paid it by mistake would be entitled to a return of the money. In the principal case, Hortsman v. Henshaw, the payee did not own the bill at the time of the indorsement in his name, so far as anything appears in the report, because the paper had not been delivered to him. It was a case of forgery alone, and did not involve any conversion of another’s property. Now in such a case there can be no doubt, either in principle or on authority, of the soundness of the test in Hortsman v. Henshaw. The drawer of a bill may make it payable to whomsoever he will, and until he has delivered the paper to the payee, all right of property in it is in himself; and he may therefore indorse it in the name of such payee, subject of course to liability under the criminal law for forgery. There is no question of title to the paper in such a case, and the acceptor must be bound by his contract. The paper is in proper hands. But the situation would be very different if the payee had become the owner of the bill by a delivery to him. Forgery by the drawer thereafter would include conversion of another man’s property, and no title would pass. It is not to be supposed that the test in Hortsman v. Henshaw was intended to be applicable to such a case. The rule in Young v. Grote, 4 Bing. 253, as interpreted until recently, might possibly justify carrying the test of the principal case to that extreme: the acceptor, being entitled to a credit against the drawer for the consequences of the latter’s negligence, would be entitled to a

BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

credit when misled by a criminal act of the drawer. But Young v. Grote has been limited of late, as will be seen presently.

The cases of Burchfield v. Moore, 3 E. & B. 683, Talbot v. Bank of Rochester, 1 Hill, 295, and a great many others, show that the drawee may deny the genuineness of the indorsement, if the forgery occurred after the bill passed out of the drawer’s hands; and this is the line of distinction drawn in the principal case.

Though it is true in general that the acceptor does not warrant the genuineness of the signature of any indorser, still, if he accept and negotiate the bill with knowledge that there is a forged indorsement upon it, he is estopped to deny the genuineness of such indorsement. Beeman v. Duck, 11 Mees. & W. 251.

The rule of estoppel does not apply where the forgery is in the body of the bill, as in the sum to be paid. With the drawer’s handwriting, as indicated in his signature, the drawee is bound to be familiar, but with nothing else. Bank of Commerce v. Union Bank, 8 Comet. 230; National Park Bank v. Ninth National Bank, 46 N. Y. 77; Worrall v. Gheen, 89 Penn. St. 388; Hall v. Fuller, 6 Barn. & C. 750; Bruce v. Bruce, 5 Taunt. 495. In the first case, the amount of the bill was altered from $105 to $1,005; and the acceptor, having paid the latter sum, was held entitled to recover it from him to whom he had paid it. See also National Park Bank v. Ninth National Bank, 46 N. Y. 77; Worrall v. Gheen, 89 Penn. St. 388; Hall v. Fuller, 5 Barn. & C. 750. Compare Byles, Bills, 323; Ward v. Allen, 2 Met. 53, and Langton v. Lazarus, 5 Mees. & W. 629, in which cases it is held that the fraudulent alteration made before acceptance, is no defence to the acceptor in an action by a bona fide holder. See post, p. 578.

<br>

### --- Page 0621 ---

WHAT ACCEPTANCE ADMITS.

If the drawer facilitated or gave occasion to the forgery, it has been held that he must bear the loss himself. Young v. Grote, 4 Bing. 253. In that case, the bill had been so drawn by leaving a space after the mark “£,” that the amount was changed from £52.2 to £352.2, and the drawer was required to bear the loss, after payment by the drawee. See Byles, Bills, 323; Brown v. Reed, 79 Penn. St. 870; Phelan v. Moss, 67 Penn. St. 59; Garrard v. Haddan, 67 Penn. St. 82; Zimmerman v. Rote, 75 Penn. St. 188; Capital Bank v. Armstrong, 62 Mo. 69; Iron Mountain Bank v. Murdock, 62 Mo. 70; Belknap v. National Bank, 100 Mass. 873; Putnam v. Sullivan, 4 Mass. 45. But the authority of Young v. Grote, so far as it was formerly supposed to enunciate a broad rule of law as to the effect of facilitating (by want of care) the commission of a forgery, has been narrowed, if not shaken, and confined to cases just like it. See Holmes v. Trumper, ante, p. 644; Baxendale v. Bennett, Law Rep. 3 Q. B. Div. 525, Ct. of App.; Greenfield Savings Bank v. Stowell, 123 Mass. 196; post, p. 574. The distinction between altering a completed instrument and filling a blank contrary to the intention of the person who has signed an incomplete instrument must, however, always be kept in mind. The latter subject will be considered later.

The acceptance of a bill drawn by procuration admits the handwriting of the drawer, and also the procuration; but it does not admit the agent’s power to indorse, though the handwriting is the same as that of the drawer, and though the indorsement preceded the acceptance. Robinson v. Yarrow, 7 Taunt. 455; Smith v. Chester, 1 T. R. 654; Garland v. Jacomb, Law Rep. 8 Ex. 216; post, p. 571.

If, however, the drawer is a fictitious person, and the bill is drawn payable to the drawer’s order, the acceptor’s undertaking is that he will pay to the signature of the same person that signed for the drawer; and in such case the indorsee may show, as against the acceptor, that the signatures of the fictitious drawer and of the first indorser are in the same handwriting. Cooper v. Meyer, 10 Barn. & C. 468.

A party who admits that a signature is in his own handwriting, and thereby induces another to take the paper, is precluded thereafter from denying the genuineness of the signature. Leach v. Buchanan, 4 Esp. 226; Woodruff v. Munroe, 33 Md. 146; Melvin v. Hodges, 71 Ill. 422; Mather v. Maidstone, 18 Com. B. 278; Morris v. Bethell, Law R. 5 C. P. 47.

The last case cited was an action by the holder against the defendant as the acceptor of a bill. The case was this: In August, 1867, the defendant paid a bill of exchange, of which the plaintiff was the holder, upon which the defendant’s name had been written as acceptor without his authority. In an action against him upon another bill, similarly accepted, the jury found that the acceptance was not the defendant’s signature, or written with his authority; that the forged signature was not adopted by the defendant; that the defendant did not know that the plaintiff was the holder of the former bill; and that he did not lead the plaintiff to believe that the acceptance written on the bill sued upon was his. The court unanimously held that the fact that the defendant had paid the bill in August, above mentioned, did not estop him from denying that the bill declared on was accepted by him or with his authority; that the circumstances were properly submitted to the jury; and that the judge was not bound to tell them that, as matter of law, the plaintiff was entitled to recover.

<br>

### --- Page 0622 ---

570

cover. The court distinguished the case from Barber v. Gingell, 3 Esp. 60. Bovill, C. J., said: "If it were made to appear that there had been a regular course of mercantile business, in which bills have been accepted by a clerk or agent whose signature has been acted upon as the signature of the principal, there would be evidence, and almost conclusive evidence, against the latter, that the acceptance was written by his authority. That was the case of Barber v. Gingell. It would have been idle to contend there that the defendant was not responsible for the signature." See also Beeman v. Duck, 11 Mees. & W. 251; Stout v. Benoist, 39 Mo. 277.

On the other hand, it is held that where a person receives from another, a bona fide holder for value, paper purporting to be his (the receiver’s) own paper (i.e., executed by him) as genuine, and passes the amount to the credit of the late holder, the party receiving cannot recover the amount so credited by alleging that the paper was forged; clearly after it is too late for the person who was thus credited to make himself good again. Bank of United States v. Bank of Georgia, 10 Wheat. 838. See Oddie v. National Bank, 45 N. Y. 735, 742; Gloucester Bank v. Salem Bank, 17 Mass. 33; Smith v. Mercer, 6 Taunt. 76.

So, if the acceptor puts the bill into circulation, he cannot be allowed to allege that he paid it before maturity. Hinton v. Bank of Columbus, 9 Port. Ala. 468.

Acceptance for the honor of an indorser does not admit the genuineness of the indorser’s signature. Wilkinson v. Johnson, 8 Barn. & C. 428. And the reasoning of Abbott, C. J., in this case is perhaps broad enough to warrant the rule laid down in 1 Parsons, Notes and Bills, 323, that acceptance for honor does not admit the genuineness of the signature of any party for whose honor the acceptance is given. But such acceptance has been held to admit the signature of the drawer, for whose honor it was given. Goddard v. Merchants’ Bank, 4 Comst. 147.

So, also, it is held that one who accepts for the honor of the drawer is, like the drawer himself, estopped from denying that the bill is a valid bill; and consequently it is not competent to him to set up as a defence to an action against him by an indorsee, that the payee is a fictitious person, and that he was ignorant of that fact at the time he accepted the bill. Phillips v. Thurn, 18 Com. B. n. 8. 694; s.c. Law Rep. 1 C. P. 463.

In this case, the bill was payable to a fictitious payee, and therefore held equivalent to a bill payable to bearer. Erle, C. J., said: "I take it to be clear that if the defendant had not intervened, and the action had been brought by the holder of the bill against the drawer, the drawer would have been by law compelled to admit that the bill was a valid bill, payable to bearer. . . . It seems to me there is good reason for saying that that which the drawer would be estopped from denying, the acceptor for honor should also be estopped from denying. I think that he is equally bound to admit that the bill is a valid bill." 18 Com. B. 701.

The party paying forged paper is bound to use diligence in making inspection of the paper so as to discover the forgery, and in giving notice thereof. If by his failure to do so the party receiving the payment is prejudiced, the party paying will not be able to recover the money. Allen v. Fourth National Bank, 59 N. Y. 12.

The doctrine of Price v. Neal, 8 Burr. 1354, was in this case said to apply in strictness only where the paper was actually presented to the

<br>

### --- Page 0623 ---

FRAUDULENT FILLING OF BLANKS.

party, and accepted or paid on or after such presentation. Where, it was said, the payment is made without presentment, and accepted subject to future examination of the paper, the case is not within Price v. Neal, and the ordinary rules as to money paid by mistake should govern. See Goddard v. Merchants’ Bank, 4 Comst. 149; National Bank v. National Banking Assoc., 65 N. Y. 211.

Besides the warranty of genuineness implied in the acceptance of a bill, there is also an implied warranty of the present capacity of the drawer to draw the same and of the payee to indorse it. So, too, the making a note is a warranty of the payee’s capacity to indorse. Drayton v. Dale, 2 Barn. & C. 293; Smith v. Marsack, 6 Com. B. 486; Hallifax v. Lyle, 8 Ex. 446; Pitt v. Chappelow, 8 Mees. & W. 616; Braithwaite v. Gardiner, 8 Q. B. 473; Sanderson v. Collman, 4 Man. & G. 209; Nightingale v. Withington, 15 Mass. 272; Burrell v. Smith, 7 Pick. 291; Peaslee v. Robbins, 8 Met. 164; Burke v. Allen, 29 N. H. 106; Hardy v. Waters, 88 Maine, 450. See further ante, p. 167.

There is, however, no warranty of the right of the drawer of a bill to indorse it. The business of attorneys, for example, is not such as to render it necessary or usual to draw or indorse bills of exchange; and hence a member of a firm of attorneys has not, as such, authority to bind his firm either by drawing or by indorsing bills. Garland v. Jacomb, Law Rep. 8 Ex. 216, Ex. Ch.; Hedley v. Bainbridge, 8 Q. B. 816. The non-assenting partners therefore should not be liable in such a case. The case of National Bank v. Morgan, 73 N. Y. 598, contra, may be doubted.

Proceeding upon this proposition, it has been decided by the English Exchequer Chamber that an acceptor is not estopped to set up, as against a bona fide indorsee for value, in an action against

571

him upon the bill, that the draft was drawn and indorsed by one member of a firm of attorneys in the firm name, without authority of the other partners. Garland v. Jacomb, supra. Though acceptance admits the capacity of the drawer to draw the bill, it does not, of itself, admit the capacity of the same person to indorse it. Garland v. Jacomb. If, however, it were accepted with intent that such person should indorse it, the case would probably be otherwise. Ib.; Beeman v. Duck, 11 Mees. & W. 261.

§ 2. Fraudulent Filling of Blanks.—As has already been intimated, the effect of the fraudulent filling of blanks depends upon the question whether the act was done to an incomplete or to a complete negotiable instrument. In the former case, the act is fraud only; in the latter, it commonly amounts to a material alteration and hence to forgery. Different rules of law prevail as to the rights of a bona fide holder for value in the two cases, and we shall therefore consider them separately. And, first, of the effect of fraudulently filling a blank in an incomplete negotiable bill, note, or check.

The general rule of law upon this subject is that one who gives his signature as maker, acceptor, drawer, or indorser, and intrusts the same to another to fill up the contract and make him party to a negotiable instrument, thereby confers upon the person so intrusted, as to all purchasers for value without notice, the right to complete the contract at pleasure, both as to names, terms, and amount; so far as is consistent with the instrument as written out at the time it is delivered to the person intrusted with the signature. Angle v. Northwestern Ins. Co., 92 U. S. 830; Whitmore v. Nickerson, 125 Mass. 496; Greenfield Sav. Bank v. Stowell, 128 Mass. 196, 199, 203; Fearing v. Clark, 16 Gray, 74; Putnam v. Sullivan, 4 Mass. 45, 64;

<br>

### --- Page 0624 ---

Blakey v. Johnson, 13 Bush, 197; Sitting v. Birkestack, 88 Md. 158; Dunham v. Clogg, 30 Md. 284; Elliott v. Chesnut, 30 Md. 562; Michigan Bank v. Eldred, 9 Wall. 554; Ledwick v. McKim, 653 N. Y. 307; Van Duzer v. Howe, 21 N. Y. 531; Burson v. Huntington, 21 Mich. 415; Van Ettav. Evenson, 28 Wis. 33; Farmers’ & M. Bank v. Horsey, 2 Houst. 385; Townsend v. France, 2 Houst. 441; Armstrong v. Harshman, 61 Ind. 62; Gothrupt v. Williamson, 61 Ind. 599; Yocum v. Smith, 68 Il. 821. See Snyder v. Van Doren, 46 Wis. 602.

It is accordingly held that one who indorses a note or bill in blank, and intrusts the paper to his clerk with authority to deliver it to another upon the latter’s signing it with the name of a firm to which he belongs, is bound by the act of the clerk if he should deliver the paper, in excess of his authority, to the person referred to before it had been signed at all. Whitmore v. Nickerson, 125 Mass. 496; Greenfield Sav. Bank v. Stowell, 123 Mass. 196, 199, 208; Fearing v. Clark, 16 Gray, 74; Putnam v. Sullivan, 4 Mass. 45, 54.

The delivery of the paper to such person carried with the act an implied authority to fill up the blank, like any other blank necessary to perfect the instrument, before issuing it; and the instrument negotiated by the person to whom it was thus delivered, signed by that person, to one who took it for value and without notice, bound the indorser. Whitmore v. Nickerson, supra; Angle v. Northwestern Ins. Co., 92 U. S. 430.

Leaving the name of the payee blank by one who has signed and delivered the note or bill to another authorizes the party receiving the same to fill in the name of anyone as payee. Armstrong v. Harshman, 61 Ind. 52; Gothrupt v. Williamson, 61 Ind. 599; Farmers’ & M. Bank v. Horsey, 2 Houst. 886; Townsend v. France, 2 Houst. 441.

BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

The rule, however, that the bona fide holder of an incomplete instrument, negotiable but for some lack capable of being supplied without forgery, has an implied authority to supply the omission, applies only where the party affected has, by his own act or by the act of another, authorized, confided in, or invested with apparent authority a person to put the instrument in circulation. Ledwick v. McKim, 53 N. Y. 807; Van Duzer v. Howe, 21 N. Y. 631.

While, too, a blank space may be filled up, contrary to the agreement of the parties, no right exists to make any material alteration of the written words. Angle v. Northwestern Life Ins. Co., 92 U.S. 330; Blakey v. Johnson, 13 Bush, 197; Franklin Ins. Co. v. Courtney, 60 Ind. 134; Coburn v. Webb, 56 Ind. 96. It matters not that the contract has been made more favorable to the defendant. Coburn v. Webb, supra.

The implied authority in these cases is deemed to be revoked by the death of the party sought to be charged, if it has not already been acted upon; so that one taking paper indorsed by him, and intrusted by him to another for use while yet in an imperfect state, may not recover upon it against the estate of the deceased. Michigan Ins. Co. v. Leavenworth, 30 Vt. 11. See Ledwick v. McKim, 58 N. Y. 307, 315.

A person who takes an acceptance, with the drawer’s name in blank, has no right to say that he may assume that that acceptance entitles any holder to insert any name as drawer which he thinks fit. Hogarth v. Latham, Law Rep. 8 Q. B. Div. 643, 652, Brett, L.J., Court of App. Any one who takes such an instrument, knowing that when it was accepted the bill had no drawer’s name signed to it, takes it at his peril; and where the acceptance is made by a partner of a firm, in the firm name, in a matter not connected with the partnership, the holder must

<br>

### --- Page 0625 ---

ALTERATION.

show that, in fact, the partner who did not write the acceptance authorized the signing of the firm name, with intent that it should be filled up by any person who took it. Ib., Bramwell, L. J. And if an unauthorized name be filled in as drawer, by the holder, the latter cannot recover even against the accepting partner. Ib. See Chemung Canal Bank v. Bradner, 44 N. Y. 680.

That there must be a delivery of the paper, either actually or constructively, is clear. First National Bank v. Strang, 72 Ill. 659; Burson v. Huntington, 21 Mich. 415; Baxendale v. Bennett, Law Rep. 3 Q. B. Div. 525, Court of Appeal.

A constructive delivery is made when the paper is intrusted to another, and put in circulation by the custodian contrary to the orders of the defendant. Burson v. Huntington, supra. So, also, when the defendant has been guilty of negligence, in failing to ascertain the nature of the contract which he has signed in favor of another. Chapman v. Rose, 66 N. Y. 187; Whitney v. Snyder, 2 Lans. 477; Foster v. Mackinnon.

Where a negotiable bill or note is stolen from the acceptor or maker, such party is not liable thereon to a bona fide indorsee for value, even though the acceptor or maker somewhat facilitated the theft by putting the paper in an unlocked drawer in chambers to which his clerk, laundress, and other persons had access. Baxendale v. Bennett, Law Rep. 3 Q. B. Div. 525, Ct. of App. It was said in this case that Young v. Grote, 4 Bing. 253, and Ingham v. Primrose, 7 C. B. N. S. 82, went a long way to justify the action in such a case; but in those cases, and in all others where the acceptor or maker has been held liable, he has voluntarily parted with the instrument; it had not been taken by the commission of a crime, before any delivery.

It was further laid down in Baxendale v. Bennett, that the doctrine of estoppel should never be applied without a necessity for it. It should not be applied except in cases where the person against whom it is used has so conducted himself, either in what he has said or done, or failed to say or do, that he would, unless estopped, be saying something contrary to his former conduct in what he had said or done, or failed to say or do. This principle does not apply to a case of theft, even where the party stolen from was negligent. The loss in such a case is not the natural and, judging in advance, probable cause of the negligence. Bramwell, L. J. See Bank of Ireland v. Evans, 5 H. L. Cas. 389.

To the same effect as Baxendale v. Bennett is Burson v. Huntington, 21 Mich. 415. This last case was misunderstood in Chipman v. Tucker, 88 Wis. 43, and its authority strained to a point not covered by it. In Chipman v. Tucker, it was held that delivery of a negotiable instrument by a custodian, contrary to the orders of the party who gave him custody of the paper, was of no avail, even in the hands of a bona fide indorsee for value, — a position at variance with the authorities. See also Roberts v. McGrath, 38 Wis. 62; Roberts v. Wood, 88 Wis. 60, to the same effect as Chipman v. Tucker. The cases of Walker v. Ebert, 29 Wis. 194, and Kellogg v. Steiner, 29 Wis. 626, are clearly distinguishable. They belong to the class of cases represented by Foster v. Mackinnon, ante, p. 554, where there has been no conscious execution of the contract in question, — a subject to be considered later.

§ 8. Alteration. — This carries us to the doctrine of the principal case, Holmes v. Trumper. The reasoning of this case is supported by Baxendale v. Bennett, supra, and has lately been expressly approved in the similar case

<br>

### --- Page 0626 ---

57¢

of Greenfield Savings Bank v. Stowell, 128 Mass. 196. It was there held upon an exhaustive examination of the authorities that an alteration of a negotiable note by one of several makers, who raised the amount by inserting words and figures in blank spaces left in the printed form upon which it was written, rendered the note absolutely void as to the non-consenting makers, even in the hands of a bona fide holder for value.

The case of Young v. Grote, 4 Bing. 253, 8. c. 12 Moore, 484, so often cited upon questions of this kind, was much considered in Greenfield Savings Bank v. Stowell, and shown to have been greatly narrowed in England. Swan v. North British Aust., 2 Hurl. & C. 176, 189, 190; Halifax Union v. Wheelwright Law Rep. 10 Ex. 183, 192; Robarts v. Tucker, 16 Q. B. 560; 8. c. 15 Jur. 987; 20 L. J. Q. B. 270; Bank of Ireland v. Evans Charities, 6 H. L. Cas. 389, 410, 413; Orr v. Union Bank, 1 Macq. 513, 523; British Linen Co. v. Caledonian Ins. Co., 4 Macq. 107, 114; Ex parte Swan, 7 C. B. n. 8. 400, 431, 433. And see Arnold v. Cheque Bank, Law Rep. 1 C. P. Div. 678, 587, 588.

Most of the American authorities were considered to be in accordance with this doctrine (the doctrine of Greenfield Savings Bank v. Stowell). The following were referred to: Fay v. Smith, 1 Allen, 477; Wade v. Withington, 1 Allen, 5661; Draper v. Wood, 112 Mass. 315; Ives v. Farmers’ Bank, 2 Allen, 286; Sewall v. Boston Water Power Co., 4 Allen, 277, 282; Belknap v. National Bank, 100 Mass. 876, 8381; Wood v. Steele, 6 Wall. 80; Agawam Bank v. Sears, 4 Gray, 95; Waterman v. Vose, 43 Maine, 504; Goodman v. Eastman, 4 N. H. 465; Redlich v. Doll, 54 N. Y. 234 (distinguished, but implying the same view); Woodworth v. Bank of America, 19 Johns. 391; Nazro v. Fuller, 24 Wend.

BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

374; McGrath v. Clark, 56 N. Y. 34 (a case like Holmes v. Trumper). See also Kitchin v. Place, 41 Barb. 465.

The Pennsylvania decisions were conceded to be opposed to the doctrine in question. These decisions permit a recovery by the bona fide holder for value to the amount of the instrument as originally executed, when the sum has been raised in such a way as not “to excite the suspicions of a man in ordinary business.” Worrall v. Gheen, 89 Penn. St. 888; Garrard v. Haddan, 67 Penn. St. 82; Phelan v. Moss, 67 Penn. St. 59. See also Zimmerman v. Rote, 75 Penn. St. 188; Brown v. Reed, 79 Penn. St. 370. The case of Worrall v. Gheen, supra, was doubted in Neff v. Horner, 63 Penn. St. 827, as being contrary to Wade v. Withington, 1 Allen, 561, Draper v. Wood, 112 Mass. 315, and Citizens’ Bank v. Richmond, 121 Mass. 110; but it was afterwards followed in the cases above cited from 67 Penn. St.

The law of Iowa must also be considered as at variance with Holmes v. Trumper. It is there held that filling a blank as to the rate of interest is not an alteration. Rainbolt v. Eddy, 84 Iowa, 440; McCramer v. Thompson, 21 Iowa, 249; McDonald v. Muscatine Bank, 27 Iowa, 819. So, too, the courts of Missouri and California speak of alterations made possible by the negligence of the maker of a note in leaving blank spaces, Capital Bank v. Armstrong, 62 Mo. 59; Iron Mountain Bank v. Murdock, 62 Mo. 70; Redington v. Woods, 45 Cal. 406; and see Schryver v. Hawkes, 22 Ohio St. 808; Abbott v. Rose, 62 Maine, 194; a ground of liability not regarded with favor even in Pennsylvania, Worrall v. Gheen, supra.

The doctrine of Young v. Grote, if to be accepted upon its facts, as it probably is, is distinguishable from cases like Holmes v. Trumper. The

<br>

### --- Page 0627 ---

ALTERATION.

Maker of a note holds no such relation to an indorsee as a customer does to his banker. The relation of banker and customer is created by their own contract — a contract binding the banker to honor the customer's drafts, Greenfield Savings Bank v. Stowell, supra, Gray, C. J.; Marzetti v. Williams, 1 Barn. & Ad. 415; Bank of the Republic v. Millard, 10 Wall. 162; Carr v. National Security Bank, 107 Mass. 47; and, if the negligence of the customer afforded opportunity to a clerk or other person in his employ to add to the terms of a draft and thereby mislead the banker, the customer might well be held liable to the banker.

“But,” said the learned Chief Justice in Greenfield Savings Bank v. Stowell, “even as between customer and banker, the former has not been held liable for an unauthorized alteration or addition by a stranger.” The alteration in that case was made by a party.

In some States, also, it is held that a contract or stipulation attached to a note, which may be separated from the same and still leave the note perfect, may be so separated; and if the paper then pass into the hands of a bona fide holder for value, he can recover upon it. Cornell v. Nebeker, 58 Ind. 425; Nebeker v. Cutsinger, 48 Ind. 436; Steele v. Moore, 54 Ind. 52; Zimmerman v. Rote, 75 Penn. St. 188; Brown v. Reed, 79 Penn. St. 370. But the contrary rule is held in many other States. Gerrish v. Glines, 66 N. H. 9; Johnson v. Heagan, 23 Maine, 829; Benedict v. Cowden, 49 N. Y. 396; Bank of America v. Woodworth, 18 Johns. 815; S. C. 19 Johns. 391; Jones v. Fales, 4 Mass. 245; Coolidge v. Ingler, 13 Mass. 26; Springfield Bank v. Merrick, 14 Mass. 822; Shaw v. First Methodist Society, 8 Met. 223; Barnard v. Cushing, 4 Met. 230; Makepeace v. Harvard College, 10 Pick. 298; Heywood v. Perrin, 10 Pick. 228;

Fletcher v. Blodgett, 16 Vt. 26; Henry v. Coleman, 5 Vt. 403. The cases of Sanders v. Bacon, 8 Johns. 485, and Tappan v. Ely, 16 Wend. 862, were distinguished and doubted in Benedict v. Cowden, supra. See also Franklin Savings Ins. v. Reed, 126 Mass. 365; Heywood v. Perrin, 10 Pick. 228; Benthall v. Hildreth, 2 Gray, 288.

There is a clear difference between cases in which a writing disconnected from the complete engagement of the maker, acceptor, or other party, is intended to be a substantive part and modification of the terms of the main contract, and cases in which the additional writing is a mere personal memorandum or ear-mark, put there, for instance, for the purpose of identification. If the writing be of the latter nature, it will not affect the contract, at least in the hands of a bona fide holder for value, to remove it. Benedict v. Cowden, supra; Brill v. Crick, 1 Mees. & W. 282. The effect of the writing in such cases is a question of intention, to be determined upon all the circumstances under which it was put there. Benedict v. Cowden, supra.

Of course, as between immediate parties, a contemporaneously executed written agreement affecting the terms of a note, bill, or check, and referring to such instrument, may be received in evidence in modification of the situation of the parties. Goodwin v. Nickerson, 51 Cal. 166.

In Brown v. Reed, supra, a written contract had been so cunningly framed that by dividing the paper perpendicularly one half would be left a complete promissory note; and it was held that so to divide it would be a forgery, so as to destroy it for all purposes, unless the line of division was so plain that the signer was negligent in not seeing it. See the observation on this case in Greenfield Savings Bank v. Stowell, 123 Mass. 196, 207.

<br>

### --- Page 0628 ---

576

An alteration of material statements made upon the back of a bill or note at the time of its execution will have the effect to annul it. Bay v. Shrader, 50 Miss. 326. See Hartley v. Wilkinson, 4 Smedes & M. 25; Brooke v. Smith, Moor, 679; Steadman v. Purchase, 6 T. R. 737; Burgh v. Preston, 8 T. R. 485; Jones v. Fales, 4 Mass. 245; Springfield Bank v. Merrick, 14 Mass. 322; Fletcher v. Blodgett, 16 Vt. 26.

If, however, the blank has been wrongfully filled by one into whose custody the paper has been given, with power to fill the blank or not in a certain contingency, there is no forgery, though the paper was delivered as complete with the blank; and the person whose confidence has thus been violated is bound as to bona fide holders for value. See Wade v. Withington, 1 Allen, 561; Ives v. Farmers’ Bank, 2 Allen, 286, 241; Sewall v. Boston Water Power Co., 4 Allen, 277, 282; Belknap v. National Bank, 100 Mass. 876, 881; Greenfield Sav. Bank v. Stowell, 128 Mass. 196, 203; Worrall v. Gheen, 39 Penn. St. 888. See ante, p. 512. But it is otherwise of an actual alteration by the person having the custody of the paper, Belknap v. National Bank, 100 Mass. 876; unless the circumstances indicate a presumable authority in the custodian to make the alteration, tna Ins. Co. v. Winchester, 43 Conn. 391. But the mere fact that the alteration has been made by a person who is agent of the defendant will not prevent the latter from alleging the wrongful act against any person. Ib. Agency confers no authority to commit forgery. Ib.; Brooks v. Allen, 62 Ind. 401.

No agency exists between several cosigners to a note, bill, or check upon their mere relation of coparties; and hence an alteration by one comaker, though before delivery of the note, if after the other maker has signed, is

BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

fatal in an action against the latter. Wood v. Steele, 6 Wall. 80; Greenfield Sav. Bank v. Stowell, supra. And this, too, though the act was not fraudulent. Wood v. Draper, 112 Mass. 315.

The general rule of law as to alterations, it may now be observed, is that if a written instrument be altered in a material particular, whether by the person claiming under it or by any other party to it, without the consent of the defendant, there can be no recovery against him either upon the instrument in its present or in its true original form. Nor indeed can a vendor of property, who has fraudulently or materially altered a promissory note given him for the price thereof, resort to the original consideration, whether the note was received in payment or not. Smith v. Mace, 44 N. H. 553; Martindale v. Follett, 1 N. H. 95; Wheelock v. Freeman, 18 Pick. 164; Arrison v. Hamstead, 2 Barr, 101; White v. Huss, 82 Ala. 430; Whittier v. Frye, 10 Mo. 848. And if the paper be complete, the effect of a material alteration, without consent, is to destroy it even in the hands of a bona fide indorsee for value. Holmes v. Trumper, ante, p. 644; Greenfield Sav. Bank v. Stowell, 123 Mass. 196; Citizens’ Bank v. Richmond, 121 Mass. 110; Wade v. Withington, 1 Allen, 561; Bank of Newark v. Crawford, 2 Houst. 282; Sudler v. Collins, 2 Houst. 538; Woolfolk v. Bank of America, 10 Bush, 604, 517; Morehead v. Parkersburg Bank, 5 W. Va. 74; Burchfield v. Moore, 8 El. & B. 683.

Nor is the effect of a material alteration changed by evidence that it was made in good faith, if it was purposely made and adhered to. Aldrich v. Smith, 87 Mich. 468; Draper v. Ward, 112 Mass. 815; Fay v. Smith, 1 Allen, 477; Evans v. Foreman, 60 Mo. 449. But it is held that an alteration made in good faith by the payee

<br>

### --- Page 0629 ---

ALTERATION.

of a note, though it will destroy his right of action upon the note, will not prevent his suing upon the consideration for which it was given. This result occurs only when the alteration was fraudulent. Booth v. Powers, 56 N.Y. 22, 31; Meyer v. Huncke, 55 N.Y. 412; Clute v. Small, 17 Wend. 238; Clough v. Seay, 49 Iowa, 111; Krause v. Meyer, 32 Iowa, 569; Vogle v. Ripper, 34 Ill. 100; Matteson v. Ellsworth, 83 Wis. 488. But to have this right of action the payee must be able to produce and surrender the note. Booth v. Powers, supra. It is doubtful if an indorsee in such a case could recover upon the consideration given for the paper. He could not recover against him from whom he has received it, because when he altered the written instrument he made it useless to that person as well as to himself; and he could not recover against an earlier party, because his only privity with such party arises upon the paper.

It matters not, in suing upon the paper, even that the alteration was favorable to the defendant, Humphries v. Gwillow, 13 N.H. 885, 887; the test being simply whether the alteration makes the instrument prima facie a new note or bill, ib.; Chism v. Toomer, 27 Ark. 108. See also infra of adding new parties to the paper.

But, if the alteration was made by mistake and afterwards corrected, it has no effect. Horst v. Wagner, 43 Iowa, 873; Krause v. Meyer, 32 Iowa, 566. So, too, of an alteration made necessary by an innocent mistake; the alteration consisting merely in correcting the mistake. Horst v. Wagner, supra.

And it is held in Pennsylvania that a recovery can be had upon a promissory note as originally executed, notwithstanding an alteration, if this were not fraudulent and the rights of others have not intervened. Kountz v. Kennedy, 63 Penn. St. 187. But this rule would not be generally accepted. Comp. ante, p. 574. See also Citizens’ Bank v. Richmond, 121 Mass. 110; post, p. 578.

When, too, the alteration or addition to a note or bill merely states the legal effect of the instrument, it is immaterial. Thus, where no time of payment is specified in a promissory note, the note is in law payable on demand; and in such a case the addition of the words “on demand,” though without the assent of the maker, does not affect the holder’s right of action, Aldous v. Cornwell, Law Rep. 8 Q.B. 578, overruling the contrary statement in the 2d resolution of Pigot’s Case, 11 Coke, 27a; Goodenow v. Curtis, 88 Mich. 605; Curtis v. Goodenow, 24 Mich. 18. In the last two cases, an instrument promising to pay money, but upon an expressed condition, was changed by the insertion of the words “or bearer” after the payee’s name; and this was held of no effect, as the contract could not thereby be made negotiable.

So, too, it is held that when the alteration expresses only what is implied by law at the date of the contract, it is immaterial, though the law be changed before the paper is sued upon, but after its maturity. Bridges v. Winters, 42 Miss. 135. See Duerson v. Alsop, 27 Gratt. 229. Secus, if the alteration was fraudulent. Bridges v. Winters.

It has also been laid down that if an alteration conforms to the true intention of the parties to the paper, and is honestly made to correct a mistake, and with no intent of procuring an advantage, it will not vitiate the instrument though material. The law will in such a case dispense with proof of an actual assent of the maker, acceptor, or drawer, because the party’s assent is to be implied. McRaven v. Crisler, 68 Miss. 642; Kershaw v.

87

<br>

### --- Page 0630 ---

Cox, 3 Esp. 246; Knill v. Williams, 10 East, 431; Brutt v. Picard, Ryl. & M. 37; Clute v. Small, 17 Wend. 238; Boyd v. Brotherson, 10 Wend. 93; Hervey v. Harvey, 15 Maine, 357; Bowers v. Jewell, 2 N. H. 648. Contra, Miller v. Gilleland, 19 Penn. St. 119; 8 c. 1 Am. Law Reg. 672, by a divided court.

A thief can pass a good title to a negotiable note, bill, or bond endorsed by the payee in blank, by mere delivery (for value and without notice); but if the thief commits a forgery in transferring the paper, the holder gets no title, notwithstanding the fact that the act of the forger was unnecessary to pass the title. Colson v. Arnot, 67 N. Y. 253.

The supposed obligation of a party to a forged note or bill cannot be helped out by evidence of a custom in favor of bona fide holders for value, —a custom showing that they are entitled to the amount of the paper. Thus, where a bank has certified a raised check to be good, without knowledge of the forgery, and, still in ignorance of this fact, has paid the check, evidence is not admissible of a custom by which certified checks are treated as good, in the hands of a bona fide holder for value, for the sum to which they may have been raised. Security Bank v. National Bank, 67 N. Y. 458.

The fact that an altered note, bill, or check has been restored to its original condition does not revive its validity. Citizens’ Bank v. Richmond, 121 Mass. 110; Warpole v. Ellison, 4 Houst. 322; Robinson v. Reed, 46 Iowa, 219. But see Kountz v. Kennedy, 63 Penn. St. 187.

Of course, when the parties have consented to an alteration, which is made accordingly, a restoration to the original form of the writing without consent is itself an alteration, and avoids the contract. Beal v. Wood, 5 Mo. App. 691.

BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

But where the maker of a note gives a new note in lieu of an old one which has been altered materially, but without his knowledge, the new note is valid in the hands of a bona fide holder for value. Fraker v. Cullum, 21 Kans. 555.

The alteration complained of must have been made after the defendant had executed his contract. It is no defense, for example, to the acceptor of a bill against a bona fide holder for value that the same was fraudulently altered before his acceptance. Ward v. Allen, 2 Met. 58; ante, p. 668.

We have now to consider what constitutes a material alteration. As to this, it is held that an alteration of the date of a bill or note is a material alteration, and renders the paper invalid even in the hands of a bona fide holder for value; and that it matters not that the holder has not been guilty of negligence in taking the paper. Vance v. Lowther, Law Rep. 1 Ex. Div. 176. See Hirachman v. Budd, Law Rep. 8 Ex. 171. As to the general proposition that an alteration of the date of a note, bill, or check, made after execution or acceptance, is material and therefore fatal, see Wood v. Steele, 6 Wall. 80; Britton v. Dierker, 46 Mo. 591; Enmons v. Meeker, 55 Ind. 821; Kennedy v. Lancaster Bank, 18 Penn. St. 347; Master v. Miller, 4 T. R. 820; s. c. 6 T. R. 637, in Ex. Ch.; Langton v. Lazarus, 5 Mees. & W. 629. The fact that the alteration postpones the time of payment does not help the plaintiff’s case. Wood v. Steele, supra.

If, however, there be proof that the alteration of the date of a bill was made with the assent of the acceptor, or that he subsequently assented, the bill will be good as to him, though invalid as to all other non-assenting parties. Paton v. Winter, 1 Taunt. 420; Tarleton v. Shingler, 7 C. B. 812. Nor will it avoid the instrument to correct a mis-

<br>

### --- Page 0631 ---

ALTERATION.

take in the date. Brutt v. Picard, Ryan & M. 87.

If a note beginning “I promise to pay,” and signed by several persons, be changed to “‘We promise to pay,” the non-consenting parties are discharged; for the alteration would change a several to a joint contract. Humphries v. Gwillow, 13 N. H. 385.

That the addition of an interest clause to a complete note or bill avoids the instrument even in the hands of a bona fide holder for value has already been seen. Holmes v. Trumper; Bradley v. Mann, 37 Mich. 1; Glover v. Robbins, 49 Ala. 219. Adding the words “to bear legal interest,” without authority, destroys the instrument. Lochnane v. Emmerson, 11 Bush, 69. So of an alteration by adding that interest is payable annually or semi-annually is fatal. Marsh v. Griffin, 42 Iowa, 403; Blakey v. Johnson, 13 Bush, 197; Lamar v. Brown, 56 Ala. 157.

It is also a material alteration to strike out the words “after maturity,” where interest is made payable “after maturity.” Brooks v. Allen, 62 Ind. 401.

Alteration of the name of the payee is material. Stoddard v. Penniman, 108 Mass. 866; S. c. 113 Mass. 386. But consent purges the offence and makes the note good. 113 Mass. 386. The same is true of changing “to the order of A.” to “to A. or bearer:” this is material and fatal. Union Bank v. Roberts, 45 Wis. 373.

To add to a negotiable note or bill such words as, “payable at the Bank of S.,” also avoids the instrument in the hands of a bona fide holder for value. Southwark Bank v. Gross, 35 Penn. St. 80; Nazro v. Fuller, 24 Wend. 374; Sudler v. Collins, 2 Houst. 538; Whitesides v. Northern Bank, 10 Bush, 601; Cowie v. Halsall, 4 Barn. & Ald. 197; Burchfield v. Moore, 8 El. & B. 683. But it is held that if an accommodation indorser deliver an accepted bill to the acceptor to enable him to raise money by its negotiation, and the acceptor change a general into a qualified acceptance by designating a particular place of payment, the indorser will still be liable to an innocent purchaser for value. Todd v. Bank of Kentucky, 3 Bush, 626; Rogers v. Poston, 1 Met. (Ky.) 643; Whitesides v. Northern Bank, supra. Comp. ante, p. 576.

The addition of another name to that of the maker of a note, without the consent of the maker, after its complete execution and delivery, is a material alteration. Hamilton v. Hooper, 46 Iowa, 515; Dickerman v. Miner, 43 Iowa, 508; Lunt v. Silver, 6 Mo. App. 186; Haskell v. Champion, 30 Mo. 138; Bowers v. Briggs, 20 Ind. 139; Henry v. Coates, 17 Ind. 161; Crandall v. First National Bank, 61 Ind. 349; Wallace v. Jewell, 21 Ohio, 171; Gardner v. Walsh, 5 El. & B. 82.

But the fact that the payee of a note procures the signature to the same of an additional surety, without the consent of the original surety, is not an alteration to discharge the new surety; though he executed the note solely because of the apparent liability of the original surety, who was now availing himself of the addition as a defence. Crandall v. First National Bank, 61 Ind. 349.

So, too, it has been decided that where the defendant has signed a note for other makers, and has then intrusted it to them to obtain money thereon, and they have procured other names as further security to the purchaser, the defendant is liable if the purchaser took without notice of the understanding of the defendant. Keith v. Goodwin, 31 Vt. 268, distinguishing Gardner v. Walsh, supra, and that class of cases, on the ground that the addition had been made after the note had

<br>

### --- Page 0632 ---

580

become effectual as a contract by delivery. But quere if (upon the supposition that the addition of a name after the note is complete is an alteration) cases like Keith v. Goodwin are not now to be treated as coming within the later authorities considered earlier in this note, so as to discharge the defendant even though he was somewhat negligent?

The addition of another signature in the nature of an indorsement, however, though put upon the face of the paper, cannot be made ground of objection. Ex parte Yates, 2 DeG. & J. 191.

Figures in the margin are said to be no part of a bill or note; and an alteration making them conform to the body of the paper, though without consent of the defendant, has no effect. Woolfolk v. Bank of America, 10 Bush, 604.

It has also been held to be no alteration for the person to whom a note is given in blank, save the figures for the amount, placed on the upper margin of the note, to change the figures and fill up the note accordingly. Schryver v. Hawkes, 22 Ohio St. 308. See, however, ante, pp. 549, 650, 674, where the question of negligence in connection with alterations is considered.

Although a signature is forged, if knowing the fact and intending to accept it, the defendant acknowledges it as his own, he thus assumes the paper, and is bound as though the signature were genuine. Wellington v. Jackson, 12 Mass. 157; Bartlett v. Tucker, 104 Mass. 336, 341; Greenfield Bank v. Crafts, 4 Allen, 447. But the evidence of assent to an alteration should be clear. Swift v. Barber, 28 Mich. 608. And there must be full knowledge of the facts. Cravens v. Gillilan, 68 Mo. 28; First National Bank v. Gay, 63 Mo. 33; German Bank v. Dunn, 62 Mo. 79.

BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

The ratification need not be based upon a consideration. Cravens v. Gillilan, supra; First National Bank v. Gay, supra.

It is clear than an erasure of indorsements subsequent to the indorsement of the payee may be made by a bona fide indorsee for value of a bill or note. Hayden v. Goodnow, 89 Conn. 164. Such an act in no way affects the liability of prior parties, and is a matter of every-day occurrence in the courts.

Proof of the signature to a note or bill affords prima facie evidence of the genuineness of the body of the instrument; but if any question is raised upon this point, and evidence introduced by the defendant, it devolves upon the plaintiff, according to the rule in Massachusetts at least, to establish the genuineness of the paper. Simpson v. Davis, 119 Mass. 269; Davis v. Jenny, 1 Met. 221, 224; Wilde v. Ormsby, 6 Cush. 814. See Elbert v. McClelland, 8 Bush, 577.

In Simpson v. Davis, supra, the following instruction of the lower court was held correct. The proof of the signature of a party to a note is prima facie evidence that the whole body of the note is the act of said party. But such proof is merely prima facie, and when (as in that case) proper pleadings present the issue, the plaintiff is bound to prove affirmatively that the note declared upon and put in evidence is substantially the note made by the defendant; and when evidence has been offered by the defendant tending to show a material alteration, the burden of proof is with the plaintiff to satisfy the court or jury, upon the whole case, that the note so declared upon was in substance and effect the note of the defendant.

In approving this instruction, the Supreme Court took occasion to say that the same rule applied to such a

<br>

### --- Page 0633 ---

ALTERATION.

Case as applied when a want of consideration is relied upon as the defence to a promissory note: the burden of proof is upon the plaintiff, upon the whole evidence, to establish the fact. Simpson v. Davis, supra. Endicott, J., referring to the following cases as to consideration: Delano v. Bartlett, 6 Cush. 364; Morris v. Bowman, 12 Gray, 467; Powers v. Russell, 18 Pick. 69, 76; ante, p. 90.

In like manner, it is held in Michigan that where evidence is given on both sides as to the time when an alteration was made, the burden of proof rests upon the plaintiff to establish the note or bill which he sues upon. Willett v. Shepard, 84 Mich. 106; Atwood v. Cornwall, 25 Mich. 142. See Chism v. Toomer, 27 Ark. 108.

But whether it is actually necessary in Massachusetts for the defendant to go as far as was done in Simpson v. Davis, supra, and in Wilde v. Armsby, 6 Cush. 314, and introduce evidence of the time the alteration was made was not clearly stated in either of those cases. It appears, however, from Ely v. Ely, 6 Gray, 489, that it is not necessary. It was there held wrong to instruct the jury that, in the absence of all proof to the contrary, the presumption of law was that interlineations and alterations were made prior to or contemporaneously with the execution of the instrument in question, a mortgage; and this too, though in fact the alterations, upon inspection, gave some color to the position of the plaintiff that they were made before or at the time of execution. The court said there was no such legal presumption, and that the plaintiff must prove the genuineness of the instrument and that the alterations were honestly and properly made. See Wilde v. Armsby, supra.

As to the existence of a presumption (apart from evidence) concerning the time when an alteration was made,

whether before or after the defendant signed, the authorities are, however, conflicting. See Gooch v. Bryant, 1 Shep. 890; Beaman v. Russell, 20 Vt. 205; Jackson v. Osborn, 2 Wend. 559; Jackson v. Jacoby, 9 Cowen, 125; Herrick v. Malin, 22 Wend. 888; Smith v. McGowan, 8 Barb. 404; Hayden v. Goodnow, 40 Conn. 164; Cole v. Hills, 44 N. H. 227; Humphries v. Gwillow, 13 N. H. 385; Hills v. Barnes, 11 N. H. 895; Henman v. Dickinson, 8 Bing. 183.

The English authorities appear to agree with those of Massachusetts. They are reviewed down to 1850 in Wilde v. Armsby, supra. Hemming v. Trenery, 9 Ad. & E. 926; Bishop v. Chambre, Moody & M. 116; 8 C. & P. 55; Henman v. Dickinson, 65 Bing. 183; Knight v. Clements, 83 Nev. & P. 875; 8 C. & Ad. & E. 215; Clifford v. Parker, 2 Man. & G. 909; Cariss v. Tattersall, 2 Man. & G. 890; Whitfield v. Collingwood, 1 Car. & K. 825; Taylor v. Mosely, 6 Car. & P. 278.

It is laid down in Connecticut, that where an erasure or alteration is found in an instrument under which a party derives his title, and the adverse party claims that such erasure or alteration was improperly made, the jury are, from all the circumstances before them, to determine whether the instrument is thereby rendered invalid. Hayden v. Goodnow, 40 Conn. 164; Bailey v. Taylor, 11 Conn. 641. The burden of accounting for the erasure or alteration is not necessarily on the party producing the instrument, according to the rule in Connecticut. Ib. See also Cole v. Hills, 44 N. H. 227, 284, to the same effect.

The alteration of an instrument by a stranger (an act called spoliation) has no effect upon the rights or obligations of the parties. Langenberger v. Kroeger, 48 Cal. 147; Brooks v. Allen, 62

<br>

### --- Page 0634 ---

582

Ind. 401; State v. Berg, 50 Ind. 496; Cochran v. Nebeker, 48 Ind. 459; 1 Greenleaf, Evidence, § 566.

It is difficult, however, in some cases to determine who is a stranger within the rule. The most that can be said by way of a general proposition of law is that every person is a stranger who has neither actual nor presumptive authority to act for the defendant. And no one has presumptive authority who has not been held out by the defendant, either directly or by a course of conduct equivalent to a direct holding out, as having the right to do the act in question. See Brooks v. Allen, supra, and Aetna Ins. Co. v. Winchester, 43 Conn. 391, that an alteration made by a person to whom a note is delivered as agent of the payee is fatal, if he had, in fact, no authority to alter it. Ante, p. 576.

§ 4. Want of Assent. Fraud.—The difference between the class of cases heretofore under consideration and those represented by the principal case, Foster v. Mackinnon, is that, while in those already considered a valid contract was, in fact, at one time executed, and its validity destroyed by a subsequent act, in cases like Foster v. Mackinnon, no assent was ever given to the execution of the contract in question.

There is another distinction worthy of notice between the two classes; to wit, that, while by most of the later authorities mere negligence in the defendant will not preclude him from alleging an alteration after he signed his name to the paper, negligence in respect of the contract itself to be executed will, by inference from Foster v. Mackinnon and other cases, preclude him from alleging against an innocent indorsee for value that he never assented to the contract to which his name is signed. In other words, if there be no alteration, negligence will be equivalent to assent. See Anderson

BONA FIDE HOLDER FOR VALUE.

v. Walter, 34 Mich. 118; Gibbs v. Linabury, 22 Mich. 479; Burson v. Huntington, 21 Mich. 415; De Camp v. Hamma, 29 Ohio St. 467; Ross v. Doland, 29 Ohio St. 473; Winchell v. Crider, 29 Ohio St. 480; Shirts v. Overjohn, 60 Mo. 305; Frederick v. Clemens, 60 Mo. 813; Corby v. Weddle, 57 Mo. 452; Martin v. Smylee, 55 Mo. 877; Briggs v. Ewart, 61 Mo. 245; Griffiths v. Kellogg, 89 Wis. 290; Butter v. Carns, 37 Wis. 61; Walker v. Ebert, 29 Wis. 194; Kellogg v. Steiner, 29 Wis. 626; Abbott v. Rose, 62 Maine, 194; Whitney v. Snyder, 2 Lans. 477; Chapman v. Rose, 66 N. Y. 187.

The general result of the authorities may be thus stated: Where the evidence shows that, without negligence on the part of the defendant, he was imposed upon by the fraudulent representations, tricks, or artifice of another party to the paper, as to the nature of the contract he was signing, and the defendant signed the instrument innocently, without knowing that it was a bill, note, or check, and under the belief thus caused that it was another sort of instrument, there can be no recovery against him by any person. If, however, the defendant were guilty of negligence in not ascertaining the nature of the engagement to which he was giving his signature, he will be liable to any holder into whose hands the paper may pass for value and without notice of the facts, and to any one claiming under such a holder, though himself not a bona fide holder for value. As to this last point (the general right of a holder to recover upon the title of an earlier party), see Cromwell v. Sac County, 96 U. S. 61, 58; ante, pp. 439, 606.

It is held, however, in Indiana and in Iowa, that one who executes a note without reading, upon a false representation that it is an engagement of

<br>

### --- Page 0635 ---

WANT OF ASSENT.

Another kind cannot set up the fraud against a bona fide holder for value. Kimble v. Christie, 55 Ind. 140; Nebeker v. Cutsinger, 48 Ind. 426; Wright v. Flinn, 33 Iowa, 159.

The court of Illinois holds that where the execution of a promissory note has been obtained through the usual device of men who go about the country as dealers in patent rights or new inventions, with papers so prepared as to obtain the signatures of the people to promissory notes, when the latter have no intention of executing such instruments, the paper is not enforceable against the maker. And this, too, even though the maker cannot explain just how the device or trick was made successful. Champion v. Ulmer, 70 Ill. 822. See Gibbs v. Linabury, 22 Mich. 479. But this has been made the subject of statute.

It is hardly necessary to say that a blank piece of paper containing a man’s signature cannot be filled up in the form of a promissory note so as to bind the party whose name has thus been used, even in favor of a bona fide holder for value, unless there was an intention to that effect. Cline v. Guthrie, 42 Ind. 227; Caulkins v. Whisler, 29 Iowa, 495.

<br>

### --- Page 0636 ---

584 DISCHARGING DRAWER OR INDORSER.

DISCHARGING DRAWER OR INDORSER.

OXIE v. SPENCER.  
(2 Wharton, 253. Supreme Court of Pennsylvania, December, 1836.)

Additional security. Extension of time.—If the holder of a promissory note takes a check upon a bank from the maker, dated six days after the maturity of the note, the check to be in full satisfaction of the note, if paid, this operates as an extension of time to the maker, and discharges an indorser.

At the maturity of the note in question, the holder took from the maker a draft on other parties, payable six days afterwards, to be in full satisfaction of the note, if duly paid.

Kennepy, J. The defendant here, having indorsed the note in question for the accommodation of the drawer, and therefore being regarded as a surety merely, it is admitted that, if further time was given when it fell due by the holder to the drawer for the payment thereof, the defendant is thereby discharged. And the only question to be decided is whether, from the facts set forth by the defendant in his special plea, to which the plaintiff has demurred, the law will imply an agreement made on the 3d of May, the day the note became payable by the holder of it, to give further time until the 6th of the same month to the drawer for the payment thereof.

Had the defendant pleaded the general issue only, and under it, as he certainly might, given evidence of the facts set forth in his special plea, and the truth of them had been

<br>

### --- Page 0637 ---

OKIE v. SPENCER. 585

clearly established by the evidence or the admission of the plaintiff, without more having been shown to the jury, it would undoubtedly have been the duty of the court to have instructed the jury that the facts thus established implied an agreement on the part of the holder of the note, for an adequate consideration received by him, to give time to the drawer for the payment of it, without having the consent of the defendant; and that the latter was thereby discharged from his liability as indorser. In the absence of all proof to the contrary, it cannot be supposed here that the drawer, when the note had become payable, could have had any other motive for giving the check of himself and his partner, securing the payment of it at the expiration of six days, than that of procuring indulgence for that space of time upon his note from the holder of it. That such, too, must have been the understanding of them both at the time seems to be the necessary inference from the facts stated, if our judgments are to be guided in this respect by what we know to be the common and ordinary motives which generally influence and produce such arrangements. Marshall, the partner of the drawer of the note, does not appear to have been bound for the payment of it in any way before it fell due, which tends generally to strengthen, and, in truth, to make the inference that the check was given to procure further time for the payment of the note irresistible. And, although the check cannot be considered as having been taken in satisfaction of the note, nor as having extinguished it, yet the right of the holder to proceed against the drawer, to enforce the payment of it by suit, was thereby suspended until after the expiration of the six days. It was in effect changing, without the consent of the defendant, the terms upon which he had agreed as indorser to become liable for the payment of the note, and depriving him of the right to pay the note at maturity, if the drawer failed to do so, and then to sue him immediately for it, and therefore amounted to a release of him from his lia-

<br>

### --- Page 0638 ---

586 DISCHARGING DRAWER OR INDORSER.

bility. He had guarantied by his indorsement the payment of the note on the 3d of May, 1833; and it was not competent for the holder and the drawer, without his concurrence, to extend his guaranty to the 9th of that month, which would clearly have been the effect of their agreement and the giving of the check, if the defendant were still to be held liable for the payment of the note. That the holder, by accepting the check, put it out of his power to proceed on the note, by suit against the drawer, until after the six days, cannot, as it appears to me, be controverted upon any ground that would seem to be consistent with the nature of the transaction, and what must have been the intent of the parties. Had the drawer given his own check merely, for the payment of the note at the expiration of the six days, there might have been some color for saying that he had not thereby precluded himself from bringing suit on it during that period, because it might then have been argued with great plausibility, if not correctly, that he had obtained by it no additional security, and consequently no adequate consideration to make a promise of indulgence binding; that by the check he acquired nothing except the personal responsibility of the drawer, which he had before by virtue of the note; and therefore, had he even made an express promise of indulgence for the six days, it might have been alleged that he would not have been bound by it, for want of a sufficient consideration; but, as the case is presented by the special plea and demurrer, no such argument can be advanced or pretended; for, by the check, the holder of the note received the additional responsibility of Marshall, as a security for the payment of it; and it would therefore seem almost impossible to imagine any other reason for giving such additional security than that of procuring an extension of payment for the six days. It is true that it may seem to have been but a short indulgence; but, being a suspension of the right of the holder of the note to sue the drawer upon it during that period, it operated as effectually to discharge the

<br>

### --- Page 0639 ---

OKIE v. SPENCER. * 587

defendant from his liability as if it had been six years; for, in either case, to hold the defendant to be still bound by his indorsement would be making him liable upon terms, and, in short, for the fulfilment of a contract different from what he had agreed to. The time of payment mentioned in a note is always a very material part of it; and if it may be enlarged without the consent of the indorser, and he, notwithstanding, be held liable upon his indorsement, there is no reason why the amount may not also be enlarged; but it is obvious that nothing of the kind can be done without operating great injustice towards him, and therefore it is, if it be done, it shall release him from his liability. Every man, as long as he is a free agent, must be permitted to declare the terms upon which he is willing to incur an obligation; and, having done so, it cannot be altered in any material point whatever without his consent, nor yet anything be done which may affect his rights in relation thereto.

The counsel for the plaintiff has cited in opposition to this the case of Pring v. Clarkson, 1 Barn. & C. 14; 8s. c. 8 Eng. Com. Law, 10, where, a bill of exchange having been dishonored, the acceptor transmitted a new bill for a larger amount to the payee, without having had any communication with him respecting the first. The payee discounted the second bill with the holder of the first, which he received back as part of the amount, and afterwards, for a valuable consideration, indorsed it to the plaintiff. It was held that the second bill was merely a collateral security, and that the receipt of it by the payee did not amount to giving time to the acceptor of the first bill, so as to exonerate the drawer. Mr. Chief Justice Abbott, in pronouncing the opinion of the court, says: “In no case has it been said that taking a collateral security from the acceptor shall have that effect,” — that is, of discharging the other parties to the bill, — and concludes by saying: “Here the second bill was nothing more than a collateral security.” Now, it is not easy to perceive why a collateral

<br>

### --- Page 0640 ---

588 DISCHARGING DRAWER OR INDORSER.

Security should not have such an effect; for surely there is nothing in the nature of it which renders the giving or the taking of it inconsistent with the holder’s agreeing to give time to the acceptor of a bill or the drawer of a note. On the contrary, such indulgence may be, and doubtless is in most cases, the very consideration upon which the collateral security is given and obtained; and, as I have endeavored to show, makes the case, in the absence of proof of an express agreement to give time, still stronger in favor of an implied agreement to that effect than where there is nothing more given than a bare renewal of the promise by the acceptor of the original bill, or the drawer of the former note, to pay the amount at a future date. But Chief Justice Abbott was mistaken when he said: “In no case had it been said that taking a collateral security from the acceptor shall have that effect;” for, in Gould v. Robson, 8 East, 576, decided some fifteen years before, it was not only said, but the case itself turned upon the very point. There the holder of the bill of exchange, when it fell due, after taking part payment of the acceptor, agreed to take a new acceptance from him for the remainder, payable at a future day, but in the meantime the holder to keep the original bill in his hands as security; and it was held that it amounted to a giving of time and a new credit to the acceptor, and therefore discharged the indorser. Besides, the authority of Pring and Clarkson has been doubted by the profession. Mr. Chitty, in his Treatise on Bills, 442, 8th Eng. ed., after repeating the principle laid down in it, adds: “But it is submitted that the mere receiving further security, payable at a future day, would in general imply an engagement to wait till it becomes due.” See also Bayley, Bills, 5th ed. 345, note 31, and Chitty, Jr., Bills, ed. of 1834, 100 w. a. note 1; and in Kendrick v. Lomax, 2 C. & J. 405, it would seem to be overruled; for it was decided there that the holder, by taking a renewed bill, impliedly agrees to give time until it becomes due, and cannot sue in the interim on the original bill. Judgment affirmed.

<br>

### --- Page 0641 ---

M’LEMORE v. POWELL. 589

McLEMORE, Plaintiff in Error, v. POWELL AND OTHERS,  
Defendants in Error.

(12 Wheaton, 554. Supreme Court of the United States, January, 1827.)

Agreement for delay.— Mere agreement by the holder with the drawer of a bill of exchange for delay, made without consideration, and not communicated to the indorser, does not discharge the indorser.

THE case is stated in the opinion of the court.

Story, J. This is a writ of error to the Circuit Court of the United States for the District of West Tennessee.

The original action was assumpsit, brought by Powell, Fosters, & Co., as holders of a bill of exchange, drawn by one Thomas Fletcher, in May, 1819, at Nashville, upon Messrs. McNeil, Fisk, & Rutherford, at New Orleans, payable to Thomas Read, or order, for $2,000 in sixty days after date, and by him indorsed to the defendant, John C. McLemore, and by him to the plaintiffs. The bill, upon presentment for acceptance, was dishonored, and due notice of the dishonor was given to the defendant.

At the trial, upon the general issue, Thomas Fletcher, the drawer, was, under a lease from the defendant, McLemore, examined as a witness, and, among other things, testified that, in the month of October following the dishonor of the bill, "one of the plaintiffs applied to him at Nashville for the money on the bill, and threatened to sue immediately if an arrangement was not made to pay the bill. The witness then proposed to the plaintiff, if he would indulge him four or five weeks, he would himself, to a certainty, pay the bill. To this the plaintiff agreed, and told the witness he was going to Louisville, Kentucky, and would return by Nashville, about the expiration of that time, and would receive said payment. Since said time the witness has never seen

<br>

### --- Page 0642 ---

590 DISCHARGING DRAWER OR INDORSER.

said plaintiff.” The witness further testified that the defendant was an accommodation indorser for him on the bill; that the plaintiff told him that the bill would be left with a Mr. Washington, at Nashville; that he expected he would himself be at that place at the time agreed on, but that, if he did not come, he would give the instructions to Mr. Washington, by letter, what to do if the witness did not pay at the expiration of the time agreed on. It did not appear that any consideration was paid or stipulated for this delay; and no suit was commenced until after this period had elapsed. The district judge instructed the jury that, if they believed the conversation above stated amounted to no more than an agreement that a suit should not be brought for four or five weeks, and that no premium or consideration was given or paid, or to be paid by Fletcher, the indorsers were not discharged; that an agreement for giving day must be an obligatory contract for a consideration which ties up the hands of the creditor and disables him from suing, thereby affecting the interests and rights of the indorser; that the indorser has a right to require and demand of the creditor to bring a suit against the drawer, and if he has disabled himself from bringing a suit by a contract for a consideration, he has thereby released the indorser; and that if the jury were satisfied from the testimony that time was given for a valuable consideration paid or to be paid, or that a new security was taken by the holder, that the indorser was discharged and absolved from all the obligations of the indorsement.

Under this instruction, the jury found a verdict for the plaintiffs, upon which there was judgment given in their favor. A bill of exceptions was taken to the charge of the court; and the present writ of error is brought for the purpose of ascertaining its legal correctness.

It is unnecessary to give any opinion upon that part of the charge which respects the right of an indorser to require the holder to commence a suit against the drawer. In general,

<br>

### --- Page 0643 ---

M'LEMORE v. POWELL. 591

the indorser, by paying the bill, has a complete power to reinstate himself in the possession and ownership of the bill, and thus to entitle himself to a personal remedy on the instrument against all antecedent parties. The same reason, therefore, does not exist, as may in common cases of suretyship, to compel the creditor to active diligence by suit against the principal. Without expressing any opinion on this point, it is sufficient to say that the error, if any, was favorable to the defendant, and, therefore, it can form no subject of complaint on his part.

The case then resolves itself into this question,—whether a mere agreement with the drawers for delay, without any consideration for it, and without any communication with or assent of the indorser, is a discharge of the latter, after he has been fixed in his responsibility by the refusal of the drawee, and due notice to himself. And we are all of opinion that it does not. We admit the doctrine that, although the indorser has received due notice of the dishonor of the bill, yet if the holder afterwards enters into any new agreement with the drawer for delay, in any manner changing the nature of the original contract, or affecting the rights of the indorser, or to the prejudice of the latter, it will discharge him. But, in order to produce such a result, the agreement must be one binding in law upon the parties, and have a sufficient consideration to support it. An agreement without consideration is utterly void, and does not suspend for a moment the rights of any of the parties. In the present case, the jury have found that there was no consideration for the promise to delay a suit, and, consequently, the plaintiffs were at liberty immediately to have enforced their remedies against all the parties. It was correctly said by Lord Eldon, in English v. Darley, 2 Bos. & Pul. 61, that “as long as the holder is passive, all his remedies remain;” and, we add, that he is not bound to active diligence. But, if the holder enters into a valid contract for delay, he thereby suspends his own

<br>

### --- Page 0644 ---

DISCHARGING DRAWER OR INDORSER.

remedy on the bill for the stipulated period; and, if the indorser were to pay the bill, he could only be subrogated to the rights of the holder, and the drawer could or might have the same equities against him as against the holder himself. If, therefore, such a contract be entered into without his assent, it is to his prejudice, and discharges him.

The cases proceed upon the distinction here pointed out, and conclusively settle the present action. In Walwyn v. St. Quentin, 1 Bos. & Pul. 652, where the action was by indorsees against the drawer of a bill, it appeared that, after the bill had become due, and been protested for non-payment, though no notice had been given to the drawer, he having no effects in the hands of the acceptor, the plaintiffs received part of the money on account from the indorser; and to an application from the acceptor, stating that it was probable he should be able to pay at a future period, they returned for answer that they would not press him. The court held it no discharge; and Lord Chief Justice Eyre, in delivering the opinion of the court, said that if this forbearance to sue the acceptor had taken place before noticing and protesting for non-payment, so that the bill had not been demanded when due, it was clear the drawer would have been discharged, for it would be giving a new credit to the acceptor. But that, after protest for non-payment, and notice to the drawer, or an equivalent to notice, a right to sue the drawer had attached, and the holder was not bound to sue the acceptor. He might forbear to sue him. The same doctrine was held in Arundel Bank v. Goble, reported in a note to Chitty on Bills. Chitty, 879, note c, ed. 1821. There the acceptor applied for time, and the holders assented to it, but said they should expect interest. It was contended that this was a discharge of the drawer; but the court held otherwise, because the agreement of the plaintiffs to wait was without consideration, and the acceptor might, notwithstanding the agreement, have been sued the next instant; and that the understanding that

<br>

### --- Page 0645 ---

TIERNAN v. WOODRUFF. 593

interest should be paid by the acceptor made no difference. So, in Badnall v. Samuel, 8 Price’s Exch. 521, in a suit by the holder against a prior indorser of a bill of exchange, it was held that a treaty for delay between the holder and acceptor, upon terms which were not finally accepted, did not discharge the defendant, although an actual delay had taken place during the negotiation, because there was no binding contract which precluded the plaintiffs from suing the acceptor at any time.

Upon authority, therefore, we are of opinion, that this writ of error cannot be sustained, and that the judgment below was right.
