But in that case (the paper being in reality accommodation paper, accepted for the benefit of the party who paid, though this was not known), if the full amount due had been paid, the holder could recover nothing more than costs; unless before him there were a party entitled to recover more.

The doctrine of Jones v. Broadhurst came under the special notice of the court in this case of Cook v. Lister, 13 Com. B. n. 8. 548, and was there explained. The learned Chief Justice points out the fact that it seemed to be the first impression of the court in Jones v. Broadhurst that the plea of payment by the drawer would have been a good answer if it had shown that the payment was made in satisfaction of the holder’s claim against all parties to the bill. And though this interlocutory opinion of the court closed with an expression to the effect that the more serious question was, conceding the plea to allege payment as satisfaction of the bill, whether the payment could inure to the defendant,—the effect of which was to leave the impression that the latter was the point afterwards considered in the elaborate judgment,—the Chief Justice thought that this was not the question of the case. “The substance of the judgment,” said he, “is, that there is no allegation that the goods were delivered in satisfaction of the claim of the holders against the acceptor. In the present case,” he continued, “the payments made by Cheeseborough & Son and Yewdall & Son [the drawers of the bills in suit] were, beyond all question, made in satisfaction pro tanto of the claim of the plaintiffs as against all parties to the bills.” It was accordingly held that the defendant, an acceptor for value,

DRAWER OR INDORSER. 667

could plead the payment by the drawers; and as the payment had equalled the face of the bill, including what the defendant had already paid, the latter was considered no longer liable on the bills. And this, too, though the drawers were still debtors to the plaintiff in respect of the matters for which the bills were drawn.

The editor of the American edition of the Common Bench Reports is mistaken in stating that Cook v. Lister has overruled Randall v. Moon. He says that the latter case is overruled by the former, inasmuch as the former “decides that an action cannot be maintained by the holder of a bill of exchange, without notice that it was an accommodation bill, against the acceptor after payment by the maker [drawer].” 18 C. B. n. 8, 697, n. Nothing inconsistent with this was decided in Randall v. Moon. That case decided only that the holder could sue and recover the amount of the bill and damages against the accommodation acceptor after payment by the drawer (not in behalf of the acceptor) when it does not appear that the drawer was the accommodated party, that is, the principal debtor; as we have already said. But in Cook v. Lister it appeared (not only that the payment had been made on behalf of the acceptor, but) that the parties so paying, the drawers, were the accommodated parties. They would therefore, without fault of their own, have been compelled to pay twice by any other rule. If such had appeared to be the situation in Randall v. Moon, the decision would have been different, as appears by the opinion of the court.

The result of the English cases we understand to be this:—

1. That payment of money by the drawer or indorser of a bill or note to the holder cannot be pleaded by the acceptor or maker, either in bar of an

<br>

### --- Page 0720 ---

668

action by the holder, or in reduction of the damages; unless (a) the payment was made and received as payment and extinguishment in full or pro tanto of the paper, and not as a mere release of the party paying; or unless (b) the bill or note was accepted or made for the accommodation of the party making the payment, of which the holder had notice.

2. If in case (b) the holder had not notice that the party paying him was the principal debtor, he is then entitled, according to Williams, J. (and there is nothing to the contrary from the other judges), to recover at least nominal damages of the acceptor or maker, and, if less than the amount of the paper was paid, the balance due thereon.

3. Compulsory receipt of payment from the drawer or indorser under a stay of proceedings (opposed by the holder) is not payment on behalf of the acceptor or maker.

4. The courts will not presume, in considering the effect of a payment by the drawer, that an accommodation acceptance of a bill was given for the benefit of the drawer; but the acceptor must show that the payment was made by the accommodated party, the principal debtor. And it would seem also that the burden of proof should be on the acceptor or maker to show that the holder had notice that the bill or note was accommodation paper. Then, if payment in full was made before action, the acceptor or maker is not liable even for costs. If but part of the amount due were paid, it would seem still to be a question of fact whether the payment were made as an extinguishment of the liability of the acceptor or maker.

The foregoing conclusions are largely enforced by very late English authority; in which it has been laid down to be a good plea, as a defence pro tanto to an action by the holder of a bill against the acceptor, that the drawer became bankrupt, and that the plaintiff received a sum named as a dividend from his estate on account of the bill, and that as to that sum he was suing only as trustee for the drawer; setting up, by way of set-off, a debt due to the defendant from the drawer. Thornton v. Maynard, Law Rep. 10 C. P. 695.

And, according to the same authority, payment partially or in full by the drawer of a bill to the indorsee, not made for the acceptor, does not disentitle the indorsee to sue the acceptor for the full amount; but in such a case the indorsee (prima facie; see ante, p. 665) sues in whole or in part, according to the amount paid by the drawer, as trustee of that party. Thornton v. Maynard, supra, Jones v. Broadhurst, 9 C. B. 173; Agra & M. Bank v. Leighton, Law Rep. 2 Ex. 56; Cochrane v. Green, 9 C. B. N. S. 448; Elkin v. Baker, 11 C. B. N. S. 526; Clark v. Cort, 1 Craig & P. 154. To the same effect Ex parte Talcott, 9 Bank Reg. 502.

The subject has not been so fully considered by the courts of this country, but it is apprehended that our law is in harmony with that of England.

In the principal case, Farmers’ Bank v. Rathbone, the claim of the plaintiff was for only the balance due on the paper, after the payment made by the drawer; and whether the bill were to be considered as accepted for accommodation or not, the decision is therefore in accordance with the doctrines above laid down.

In Murray v. Judah, 6 Cowen, 484, it appeared that a check had been transferred by the holder as collateral security for a debt. Afterwards, the drawer failing, the check was appraised, and the creditor took it absolutely at a sum less than its face, giving the holder credit for the amount of the appraisal.

<br>

### --- Page 0721 ---

ACCOMMODATION PARTIES.

The drawer now contended that the plaintiff’s claim must be reduced by the amount of the appraisal; but the court held properly that he was liable for the face of the check. The paper had indeed been drawn for the accommodation of the payee, but the part payment (through the appraisal) had not been made by him.

In Clopper v. Union Bank, 7 Har. & J. 92, a promissory note had been made by C. in favor of P. for P.’s accommodation, and by him indorsed to the holder; and, after the note fell due, P. gave the holder his own note for the debt. It was held that the first note was not extinguished unless the last was received by the holder in substitution and satisfaction of the first. This is in perfect conformity with the English rule: the note of P. was not to be treated as payment on behalf of the defendant, unless so made and received.

In Parks v. Ingram, 22 N. H. 283, 293, the court of New Hampshire say: “The fact that the holders of the drafts signed a composition deed, and agreed to discharge the acceptors upon certain terms, would have discharged the drawers from liability to them, if the drafts had been based upon funds in the hands of the drawees; because, in such case, the liability of the drawees would be antecedent to that of the drawers, and the contract of discharge would be prejudicial to the interests of the drawers. Ex parte Wilson, 11 Ves. 410; Lewis v. Jones, 4 Barn. & C. 506; Story, Bills, § 429; Scarborough v. Harris, 1 Bay. 197; Robertson v. Vogle, 1 Dall. 262; Lynch v. Reynold, 16 Johns. 41. But here the drawers having assented to the arrangement, and signed the deed themselves, are estopped from saying that it is a contract to which they did not agree. In an action of the holders, they could not successfully plead the discharge. Bruen v. Marquand, 17 Johns. 58. If a party agrees

SURETIES. 669

to the discharge of one liable before himself, he cannot afterwards take advantage of the discharge. Bradford v. Hubbard, 8 Pick. 155; Smith v. Winter, 4 Mees. & W. 454. But there is a further and complete answer to this position. These drafts having been drawn for the accommodation of the drawers, they are themselves principals on the bills. They occupy a position antecedent to that of the acceptors. The acceptors are the sureties, and any discharge of them by the holders cannot alter the liability of the principals. A discharge of a party to a bill does not affect the liability of those whose position is antecedent to the party discharged. It discharges only those who are subsequently liable. Sargent v. Appleton, 6 Mass. 85; English v. Daily, 2 Bos. & P. 61; Mallet v. Thompson, 6 Esp. 178; Walwyn v. St. Quentin, 1 Bos. & P. 652.”

It should be observed that cases of the above character arise only where the paper is not taken up by the party making the payment. Of course, if the holder delivers the paper to the party paying, he has no further remedy thereon. Still, the act is not payment of the bill or note, unless so intended; and the acceptor or maker is liable to the new holder, if he be not the principal debtor.

§ 5. Accommodation Parties. Sureties.—The above cases are sufficient to show that, in the case of paper made or accepted for accommodation, the party granting the accommodation stands in a situation similar to that of an ordinary surety; and that the party receiving the accommodation stands in a situation similar to that of a principal. It was at one time accordingly supposed, as is stated in Farmers’ Bank v. Rathbone, that an accommodation acceptor would be discharged by the giving time to the principal debtor by one having notice of the character of

<br>

### --- Page 0722 ---

670

the bill. Laxton v. Peat, 2 Campb. 185; Collett v. Haigh, 3 Campb. 281; Adams v. Gregg, 2 Stark. 531. But quod simile non est idem; and the doctrine has since been overruled. It is now settled that the giving of time to the principal debtor will not discharge the acceptor. Fentum v. Pocock, 5 Taunt. 192; Price v. Edmonds, 10 Barn. & C. 584; Nichols v. Norris, 8 Barn. & Ad. 41; Harrison v. Courtauld, 3 Barn. & Ad. 36; Cronise v. Kellogg, 20 Ill. 11; Lambert v. Sanford, 2 Blackf. 187; Hansborough v. Gray, 3 Gratt. 356; Bank of Montgomery v. Walker, 9 Serg. & R. 229; &c. 12 Serg. & R. 382; and other cases cited in Farmers’ Bank v. Rathbone, ante, p. 635. See also ante, p. 607.

The cases have not always clearly defined what an accommodation bill or note is. The court in Farmers’ Bank v. Rathbone inclined to the opinion that the bill in question in that case was not an accommodation bill; and in this we think the court were right. A bill or note does not become accommodation paper by the failure of the consideration moving to the acceptor or maker, or even by the non-existence ab initio of a consideration, if that be all. The signature must have been given for accommodation.

This is the view of Mr. Justice Byles. “An accommodation bill,” he says, “is a bill to which the accommodating party, be he acceptor, drawer, or indorser, has put his name, without consideration, for the purpose of benefiting or accommodating some other party who desires to raise money on it, and is to provide for the bill when due.” Bills, 128, 11th Lond. ed.

In another part of his work, the author further says that in common language a bill accepted or indorsed without any consideration moving to the party making himself liable on the bill is, indeed, called an accommodation bill; but in strictness an accommodation bill is not merely a bill accepted or indorsed without value received by the acceptor or indorser, but a bill accepted or indorsed without value by the acceptor or indorser to accommodate the drawer or some other party; i.e., that the party accommodated may raise money upon it or otherwise make use of it. Bills, 405.

And he adds that this distinction is of importance; for a party accepting a bill merely without consideration (as if, for example, he does not know the state of accounts between himself and the drawer) and afterwards sued on that bill cannot charge the drawer with the costs of defending the action. Bagnall v. Andrews, 7 Bing. 217; &c. 4 Moore & P. 839. See Tindal v. Bell, 11 Mees. & W. 228; Ronneberg v. Falkland Islands Co., 17 C. B. (N.S.) 81. Whereas the acceptor of an accommodation bill, properly so called, who is compelled by action to pay it, may have a claim upon the drawer for all the expenses of the action. Ex parte Marshall, 1 Atk. 262; Jones v. Brooke, 4 Taunt. 464; Stratten v. Matthews, 18 L. J. Ex. 58; &c. 3 Ex. 48; Garrard v. Cottrell, 10 Q. B. 679. An accommodation acceptor, however, cannot charge the party, it is said, with the costs of an action to which the accommodation acceptor had evidently no defence. Byles, ut supra; Roach v. Thompson, Moody & M. 487; Beech v. Jones, 5 Com. B. 696.

To this should be added the distinctions above stated between the position of an acceptor for value and an accommodation acceptor; to wit, the effect of payment by the drawer.

§ 6. Payment in Worthless Paper.— The conflict of authority between the courts of Pennsylvania and New York upon the question whether one who

<br>

### --- Page 0723 ---

PAYMENT IN WORTHLESS PAPER.

has taken for value the paper of an insolvent bank, both parties supposing it to have been solvent, and the vendor having also paid value for the paper, can recover back the consideration paid, has extended itself to other States. The Pennsylvania rule prevails in Alabama, Lowrey v. Murrell, 2 Porter, 280; in Delaware, Corbit v. Bank of Smyrna, 2 Harr. 235; in Tennessee, Ware v. Street, 2 Head, 609; and in Virginia, Edmunds v. Digges, 1 Gratt. 359.

The New York rule prevails in New Hampshire, Fogg v. Sawyer, 9 N. H. 865; in Vermont, Wainwright v. Webster, 11 Vt. 676; Gilman v. Peck, 11 Vt. 516; in Maine, Frontier Bank v. Morse, 22 Maine, 88; in South Carolina, Harley v. Thornton, 2 Hill, 609; in Wisconsin, Townsends v. Bank of Racine, 7 Wis. 185; in Ohio, Westfall v. Braley, 10 Ohio St. 188; in Illinois, Magee v. Carmack, 13 Ill. 289; and in England, Timmins v. Gibbins, 18 Q. B. 722. See further, Thomas v. Todd, 6 Hill, 340; Houghton v. Adams, 18 Barb. 545; Baker v. Bonesteel, 2 Hill. 897; Commonwealth v. Stone, 4 Met. 43; Snow v. Perry, 9 Pick. 539; Alexander v. Dennis, 9 Port. (Ala.) 174; Alexander v. Byers, 19 Ind. 801; Dakin v. Anderson, 18 Ind. 52; Aldrich v. Jackson, 6 R. I. 218.

Mr. Justice Story is of the opinion that the question after all is mainly one of fact as to the intention of the parties; and it must, he thinks, turn upon this inquiry, whether, taking all the circumstances together, the paper was taken as absolute payment by the purchaser, at his own risk, or only as conditional payment, with the requirement merely of due diligence to obtain payment. Promissory Notes, § 389.

But it may be questioned if this reaches the difficulty. The real question in cases like Bayard v. Shunk is,

What is the presumption of law in the absence of evidence upon the question of present solvency when the paper is given for cash as a payment of property? If the paper itself is to be treated as a commodity, as property, and not as merely the representative thereof, then the Pennsylvania court appear to be right in applying the doctrine of caveat emptor to the transaction. If on the other hand it is only the symbol of property, if the purchaser acquires only a promise to pay money, it may well be doubted whether the maxim of sales should apply. The rule of caveat emptor, though well established (within limits) in the case of sales of goods, does not sufficiently commend itself to favor to justify an extension of it to cases not strictly falling within its admitted application. Nor should the fact of hardship to the vendor be permitted to enter into the consideration of a case in which the purchaser is in no way responsible for that which brings upon him that result. If the vendor loses his money, he loses it because of a fact anterior to the purchase by the plaintiff,—a fact which existed while the paper was in the vendor’s hands. The vendor had already lost: the paper was good for nothing to him. Nor does the fact that he supposed it to be good justify him in keeping the money or property received, which could only have been given for it by the purchaser upon the same supposition that it was good. There has been a failure of consideration. See Timmins v. Gibbins, 18 Q. B. 722, 725, Lord Campbell.

This suggestion, however, will give way before any evidence tending to show that the purchaser in fact undertook to purchase at his own risk. And it will have no bearing upon the situation of parties solvent when the paper was purchased, who become in-

<br>

### --- Page 0724 ---

672 PAYMENT.

solvent before its maturity. In cases of this kind, when the paper has been actually bought, or taken in actual payment of property then sold, and not as security for a debt, the risk of the ultimate productiveness of the paper is of course assumed, in the absence of evidence of agreement to the contrary, by the purchaser. That is not an arbitrary implication of law, but is only a declaration of the common understanding and intention of men in such transactions. Caveat emptor has nothing to do with making the rule as to transactions of that kind.

<br>
